License: arXiv.org perpetual non-exclusive license
arXiv:2604.27079v1 [math.SG] 29 Apr 2026
\dgARROWLENGTH

=1em

Holomorphic disks and tropical Lagrangians

Chris T. Woodward
Abstract.

We develop a calculus for counting pseudoholomorphic disks with boundary in tropical Lagrangians submanifolds of almost toric manifolds, using our previous work with Venugopalan [35], [34]. The results are mostly in dimension four under monotonicity assumptions although in principle the same technique works in any dimension and without monotonicity. The calculus is given as a sum over tropical graphs that interact with the tropical graph of the Lagrangian, generalizing results of Mikhalkin [25] and Nishinou-Siebert [sn] for holomorphic spheres in toric varieties, and our previous result with Venugopalan [34] which dealt with disks bounding almost toric moment fibers. The main contribution of this paper is the calculation of several multiplicities of vertices corresponding to disks, such as the holomorphic pant (half of the holomorphic pair of pants) and various univalent vertices occuring at trivalent vertices of the graph of the Lagrangian; a key tool is a Lagrangian isotopy from the Lagrangian pair of pants in the del Pezzo of degree seven to the inverse image of a diagonal, which is a special case of results of Hind [16] and Evans [9]. We show that every integer eigenvalue of non-maximal modulus for quantum multiplication by the first Chern class is realized by such a sphere.

This work was partially supported by NSF grant DMS 2105417. Any opinions, findings, and conclusions or recommendations expressed in this material are those of the author(s) and do not necessarily reflect the views of the National Science Foundation.

1. Introduction

Tropical geometry describes the limits of various geometric objects under adiabatic limits, such as the limit of a Lagrangian torus fibration when the volume of the fibers tends to zero. By a tropical Lagrangian we mean a Lagrangian in a family that approximates some piecewise linear subset of the base of such a fibration in the sense of Definition 2.8. We give in Theorem 2.9 below a slight generalization of work of Hicks [13] and Mikhalkin [27] in the toric case showing that such families can be associated to tropical graphs in the bases of almost toric fibrations.

In particular, del Pezzo surfaces equipped with monotone symplectic forms (that is, symplectic forms ω\omega for which [ω]=λ​c1​(X)[\omega]=\lambda c_{1}(X) for some λ>0\lambda>0) have systems of embedded Lagrangian two-spheres whose tropical graphs have edges and nodes corresponding to the nodes and edges of the affine Coxeter-Dynkin diagrams introduced by Manin [21] at the level of cohomology. We call these Manin configurations of Lagrangian spheres. Examples of tropical graphs associated to these configurations are shown in Figure 1, where the relevant Lagrangians have tropical graphs connecting two or three adjacent critical values in the almost tori diagrams introduced by Vianna [36].

In this paper, we develop tropical techniques for counting disks with boundary in tropical Lagrangians, and in particular, computing the disk potentials of the Manin Lagrangians in del Pezzo surfaces. By the disk potential, we mean the count WL∈ℤW_{L}\in\mathbb{Z} of rigid holomorphic disks passing through a generic point as in Oh [29], where the moduli spaces are oriented using the unique relative spin structure on these Lagrangian spheres as in [10]. By Sheridan [31, Corollary 2.10], each such integer is an eigenvalue of quantum multiplication by the first Chern class. We prove the following:

Theorem 1.1.

Let XX be a del Pezzo surface equipped with a monotone symplectic form. Every integer eigenvalue w∈ℤw\in\mathbb{Z} of quantum multiplication by the first Chern class c1​(X)c_{1}(X) on Q​H​(X)QH(X) whose absolute value |w||w| is not maximal is the potential WLW_{L} of a Lagrangian sphere L⊂XL\subset X, whose homology class represents a simple root α∈S\alpha\in S for some semisimple root system of A​D​EADE type in a Manin configuration in XX.

The eigenvalues of quantum multiplication by the first Chern class are shown in Table 1 (with critical values computed numerically with Mathematica). The notation Blk⁡ℙ2\operatorname{Bl}^{k}\mathbb{P}^{2} means ℙ2\mathbb{P}^{2} blown-up kk times at generic points.

There is another way of phrasing Theorem 1.1. Given an integer eigenvalue ww, one can ask whether it is the potential of some Lagrangian sphere. Since it is quite obvious that the open-closed map on the Floer cohomology of such a sphere is dimension at least two (the inclusion map on classical homology is injective), the corresponding eigenspace must have multiplicity at least two. Since del Pezzo surfaces satisfy conjecture 𝒪\mathcal{O}, by Hu-Ke-Li-Yang [18], only the integer eigenvalues with non-maximal modulus have a chance of corresponding to Lagrangian two-spheres. What we show is that every such eigenvalue is in fact represented by a sphere, in the case of del Pezzo surfaces.

The proof of Theorem 1.1 is not quite given in this paper; what we show here is that each of the eigenvalues ww in Table 1 with |w|∈ℤ|w|\in\mathbb{Z} non-maximal is realized by a Lagrangian sphere. To see that the numbers in the last column of Table 1 are all the eigenvalues of quantum multiplication by c1​(X)c_{1}(X), one either needs a presentation of the quantum cohomology with the first Chern class identified in the presentation (as is available for the cubic surface, as in Sheridan [31, Appendix]) or know that the Lagrangians split-generate the Fukaya category (as we show in a sequel to this paper.) Using extensions of these results to immersed Lagrangians with transverse self-intersection, we compute in a sequel [39] the open-closed maps for Manin configurations of Lagrangians in almost toric manifolds, and show split-generation of the corresponding Fukaya eigencategories.

We remark that tropical formulas for disk counts were already considered in Mikhalkin [26], while tropical formulas for disk counts in the case of almost toric fibers were studied in [34]. In the former case, the image of the Lagrangian is dimension two (being the real locus) while in the latter case, the image of the Lagrangian is one; therefore, the results of this paper treat the intermediate case.

1.1. Disk counts for tropical Lagrangians

We describe in more detail the disk counts that we wish to compute. Let XX be a compact connected almost toric four-manifold. Thus XX admits a singular Lagrangian fibration over base BB with projection Φ:X→B\Phi:X\to B, as defined in Leung-Symington [20]. We call Φ\Phi the almost toric moment map and

Δ:=Φ​(X)⊂B\Delta:=\Phi(X)\subset B

the moment polytope. The union of nodal singularities in the fibers, called focus-focus singularities, is denoted Xfoc⊂XX^{\operatorname{foc}}\subset X. Let

Bfoc=Φ​(Xfoc)⊂BB^{\operatorname{foc}}=\Phi(X^{\operatorname{foc}})\subset B

denote the images of the focus-focus singularities under the projection Φ\Phi, called the focus-focus values. We focus on the case dim(X)=4\dim(X)=4, which we now assume. We assume that Φ\Phi is proper with connected fibers, so that Φ​(X)\Phi(X) is locally polyhedral with respect to the affine structure on the complement of the focus-focus values. By a tropical Lagrangian we mean a Lagrangian LL whose image under the projection to the base approximates some tropical graph

Λ=(Vert⁡(Λ),Edge⁡(Λ))\Lambda=(\operatorname{Vert}(\Lambda),\operatorname{Edge}(\Lambda))

in the moment polytope BB of XX; see Definition 2.8 for a precise definition. In the Figures such as Figure 2, the tropical graph Λ\Lambda of the Lagrangian is shown in aqua, while the tropical graphs Γ\Gamma of the holomorphic disks are shown in dark blue. We give a minor generalization of a result of Mikhalkin [27] and Matessi [22], [23] we explain how tropical graphs Γ\Gamma in the base BB give rise to Lagrangian submanifolds LL in the symplectic manifold XX. Let

𝒫={P⊂B}\mathcal{P}=\{P\subset B\}

be a polyhedral decomposition of BB into simple rational polytopes; that is, the intersection of finitely many rational half-spaces so that at most dim(X)\dim(X) of the boundaries of these half-spaces meet at any point. The quotient construction for the circle actions generated by the components of the moment map normal to the facets gives a degeneration denoted 𝕏\mathbb{X}. We choose particularly simple decompositions in order to compute disk potentials:

Definition 1.2.

The decomposition 𝒫\mathcal{P} is elementary if one of the possibilities holds exclusively: Each P∈𝒫P\in\mathcal{P} either

  1. (a)

    contains at most one focus-focus value b∈Bfocb\in B^{\operatorname{foc}} and in which case is equivalent to the polytope shown after item (d), up to the action of G​L​(2,ℤ)GL(2,\mathbb{Z});

  2. (b)

    contains the projection ℓ=Φ​(L)\ell=\Phi(L) of the Lagrangian LL;

  3. (c)

    intersects the boundary of Φ​(X)\Phi(X) and contains at most one vertex of Φ​(X)\Phi(X); in this case P∩Φ​(X)P\cap\Phi(X) is a trapezoid and, if it contains a vertex, P∩Φ​(X)P\cap\Phi(X) is a parallelogram.

Any polyhedral decomposition of the type above induces a decomposition and degeneration of the symplectic manifold as follows. First, the manifold XX is a disjoint union of the preimages of the polyhedra in the collection 𝒫\mathcal{P}:

X=⋃P∈𝒫Φ−1​(P).X=\bigcup_{P\in\mathcal{P}}\Phi^{-1}(P).

On the other hand, we also have a degeneration (loosely speaking) of XX of obtained by identifying the points in XX by the action of a torus TPT_{P} over the elements of 𝒫\mathcal{P}. We say that the polyhedral decomposition is admissible if these groups act with only finite stabilizers, so that the quotient

X/∼=⋃P∈𝒫Φ−1(P)/TPX/_{\sim}=\bigcup_{P\in\mathcal{P}}\Phi^{-1}(P)/T_{P}

is a union of symplectic orbifolds, as explained in [35]. For each such polyhedral decomposition, we choose a set of cutting datum, consisting of a dual polytope P∨P^{\vee} each PP in BB, as described in [35]. The dual complex associated to the cutting datum is the union of dual polytopes, modulo the face equivalence relation:

B∨=⋃P∈𝒫P∨/∼.B^{\vee}=\bigcup_{P\in\mathcal{P}}P^{\vee}/\sim.

By the boundary of the dual complex we mean the union

∂B∨:=⋃P∈𝒫,P∩∂Δ≠∅P∨/∼\partial B^{\vee}:=\bigcup_{P\in\mathcal{P},P\cap\partial\Delta\neq\emptyset}P^{\vee}/\sim

of duals of polytopes meeting the boundary ∂Δ\partial\Delta of the moment polytope Δ\Delta. One sees easily from the definitions that B∨B^{\vee} is a topological manifold with boundary ∂B∨\partial B^{\vee}, with an affine structure on the complement of the duals P∨P^{\vee} of polytopes containing the focus-focus values. We denote by

(1) Λ∨=⋃P∈𝒫,P∩Λ≠∅P∨/∼\Lambda^{\vee}=\bigcup_{P\in\mathcal{P},P\cap\Lambda\neq\emptyset}P^{\vee}/\sim

the part of the dual complex corresponding to pieces meeting the Lagrangian; by our assumption, Λ∨\Lambda^{\vee} is a one dimensional complex in B∨B^{\vee}, with the same topology as Λ\Lambda. However, each edge in Λ\Lambda dualizes to a collection of edges in Λ∨\Lambda^{\vee} which may not have the same direction, with respect to the dual affine structure.

A cutting datum gives rise to a neck-stretched family of almost complex structures. In the neck-stretching limit corresponding to the decomposition, any sequence of holomorphic maps with bounded energy limits, after passing to a subsequence, to a broken map: a collection of holomorphic maps uvu_{v} associated to the vertices vv of a graph Γ\Gamma with vertices Vert⁡(Γ)\operatorname{Vert}(\Gamma) and edges Edge⁡(Γ)\operatorname{Edge}(\Gamma). The graph Γ\Gamma is equipped with a map

Vert⁡(Γ)→𝒫,v↦P​(v).\operatorname{Vert}(\Gamma)\to\mathcal{P},\quad v\mapsto P(v).

Each edge ee of Γ\Gamma connecting vertices v−v_{-} and v+v_{+} has an assigned direction

𝒯​(e)∈𝔱P​(e),P​(e):=P​(v−)∩P​(v+)\mathcal{T}(e)\in\mathfrak{t}_{P(e)},\quad P(e):=P(v_{-})\cap P(v_{+})

where 𝔱P​(e)\mathfrak{t}_{P(e)} is a certain vector space to be defined later, dual to the span of the polytope P​(e)P(e). If ee connects two vertices v−,v+v_{-},v_{+}, we call ee an ordinary edge. If ee contains a single vertex vv we call ee a leaf. A picture of some of the tropical graphs Γ\Gamma associated to holomorphic disks in a del Pezzo of degree four is shown on the right in Figure 2; in the depiction we have overlayed the dual polyhedral complex with the original polyhedral complex; otherwise the pictures become difficult to visualize. The left picture in Figure 2 shows the almost toric diagram and the center picture shows the tropical graph of the Lagrangian. The right figures show the four tropical graphs of the holomorphic disks with boundary in the Lagrangian and passing through the given constraint (shown as a burgundy dot on top of the graph of the Lagrangian.) The potential is calculated in Example 1.8.

For each vertex vv in Vert⁡(Γ)\operatorname{Vert}(\Gamma) there is a holomorphic disk or sphere

uv:Cv→𝕏P​(v)u_{v}:C_{v}\to\mathbb{X}_{P(v)}

in some target 𝕏P​(v)\mathbb{X}_{P(v)} obtained by taking the thickening of the inverse image of P​(v)P(v) under the moment map. We denote by Vert∘⁡(Γ)\operatorname{Vert}_{\circ}(\Gamma) resp. Vert∙⁡(Γ)\operatorname{Vert}_{\bullet}(\Gamma) the open resp. closed vertices corresponding to disks and spheres respectively. The edges correspond to long cylinders or strips with boundary in the Lagrangian connecting the components uvu_{v}; we denote by Edge∙⁡(Γ)\operatorname{Edge}_{\bullet}(\Gamma) resp. Edge∘⁡(Γ)\operatorname{Edge}_{\circ}(\Gamma) the corresponding sets of closed resp. open edges. For example, the map uvu_{v} may be a holomorphic pant (the singular is intentional here) by which we mean a holomorphic disk with a single cylindrical end and a single strip-like end pictured on the right in Figure 30, with the corresponding bivalent vertex of a tropical graph pictured on the left. This curve is half of the holomorphic pair-of-pants considered in Mikhalkin [25] and, perhaps not surprisingly, counts in the formulas with a factor of half of the determinant that appears in [25].

The broken maps are required to satisfy matching conditions at the edges. In our previous work [35], we performed an additional degeneration so that the diagonal condition at the edges was deformed to split form. We show in Theorem 3.33 below that the moduli space ℳΓ​(𝕃)\mathcal{M}_{\mathbb{\Gamma}}({\mathbb{L}}) of broken maps with type Γ\mathbb{\Gamma} is a product

ℳΓ​(𝕃)≅∏v∈Vert⁡(Γ)ℳΓ​(v)​(𝕃P​(v),𝕐v)\mathcal{M}_{\mathbb{\Gamma}}({\mathbb{L}})\cong\prod_{v\in\operatorname{Vert}(\mathbb{\Gamma})}\mathcal{M}_{\mathbb{\Gamma}(v)}({\mathbb{L}}_{P(v)},\mathbb{Y}_{v})

where

𝕐¯v=(𝕐¯v,∘,𝕐¯v,∙)\underline{\mathbb{Y}}_{v}=(\underline{\mathbb{Y}}_{v,\circ},\underline{\mathbb{Y}}_{v,\bullet})

is a collection of constraints attached to the leaves of Γ​(v)\Gamma(v) for each vertex vv; in the current situation, these constraints will be point constraints. The counts of broken maps then reduce to counts of tropical graphs Γ\Gamma with multiplicities m​(Γ)m(\Gamma). If the tropical graph Γ\mathbb{\Gamma} of the curve is trivalent and satisfies the conditions above, we assign to Γ\mathbb{\Gamma} a multiplicity m​(Γ)m(\mathbb{\Gamma}) that is, up to a combinatorial factor, a product of multiplicities assigned to vertices

m​(Γ)=(d∙​(Γ))−1​∏v∈Vert⁡(Γ)m​(v)∈ℚ;m(\mathbb{\Gamma})=(d_{\bullet}(\mathbb{\Gamma}))^{-1}\prod_{v\in\operatorname{Vert}(\mathbb{\Gamma})}m(v)\in\mathbb{Q};

here d∙​(Γ)∈ℤ>0d_{\bullet}(\mathbb{\Gamma})\in\mathbb{Z}_{>0} is the number of interior leaves corresponding to intersections with a Donaldson hypersurface D⊂XD\subset X used for the regularization of pseudoholomorphic curves. In case Γ\mathbb{\Gamma} has vertices vv with valence |v||v| greater than 33, (or greater than two and at least two of the adjacent edges are of closed type) the multiplicity m​(Γ)m(\mathbb{\Gamma}) is defined by a desingularization procedure explained in [34], which perturbs the positions of the closed edges. Given a collection 𝕐¯=(𝕐¯∙,𝕐¯∘)\underline{\mathbb{Y}}=(\underline{\mathbb{Y}}_{\bullet},\underline{\mathbb{Y}}_{\circ}) of such constraints at the boundary leaves and interior leaves respectively, we consider the moduli space ℳ​(𝕃,𝕐¯)\mathcal{M}({\mathbb{L}},\underline{\mathbb{Y}}) of broken maps bounding 𝕃{\mathbb{L}} with the corresponding constraints 𝕐¯\underline{\mathbb{Y}} at the leaves. We have in mind especially the disk counts that contribute to the disk potentials and open-closed map, and denote by

m​(𝕃,𝕐¯)∈ℚm({\mathbb{L}},\underline{\mathbb{Y}})\in\mathbb{Q}

the weighted count of its elements. In particular, the disk potential of a monotone Lagrangian is given by the count W𝕃W_{\mathbb{L}} of disks with a single point constraint, independent of the choice of point ptL∈L\operatorname{pt}_{L}\in L.

We will be particularly interested in the following graphs for which we can compute the contributions using the main result:

Definition 1.3.

A tropical graph Γ\Gamma in B∨B^{\vee} has collisions in the interior if for any vertex of Γ\Gamma for which the polytope P​(v)P(v) intersects ∂Φ​(X)\partial\Phi(X),

  1. (a)

    the valence |v||v| of vv is one and

  2. (b)

    P​(v)P(v) intersects exactly a single facet QQ of Φ​(X)\Phi(X) and the direction 𝒯​(e)∈𝔱\mathcal{T}(e)\in\mathfrak{t} of the adjacent edge e∈Edge⁡(Γ)e\in\operatorname{Edge}(\Gamma) is the primitive normal to QQ.

A tropical graph Γ\Gamma in B∨B^{\vee} has collisions only at smooth points if for any vertex v∈Vert⁡(Γ)v\in\operatorname{Vert}(\Gamma), the cut space XP​(v)X_{P(v)} contains a focus-focus singularity, then the valence |v||v| of vv is one. A tropical graph Γ\Gamma is generic if Γ\Gamma has only collisions in the interior and the valence |v||v| of each vertex v∈Vert⁡(Γ)v\in\operatorname{Vert}(\Gamma) is at most three, and |v||v| is at most two if there is a single open edge adjacent to vv.

Theorem 1.4.

Let XX be a compact monotone almost toric four-manifold with base BB. Let LL be an embedded Lagrangian sphere that realizes a tropical graph Λ⊂B\Lambda\subset B. Let 𝒫={P}\mathcal{P}=\{P\} be a polyhedral admissible decomposition of BB equipped with cutting data. The count m​(𝕃,𝕐¯)m({\mathbb{L}},\underline{\mathbb{Y}}) of broken rigid disks in 𝕏\mathbb{X} with boundary in the broken Lagrangian 𝕃{\mathbb{L}} with a point constraint 𝕐¯=({pt})⊂𝕃\underline{\mathbb{Y}}=(\{\operatorname{pt}\})\subset{\mathbb{L}} is a count of types Γ\mathbb{\Gamma} in the dual complex B∨B^{\vee}:

m​(𝕃,𝕐¯)=∑Γ∈𝒯​(𝕐¯)(#​Aut⁡(Γ))−1​∏v∈Vert⁡(Γ)m​(𝕃v,𝕐¯v)m({\mathbb{L}},\underline{\mathbb{Y}})=\sum_{\mathbb{\Gamma}\in\mathcal{T}(\underline{\mathbb{Y}})}(\#\operatorname{Aut}(\mathbb{\Gamma}))^{-1}\prod_{v\in\operatorname{Vert}(\mathbb{\Gamma})}m({\mathbb{L}}_{v},\underline{\mathbb{Y}}_{v})

where 𝒯​(𝕐¯)\mathcal{T}(\underline{\mathbb{Y}}) is the set of rigid graphs in B∨B^{\vee} with constraints 𝕐¯\underline{\mathbb{Y}}, and m​(𝕃v,𝕐¯v)m({\mathbb{L}}_{v},\underline{\mathbb{Y}}_{v}) is a count of holomorphic disks in 𝕏P​(v)\mathbb{X}_{P(v)} with some collection of constraints 𝕐¯v\underline{\mathbb{Y}}_{v}. Furthermore, if Γ\mathbb{\Gamma} is generic then each m​(v)=m​(𝕃v,𝕐¯v)m(v)=m({\mathbb{L}}_{v},\underline{\mathbb{Y}}_{v}) is a multiplicity given in Definition 1.5.

We explain how to obtain this Theorem by modifying the arguments in Venugopalan-Woodward [35] in Section 3.2. We remark that in contrast to the case of toric moment fibers in [34], it is not possible to achieve the valence conditions stated in the theorem through perturbation. Indeed, the positions of the open edges of the tropical graph Γ\mathbb{\Gamma} cannot be perturbed, since they coincide with open edges of the tropical graph Λ\Lambda of the Lagrangian. It follows that the condition that the valence of each vertex is at most three cannot be achieved by perturbation. As a result, the list in Theorem 1.4 is only partial as we do not compute the higher valence multiplicities. Nevertheless, the case of vertices of valence at most three will suffice to compute the disk potential for the Lagrangian spheres considered here.

1.2. Multiplicities of tropical vertices

The multiplicities of certain vertices are given as follows, and will be shown to be counts of holomorphic disks bounding the Lagrangian: (The meaning of the dots in the pictures will be explained later.) The multiplicities are non-canonical; they depend on the choices of relative spin structures on certain extensions of the boundary value problems over the punctures, and in the case of the holomorphic pant, on a particular choice of complex structure and perturbation.

Definition 1.5.

Define multiplicities m​(v)∈ℚm(v)\in\mathbb{Q} associated to vertices v∈Vert⁡(Γ)v\in\operatorname{Vert}(\Gamma) with valence |v||v| at most three as follows: (Each item heading describes the corresponding holomorphic curves.)

  1. (a)

    (Holomorphic cylinders) The multiplicity

    m​(v)=1m(v)=1

    for bivalent vertices vv with adjacent edges e1,e2∈Edge∙⁡(Γ)e_{1},e_{2}\in\operatorname{Edge}_{\bullet}(\Gamma) in the same directions

    e1,e2∈Edge∙⁡(Γ),𝒯​(e1)=𝒯​(e2).e_{1},e_{2}\in\operatorname{Edge}_{\bullet}(\Gamma),\quad\mathcal{T}(e_{1})=\mathcal{T}(e_{2}).
  2. (b)

    (Holomorphic pairs of pants) As in Mikhalkin [25], the multiplicity

    m​(v)=|det(𝒯​(e1)​𝒯​(e2))|m(v)=|\det(\mathcal{T}(e_{1})\mathcal{T}(e_{2}))|

    for trivalent vertices v∈Vert∙⁡(Γ)v\in\operatorname{Vert}_{\bullet}(\Gamma) with adjacent edges e1,e2,e3∈Edge⁡(Γ)e_{1},e_{2},e_{3}\in\operatorname{Edge}(\Gamma) whose edge directions (exactly two of which are incoming) satisfying the balancing condition

    (2) 𝒯​(e1)+𝒯​(e2)+𝒯​(e3)=0;\quad\mathcal{T}(e_{1})+\mathcal{T}(e_{2})+\mathcal{T}(e_{3})=0;
  3. (c)

    (Disks meeting the toric boundary) As in Cho-Oh [5], the multiplicity

    m​(v)=1m(v)=1

    if v∈Vert∙⁡(Γ)v\in\operatorname{Vert}_{\bullet}(\Gamma) is a univalent vertex at a facet QQ of the toric boundary ∂Δ\partial\Delta whose adjacent edge ee has direction 𝒯​(e)∈𝔱P​(v),ℤ\mathcal{T}(e)\in\mathfrak{t}_{P(v),\mathbb{Z}} is the primitive generator of the normal direction to T​QTQ.

  4. (d)

    (Multiple covers near focus-focus singularities) As in Bryan-Pandharipande [3], the multiplicity

    m​(v)=(−1)ℓ​(μ)−1/ℓ​(μ)2m(v)=(-1)^{\ell(\mu)-1}/\ell(\mu)^{2}

    for the univalent closed vertices v∈Vert∙⁡(Γ)v\in\operatorname{Vert}_{\bullet}(\Gamma) at the focus-focus singularities, where

    ℓ​(μ)∈ℤ>0\ell(\mu)\in\mathbb{Z}_{>0}

    is the lattice length of the direction μ=𝒯​(e)∈𝔱P​(v),ℤ\mu=\mathcal{T}(e)\in\mathfrak{t}_{P(v),\mathbb{Z}} of the adjacent edge e∈Edge⁡(Γ)e\in\operatorname{Edge}(\Gamma); that is, μ=ℓ​(μ)​μ0\mu=\ell(\mu)\mu_{0} where μ0\mu_{0} is the primitive lattice vector in the same direction as μ\mu;

  5. (e)

    (Cylinders colliding with the Lagrangian perpendicularly) The multiplicity

    m​(v)=(−1)ℓ​(e)m(v)=(-1)^{\ell(e)}

    if v∈Vert∘⁡(Γ)v\in\operatorname{Vert}_{\circ}(\Gamma) is a univalent vertex whose polytope P​(v)P(v) meets edge ϵ∈Edge⁡(Λ)\epsilon\in\operatorname{Edge}(\Lambda) of the Lagrangian LL with the adjacent edge e∈Edge⁡(Γ)e\in\operatorname{Edge}(\Gamma) having direction 𝒯​(e)∈𝔱P​(v),ℤ\mathcal{T}(e)\in\mathfrak{t}_{P(v),\mathbb{Z}} with lattice length

    ℓ​(e)∈ℤ>0\ell(e)\in\mathbb{Z}_{>0}

    perpendicular to the direction 𝒯​(ϵ)\mathcal{T}(\epsilon) of ϵ∈Edge⁡(Λ)\epsilon\in\operatorname{Edge}(\Lambda).

  6. (f)

    (Holomorphic pant, Lagrangian with direction (±1,1)(\pm 1,1)) The multiplicity

    m​(v)\displaystyle m(v) =\displaystyle= (−1)|det(𝒯​(e∘)​𝒯​(e∙))|−1/2​|det(𝒯​(e∘)​𝒯​(e∙))|\displaystyle(-1)^{|\det(\mathcal{T}(e_{\circ})\mathcal{T}(e_{\bullet}))|-1/2}|\det(\mathcal{T}(e_{\circ})\mathcal{T}(e_{\bullet}))|
    =\displaystyle= 12(−1)|2det(𝒯(e∙)r(𝒯(e∙))|−1|det(𝒯(e∙)r(𝒯(e∙))|\displaystyle{\frac{1}{2}}(-1)^{|2\det(\mathcal{T}(e_{\bullet})r(\mathcal{T}(e_{\bullet}))|-1}|\det(\mathcal{T}(e_{\bullet})r(\mathcal{T}(e_{\bullet}))|

    for bivalent vertices v∈Vert∘⁡(Γ)v\in\operatorname{Vert}_{\circ}(\Gamma) mapping to an edge ϵ\epsilon of LL with open edge e∘=(1,1)⊂ϵe_{\circ}=(1,1)\subset\epsilon and closed edges e∙,e∙′e_{\bullet},e_{\bullet}^{\prime} with directions

    𝒯​(e∙)=(x,y),r​(𝒯​(e∙))=𝒯​(e∙′)=(y,x)∈ℤ2\mathcal{T}(e_{\bullet})=(x,y),r(\mathcal{T}(e_{\bullet}))=\mathcal{T}(e_{\bullet}^{\prime})=(y,x)\in\mathbb{Z}^{2}

    for some integers x,yx,y, with directions 𝒯​(e∘)∈𝔱P​(v),𝒯​(e∙)∈𝔱P​(v),ℤ\mathcal{T}(e_{\circ})\in\mathfrak{t}_{P(v)},\mathcal{T}(e_{\bullet})\in\mathfrak{t}_{P(v),\mathbb{Z}} satisfying the balancing condition

    2​𝒯​(e∘)+𝒯​(e∙)+r​(𝒯​(e∙))=(1+x+y,1+x+y)=02\mathcal{T}(e_{\circ})+\mathcal{T}(e_{\bullet})+r(\mathcal{T}(e_{\bullet}))=(1+x+y,1+x+y)=0

    where r:ℝ2→ℝ2r:\mathbb{R}^{2}\to\mathbb{R}^{2} is the reflection over the span of ϵ\epsilon.111Note that we insist that the direction of the edge of the graph of the Lagrangian is the diagonal or antidiagonal. Of course, any edge may be put in this position by some element AA of S​L​(2,ℤ).SL(2,\mathbb{Z}). However, the same transformation acts by the transpose AtA^{t} on the directions of the edges of the graph of the broken map, so there seems to be only a clean statement in the diagonal or anti-diagonal cases.

  7. (g)

    (Disks dividing Lagrangian pants in half) The multiplicity

    m​(v)=1m(v)=1

    for univalent vertices v∈Vert∘⁡(Γ)v\in\operatorname{Vert}_{\circ}(\Gamma) mapping to a trivalent vertex ℓ∈Vert⁡(Λ)\ell\in\operatorname{Vert}(\Lambda) with adjacent edge e∈Edge∘⁡(Γ)e\in\operatorname{Edge}_{\circ}(\Gamma) having direction 𝒯​(e)\mathcal{T}(e) the same as the direction 𝒯​(ϵ)\mathcal{T}(\epsilon) of one of the adjacent edges ϵ∈Edge⁡(Λ)\epsilon\in\operatorname{Edge}(\Lambda) of the tropical graph Λ\Lambda of LL.

  8. (h)

    (Two-ended strips in Lagrangian pants) The multiplicity

    m​(v)=1m(v)=1

    for bivalent vertices v∈Vert∘⁡(Γ)v\in\operatorname{Vert}_{\circ}(\Gamma) mapping to trivalent vertices ℓ∈Vert⁡(Λ)\ell\in\operatorname{Vert}(\Lambda) with edge directions 𝒯​(e1),𝒯​(e2)\mathcal{T}(e_{1}),\mathcal{T}(e_{2}) of adjacent edges e1∈Edge∘⁡(Γ),e2∈Edge∘⁡(Γ)e_{1}\in\operatorname{Edge}_{\circ}(\Gamma),e_{2}\in\operatorname{Edge}_{\circ}(\Gamma) with directions

    𝒯​(e1)=𝒯​(ϵ1),𝒯​(e2)=𝒯​(ϵ2)∈𝔱P​(v)\mathcal{T}(e_{1})=\mathcal{T}(\epsilon_{1}),\mathcal{T}(e_{2})=\mathcal{T}(\epsilon_{2})\in\mathfrak{t}_{P(v)}

    equal to the directions 𝒯​(ϵ1),𝒯​(ϵ2)\mathcal{T}(\epsilon_{1}),\mathcal{T}(\epsilon_{2}) of adjacent edges of the graph Λ\Lambda of LL, and either one point boundary constraint p∈Lp\in L or one point constraint p∈ℛ∘​(P​(ei)),i∈{1,2}p\in\mathcal{R}_{\circ}(P(e_{i})),i\in\{1,2\} on the Reeb orbit at a strip-like end e1e_{1} or e2e_{2}. The situation is depicted in the figure at left below:

  9. (i)

    (Three-ended strips in Lagrangian pants) The multiplicity

    m​(v)=1m(v)=1

    for trivalent vertices v∈Vert∘⁡(Γ)v\in\operatorname{Vert}_{\circ}(\Gamma) with adjacent edges e1,e2,e3e_{1},e_{2},e_{3} having directions 𝒯​(e1),𝒯​(e2),𝒯​(e3)∈𝔱P​(v)\mathcal{T}(e_{1}),\mathcal{T}(e_{2}),\mathcal{T}(e_{3})\in\mathfrak{t}_{P(v)} equal to the directions of the adjacent edges ϵ1,ϵ2,ϵ3∈Edge⁡(Λ)\epsilon_{1},\epsilon_{2},\epsilon_{3}\in\operatorname{Edge}(\Lambda) of LL with a point constraint p∈Lp\in L or a point constraint at a Reeb chord at one of the strip-like ends. (Recall that the Mikhalkin multiplicity m​(v)=1m(v)=1 of v∈Vert⁡(Λ)v\in\operatorname{Vert}(\Lambda) by assumption.) The figure is included at right in the previous item.

The appearance of the holomorphic pant, i.e., half-pair-of-pants seems to be new in the literature on disks bounding tropical Lagrangians, which was somewhat surprising to us.

1.3. Sample computations of potentials

As examples, we compute the disk potential for some tropical Lagrangians in del Pezzo surfaces.

Example 1.6.

We compute the disk potential for a tropical Lagrangian in the blow-up XX of the product of projective lines ℙ1×ℙ1\mathbb{P}^{1}\times\mathbb{P}^{1} at (0,0)(0,0) and (∞,∞)(\infty,\infty). The moment polytope may be taken to be the hexagon that is the convex hull

Φ(X)=hull{(−1,0),(0,−1),(1,0),(0,1),(1,−1),(−1,1).}\Phi(X)=\operatorname{hull}\{(-1,0),(0,-1),(1,0),(0,1),(1,-1),(-1,1).\}

Consider the Lagrangian

L0={([z1,z2],[z¯2,z¯1])|(z1,z2)∈ℂ2−{0}}⊂ℙ1×ℙ1L_{0}=\{([z_{1},z_{2}],[\overline{z}_{2},\overline{z}_{1}])|(z_{1},z_{2})\in\mathbb{C}^{2}-\{0\}\}\subset\mathbb{P}^{1}\times\mathbb{P}^{1}

which is the graph of the anti-symplectic involution

τ:X→X,[z1,z2]↦[z¯2,z¯1].\tau:X\to X,\quad[z_{1},z_{2}]\mapsto[\overline{z}_{2},\overline{z}_{1}].

The Lagrangian L0L_{0} is disjoint from the cut locus in ℙ1×ℙ1\mathbb{P}^{1}\times\mathbb{P}^{1} and lifts to a Lagrangian LL in the blow-up XX.

Since XX is toric, the codomain of the moment map is B=ℝ2B=\mathbb{R}^{2}. The moment image of LL is the segment Λ\Lambda between the vertices (1,−1)(1,-1) and (−1,1)(-1,1), as shown on the left in Figure 12. In particular, LL is a realization of Λ\Lambda in the sense of Mikhalkin [27]; see Theorem 2.9.

Let p∈Lp\in L be a point mapping to (0,0)(0,0) under the moment map. Let 𝒫={P}\mathcal{P}=\{P\} be an elementary polyhedral decomposition of Φ​(X)\Phi(X) in the sense of Definition 1.2, with the property that there exists an element P0∈𝒫P_{0}\in\mathcal{P} so that P0P_{0} contains the image Φ​(p)\Phi(p) but no part of the boundary ∂Φ​(X)\partial\Phi(X). The dual complex B∨B^{\vee} shown on the right in Figure 12; the dual graph Λ∨\Lambda^{\vee} is shown in aqua. The dual complex B∨B^{\vee} may be identified with a subset of the dual of ℝ2\mathbb{R}^{2}, which we may identify with ℝ2\mathbb{R}^{2} via the standard Euclidean metric. The dual complex B∨B^{\vee} in the middle and right parts of Figure 12 is colored in peach to distinguish it from the moment polytope Φ​(X)\Phi(X) shown in grey on the left.

Indeed, there are two rigid tropical graphs Γ1,Γ2⊂Φ​(X)\Gamma_{1},\Gamma_{2}\subset\Phi(X) representing Maslov-index-two disks shown on the right-most two figures in blue in Figure 12.

  • •

    The graph Γ1\Gamma_{1} is a single segment connecting (0,0)(0,0) to (1/2,1/2)(1/2,1/2).

  • •

    The graph Γ2\Gamma_{2} is a single segment connecting (0,0)(0,0) to (−1/2,−1/2)(-1/2,-1/2).

Each of the graphs Γ1,Γ2\Gamma_{1},\Gamma_{2} has two vertices v1,v2∈Vert⁡(Γ)v_{1},v_{2}\in\operatorname{Vert}(\Gamma), one at the Lagrangian with m​(v1)=−1m(v_{1})=-1 as in Definition 1.5 (e) and one at the boundary ∂B∨\partial B^{\vee} of the dual complex with m​(v2)=1m(v_{2})=1 as in Definition 1.5 (c).

There can be no tropical graphs Γ\Gamma contributing with a vertex v∈Vert⁡(Γ)v\in\operatorname{Vert}(\Gamma) with valence|v|≥3|v|\geq 3. Indeed, any such graph Γ\Gamma cannot be rigid as the position 𝒯​(v)\mathcal{T}(v) of the vertex vv is deformable. Alternatively, note using Remark 4.21 below that any such graph Γ\Gamma must represent a disk uu having at least two intersections with the boundary divisor Y=Φ−1​(∂Φ​(X))Y=\Phi^{-1}(\partial\Phi(X)). Thus uu is a disk of Maslov index at least four.

Combining the previous two paragraphs we obtain the value of the disk potential as

WL=m​(Γ1)+m​(Γ2)=(−1)+(−1)=−2.W_{L}=m(\Gamma_{1})+m(\Gamma_{2})=(-1)+(-1)=-2.
Example 1.7.

We compute the disk potential for the tropical Lagrangian in the blow-up of the product of projective lines at a point whose graph has a single trivalent vertex. Let XX be the blow-up of ℙ1×ℙ1\mathbb{P}^{1}\times\mathbb{P}^{1} with moment polytope the pentagon that is the convex hull

Δ=hull⁡((−1,1),(1,0),(0,1),(1,−1),(−1,1)).\Delta=\operatorname{hull}((-1,1),(1,0),(0,1),(1,-1),(-1,1)).

Let Λ\Lambda denote the tropical graph with a unique trivalent vertex at ν0=(0,0)\nu_{0}=(0,0) and univalent vertices at ν1=(1,0),ν2=(0,1),ν3=(−1,−1)\nu_{1}=(1,0),\nu_{2}=(0,1),\nu_{3}=(-1,-1), with three edges ϵ1,ϵ2,ϵ3\epsilon_{1},\epsilon_{2},\epsilon_{3} containing a univalent vertex ν1,ν2,ν3\nu_{1},\nu_{2},\nu_{3}, respectively. By, for example, Mikhalkin’s realization theorem [27] the graph Λ\Lambda is realized by a Lagrangian sphere denoted L⊂XL\subset X.

We compute the number of disks passing through a particular point. Let 𝒫\mathcal{P} be a polyhedral decomposition so that P0P_{0} is a polytope of top dimension that contains the vertex of Λ\Lambda, PiP_{i} is a polytope of top dimension that contains Φ​(pi)\Phi(p_{i}) for i=1,2,3i=1,2,3 but no point in the boundary ∂Φ​(X)\partial\Phi(X).

Let p3p_{3} be a constraint with moment image Φ​(p3)=(0,ϵ),ϵ>0\Phi(p_{3})=(0,\epsilon),\epsilon>0. There are two tropical graphs Γ1,Γ2\Gamma_{1},\Gamma_{2} (shown in the upper part of Figure 38) that have a bivalent vertex at Φ​(p3)\Phi(p_{3}), and edges with directions (1,1)(1,1) and (0,−1)(0,-1) resp. (−1,0)(-1,0).

We claim that there can be no graphs Γ\Gamma corresponding to Maslov-index-two disks with boundary in LL that have trivalent (or higher) vertices at (0,0)(0,0). Indeed, each outgoing edge of such Γ\Gamma must connect to a leaf ee of Γ\Gamma that ends at a vertex vv with P​(v)P(v) meeting the boundary of the moment polytope.

The Maslov index of such disks is at least four, by Remark 4.21. Similarly, for a bivalent vertex at (0,0)(0,0) the Maslov index of the corresponding disk would be at least four. Each of the graphs Γ1,Γ2\Gamma_{1},\Gamma_{2} has two vertices, with one trivalent vertex with multiplicity m​(v1)=1m(v_{1})=1, and one bivalent vertex with multiplicity

m​(v2)=(−1)|det(I)|​|det(I)|/2=−1/2.m(v_{2})=(-1)^{|\det(I)|}|\det(I)|/2=-1/2.

The total contribution of this graph to the potential is

m​(Γk)=m​(v2)​m​(v1)=(−1/2)​(1)=−1/2.m(\Gamma_{k})=m(v_{2})m(v_{1})=(-1/2)(1)=-1/2.

So the total number of Maslov-index-two disks is

WL=m​(Γ1)+m​(Γ2)=−1/2−1/2=−1.W_{L}=m(\Gamma_{1})+m(\Gamma_{2})=-1/2-1/2=-1.
Example 1.8.

We compute the potential of a Lagrangian in a del Pezzo of degree four. Let XX be the del Pezzo of degree four equipped with a monotone symplectic form. We claim that there exists a Lagrangian sphere LL so that the number of Maslov-index-two holomorphic disks with boundary in LL and passing through a generic point in LL is

WL=−4.W_{L}=-4.

The almost toric diagram for the degree four del Pezzo is shown on the left in Figure 2. The diagram is a slight modification of the diagram B1B_{1} in Vianna [36, Figure 11]. A tropical graph Λ\Lambda for a Lagrangian sphere LL is shown at center, given by Theorem 2.9. There are four rigid tropical graphs

Γ1,Γ2,Γ3,Γ4⊂Φ​(X)\Gamma_{1},\Gamma_{2},\Gamma_{3},\Gamma_{4}\subset\Phi(X)

representing Maslov-index-two disks passing through a generic point, shown as cartoon diagrams on the right in Figure 2. Each tropical graph Γ1,Γ2,Γ3,Γ4\Gamma_{1},\Gamma_{2},\Gamma_{3},\Gamma_{4} has four vertices. For any i=1,2,3,4i=1,2,3,4, all multiplicities of vertices vv of Γi\Gamma_{i} are equal to m​(v)=1m(v)=1 except the vertex at the Lagrangian, for which m​(v)=−1m(v)=-1. Therefore, the contribution of each graph Γi\Gamma_{i} is m​(Γi)=−1m(\Gamma_{i})=-1, and the potential is

WL=m(Γ1)+m(Γ2)+m(Γ3)+m(Γ4)=−1+−1+−1+−1=−4.W_{L}=m(\Gamma_{1})+m(\Gamma_{2})+m(\Gamma_{3})+m(\Gamma_{4})=-1+-1+-1+-1=-4.

We are unaware of a simple presentation in the literature for all del Pezzos, generalizing that of Givental described in Sheridan [31, Appendix], that would give eigenvalues of quantum multiplication by the first Chern class, although presumably a Hori-Vafa-style presentation is known to experts.

Example 1.9.

We compute the potential of the Lagrangian with trivalent graph in the del Pezzo of degree six shown in Figure 14, for two different choices of constraint.

Consider first the case that the point constraint is on the lower-left leg with direction (1,1)(1,1). In this case, there are two tropical graphs Γ1,Γ2\Gamma_{1},\Gamma_{2} shown with two univalent vertices, each with contribution m​(Γ1)=m​(Γ2)=−1m(\Gamma_{1})=m(\Gamma_{2})=-1, and two tropical graphs Γ3,Γ4\Gamma_{3},\Gamma_{4} shown with two univalent vertices and one bivalent vertex. The direction of the open edge is (1/2,1/2)(1/2,1/2). The associated determinant is 1/21/2, and the sign is negative. Thus the contribution of each is

m​(Γ3)=m​(Γ4)=−1/2m(\Gamma_{3})=m(\Gamma_{4})=-1/2

for a total potential of WL=−3W_{L}=-3.

Example 1.10.

We compute the potential of the tropical Lagrangian with a trivalent graph in the Manin E6E_{6} system of Lagrangian spheres in the cubic surface, shown in Figure 15.

We assume that the point constraint is on the leg of the Lagrangian with direction (1/2,1/2)(1/2,1/2). There are two tropical graphs Γ1,Γ2\Gamma_{1},\Gamma_{2} with a single bivalent vertex, and determinant 33, contributing

m​(Γ1)=m​(Γ2)=3/2m(\Gamma_{1})=m(\Gamma_{2})=3/2

each according to the sign rule in item (f) above. There are six tropical graphs Γ3,…,Γ8\Gamma_{3},\ldots,\Gamma_{8} with a single trivalent vertex with all edges of closed type, and a negative sign arising from the vertex at the Lagrangian, contributing

m​(Γ3)+…​m​(Γ8)=−6m(\Gamma_{3})+\ldots m(\Gamma_{8})=-6

in total. Finally, there are six tropical graphs Γ9,…,Γ14\Gamma_{9},\ldots,\Gamma_{14} with a single trivalent vertex representing a holomorphic pant, each with determinant 1/21/2 and negative sign, with a total contribution of

m​(Γ9)+…​…​m​(Γ14)=−3.m(\Gamma_{9})+\ldots\ldots m(\Gamma_{14})=-3.

Thus the total potential is WL=−6W_{L}=-6.

We denote references to our earlier works [35] with the prefix T-and the work [34] with the prefix P-.

2. Tropical Lagrangians in almost toric manifolds

In this section is to extend the construction of Lagrangians associated to tropical graphs in Hicks [13] and Mikhalkin [27] to the case of almost toric manifolds, and describe the Manin collections associated to simply-laced systems in monotone del Pezzo surfaces. The tropical graphs corresponding to the Lagrangians are required to have certain directions at the focus-focus singularities.

2.1. Almost toric manifolds

An almost toric manifold is equipped with a singular Lagrangian fibration so that the fibers are either tori or nodal tori:

Definition 2.1.

An almost toric structure on a symplectic manifold (X,ω)(X,\omega) is a smooth map Φ:X→B\Phi:X\to B to a manifold BB so that the map Φ\Phi is given locally as follows: For any x∈Xx\in X, there exist Darboux coordinates (q1,…,qn,p1,…,pn)(q_{1},\ldots,q_{n},p_{1},\ldots,p_{n}) on an open neighborhood UU of xx so that the restriction of Φ\Phi to UU is a product Φ1×…×Φk\Phi_{1}\times\ldots\times\Phi_{k} of maps Φj,j=1,…,k\Phi_{j},j=1,\dots,k, each of which is of the following form:

  1. (a)

    (Linear) Φj​(qj,pj)=pj\Phi_{j}(q_{j},p_{j})=p_{j};

  2. (b)

    (Elliptic) Φj​(qj,pj)=12​(qj2+pj2)\Phi_{j}(q_{j},p_{j})={\frac{1}{2}}(q_{j}^{2}+p_{j}^{2});

  3. (c)

    (Focus-focus) Φj​(qj,pj,qj+1,pj+1)=(qj​pj+qj+1​pj+1,qj​pj+1−qj+1​pj)\Phi_{j}(q_{j},p_{j},q_{j+1},p_{j+1})=(q_{j}p_{j}+q_{j+1}p_{j+1},q_{j}p_{j+1}-q_{j+1}p_{j}).

We are mostly concerned with the case of almost toric four-manifolds. Near any focus-focus singularity there is a pair of holomorphic disks, given as the unstable and stable manifolds for the first componen of Φj\Phi_{j}, as well as as a pair of Lagrangian vanishing thimbles, the anti-diagonals in the local model, whose images under the projection are shown in Figure 17.

The holomorphic and Lagrangian disks are related by a hyper-Kähler rotation. Recall [17] that a hyper-Kähler manifold is a datum (X,g,I,J,K)(X,g,I,J,K) consisting of a smooth manifold XX, necessarily of some dimension dim(X)\dim(X) that is a multiple of 44, and compatible Kähler structures

I,J,K:T​X→T​XI,J,K:TX\to TX

satisfying the relations

I2=J2=K2=−Id,I​J=K,J​K=I,K​I=J.I^{2}=J^{2}=K^{2}=-\operatorname{Id},\quad IJ=K,JK=I,KI=J.

The two-forms

ωI=g(⋅,I⋅),ωJ=g(⋅,J⋅),ωK=g(⋅,K⋅)∈Ω2(X)\omega_{I}=g(\cdot,I\cdot),\omega_{J}=g(\cdot,J\cdot),\omega_{K}=g(\cdot,K\cdot)\in\Omega^{2}(X)

are symplectic, and the hyper-Kähler rotation

(X,g,I,J,K)↦(X,g,J,I,−K)(X,g,I,J,K)\mapsto(X,g,J,I,-K)

interchanges special Lagrangians with respect to the form ωI\omega_{I} with holomorphic submanifolds with respect to II as in Strominger-Yau-Zaslow [32]. Explicitly, let

X=ℂ2={(q1+i​p1,q2+i​p2)}X=\mathbb{C}^{2}=\{(q_{1}+ip_{1},q_{2}+ip_{2})\}

and let I,JI,J be the complex structures defined by

I​(x1,x2)=(i​x1,i​x2),J​(x1,x2)=(−i​x2¯,i​x1).I(x_{1},x_{2})=(ix_{1},ix_{2}),\quad J(x_{1},x_{2})=(-i\overline{x_{2}},ix_{1}).

The almost complex manifold (X,J)(X,J) may be identified with (X,I)(X,I) by the map

(3) ϕ:ℂ2↦ℂ2,(q1+i​p1,q2+i​p2)↦(q1−i​p2,q2+i​p1).\phi:\mathbb{C}^{2}\mapsto\mathbb{C}^{2},\quad(q_{1}+ip_{1},q_{2}+ip_{2})\mapsto(q_{1}-ip_{2},q_{2}+ip_{1}).

Since ω​(D​ϕ​v,D​ϕ​J​v)=0\omega(D\phi v,D\phi Jv)=0, for any vector v∈T​Xv\in TX, the rotation maps any embedded holomorphic curve to a Lagrangian submanifold.

We now turn to the global picture for almost toric four-manifolds. Via the Arnold-Liouville theorem as explained in Duistermaat [8], the complement B−BfocB-B^{\operatorname{foc}} of the focus-focus values has an affine structure so that Φ\Phi is locally the moment map for a Hamiltonian torus action over B−BfocB-B^{\operatorname{foc}}. The image Φ​(X)\Phi(X) in BB is the moment polytope of XX. The set Φ​(X)\Phi(X) is locally polyhedral on B−BfocB-B^{\operatorname{foc}} with respect to the given affine structure. The monodromy of a small loop around each critical value b∈Bfocb\in B^{\operatorname{foc}} is an automorphism of the fiber given by

αb∈Aut⁡(Φ−1​(b))≅Aut⁡(T​Bℤ),αb​(ν)=ν+⟨Σb,ν⟩​Πb\alpha_{b}\in\operatorname{Aut}(\Phi^{-1}(b))\cong\operatorname{Aut}(TB_{\mathbb{Z}}),\quad\alpha_{b}(\nu)=\nu+\langle\Sigma_{b},\nu\rangle\Pi_{b}

for some vectors

(4) Σb∈Tb∨​B,Πb∈Tb​B\Sigma_{b}\in T^{\vee}_{b}B,\quad\Pi_{b}\in T_{b}B

defining the hyperplane and direction of the shear, respectively. We choose a collection of hyperplanes

C¯=(Ci⊂B,i∈I)\underline{C}=(C_{i}\subset B,i\in I)

called branch cuts (these are rays in the two-dimensional case) associated to the components of BfocB^{\operatorname{foc}}. Each branch cut CiC_{i} connects a focus-focus value b∈Bfocb\in B^{\operatorname{foc}} to the boundary ∂Φ​(X)\partial\Phi(X) of the moment polytope. The complement B−Bfoc−∪iCiB-B^{\operatorname{foc}}-\cup_{i}C_{i} has trivial monodromy and so, if simply connected, may be embedded in a vector space. The codomain BB equipped with the moment polytope Φ​(X)\Phi(X), the focus-focus values BfocB^{\operatorname{foc}} and branch cuts C¯\underline{C} is the almost toric base diagram of the almost toric manifold XX.

By a del Pezzo surface we mean a projective Fano surface. As del Pezzo showed (see Kollar-Smith-Corti [19]) any such surface is either a quadric surface or obtained by blowing up at most eight points in general position on the projective plane. By a result of McDuff [24], the monotone symplectic form in the first Chern class c1​(X)c_{1}(X) is unique up to symplectomorphism. The degree of a del Pezzo surface X=Blk⁡ℙ2X=\operatorname{Bl}^{k}\mathbb{P}^{2} is the self-intersection number of the canonical class

c1​(Blk⁡ℙ2)2=c1​(ℙ2)2−k=9−k.c_{1}(\operatorname{Bl}^{k}\mathbb{P}^{2})^{2}=c_{1}(\mathbb{P}^{2})^{2}-k=9-k.

In the case of monotone del Pezzo’s, we may take Φ​(X)\Phi(X) to be a convex polytope in ℝ2\mathbb{R}^{2} so that each shear has the direction of a ray connecting the barycenter with a vertex, as in Vianna [36]. Some of the almost toric diagrams for del Pezzo surfaces studied in [36] are shown in Figure 1.

2.2. Realizability for tropical Lagrangians

We introduce tropical Lagrangians in almost toric manifolds, following Hicks [15, Section 4] [13] [14, Section 7] and Mikhalkin [27] in the toric case; note that Matessi [23] has generalized the constructions to tropical hypersurfaces but here we are restricting to tropical Lagrangians corresponding to graphs.

Definition 2.2.

Let n>0n>0 be an integer. An affine manifold is a smooth nn-manifold BB equipped with a torsion-free flat connection on T​BTB, so that the transition maps with respect to flat coordinates are constant maps with values in ℝn⋉G​L​(n,ℝ)\mathbb{R}^{n}\ltimes GL(n,\mathbb{R}). An integral affine manifold is an affine manifold with the additional structure of a covariant-constant family of lattices Tb,ℤ​B⊂Tb​B,b∈BT_{b,\mathbb{Z}}B\subset T_{b}B,b\in B so that the transition maps may be taken to lie in ℝn⋉G​L​(n,ℤ)\mathbb{R}^{n}\ltimes GL(n,\mathbb{Z}). An integral affine two-manifold with singularities is a topological two-manifold BB, a finite set B0⊂BB_{0}\subset B and an integral affine structure on the complement B−B0B-B_{0}.

We have in mind the case that B0B_{0} is the set of focus-focus values for an almost toric moment map.

Definition 2.3.

Let BB be an integral affine two-manifold with singularities. A tropical graph in BB is a graph

Γ=(Vert⁡(Γ),Edge⁡(Γ))\Gamma=(\operatorname{Vert}(\Gamma),\operatorname{Edge}(\Gamma))

equipped with

  1. (a)

    ι:Vert⁡(Γ)→B\iota:\operatorname{Vert}(\Gamma)\to B and

  2. (b)

    for each e∈Edge∙⁡(Γ)e\in\operatorname{Edge}_{\bullet}(\Gamma) joining vertices, a geodesic

    γe:[0,ℓ​(e)]→B\gamma_{e}:[0,\ell(e)]\to B

    joining ι​(v−)\iota(v_{-}) and ι​(v+)\iota(v_{+}) given by a path γe\gamma_{e} with some covariant constant direction

    𝒯​(e):=dd​t​γe​(t)∈Tγ​(t),ℤ​B\mathcal{T}(e):=\frac{d}{dt}\gamma_{e}(t)\in T_{\gamma(t),\mathbb{Z}}B

    in the integral lattice Tγ​(t),ℤ​B,t∈(0,ℓ​(e))T_{\gamma(t),\mathbb{Z}}B,t\in(0,\ell(e)).

Definition 2.4.

A tropical graph Γ\Gamma is balanced if for any vertex ν\nu with at least two adjacent edges ϵ∋v\epsilon\ni v the balancing condition

∑ϵ∋ν𝒯​(ϵ)=0.\sum_{\epsilon\ni\nu}\mathcal{T}(\epsilon)=0.

holds.

We associate to certain tropical graphs Lagrangian submanifolds following Mikhalkin [27]. Let Λ\Lambda be a tropical graph in the moment polytope Φ​(X)⊂B\Phi(X)\subset B of an almost toric diagram with image of the focus-focus singularities denoted BfocB^{\operatorname{foc}}. For a face QQ of the polytope Φ​(X)\Phi(X), denote by TQi∨​BT^{\vee}_{Q_{i}}B the cotangent space of BB at any point q∈Qq\in Q; these are identified via the natural affine structure. Therefore, the tangent spaces to QiQ_{i} are canonically isomorphic. Our first examples will be embedded graphs; however in the sequel [39] we consider immersed graphs Λ\Lambda.

Definition 2.5.

A direction 𝒯​(ϵ)\mathcal{T}(\epsilon) of an edge ϵ∈Edge⁡(Λ)\epsilon\in\operatorname{Edge}(\Lambda) at a codimension two face QQ of the moment polytope Φ​(X)\Phi(X) contained in facets Qi,Qj⊂Φ​(X)Q_{i},Q_{j}\subset\Phi(X) with primitive normal vectors

λi∈Tqi∨​B,qi∈Qi,λj∈Tqj∨​B,qj∈Qj\lambda_{i}\in T^{\vee}_{q_{i}}B,\ q_{i}\in Q_{i},\quad\lambda_{j}\in T_{q_{j}}^{\vee}B,q_{j}\in Q_{j}

is a bisectrice if the pairings

⟨𝒯​(ϵ),λi⟩=⟨𝒯​(ϵ),λj⟩=1\langle\mathcal{T}(\epsilon),\lambda_{i}\rangle=\langle\mathcal{T}(\epsilon),\lambda_{j}\rangle=1

of the direction 𝒯​(ϵ)\mathcal{T}(\epsilon) with the normal vectors to the corresponding facets νi,νj\nu_{i},\nu_{j} are equal to 1.

Definition 2.6.

A tropical graph Λ⊂Φ​(X)\Lambda\subset\Phi(X) is allowable if

  1. (a)

    all univalent vertices are at the boundary ∂Φ​(X)\partial\Phi(X) or at the images BfocB^{\operatorname{foc}} of focus-focus singularities;

  2. (b)

    all vertices ν∈Vert⁡(Γ)\nu\in\operatorname{Vert}(\Gamma) have valence at most 33;

  3. (c)

    if ν∈Vert⁡(Γ)\nu\in\operatorname{Vert}(\Gamma) has valence 33 then the corresponding Mikhalkin determinant m​(ν)m(\nu) is equal to 11;

  4. (d)

    the edges ϵ∈Edge⁡(Λ)\epsilon\in\operatorname{Edge}(\Lambda) do not contain any focus-focus values b∈Bfocb\in B^{\operatorname{foc}} in their interiors ϵ∘⊂ϵ\epsilon^{\circ}\subset\epsilon;

  5. (e)

    if ν∈Vert⁡(Λ)\nu\in\operatorname{Vert}(\Lambda) is univalent then either Φ​(ν)\Phi(\nu) in a codimension two boundary face QQ of ∂Φ​(X)\partial\Phi(X) in which case the adjacent directions 𝒯​(ϵ)\mathcal{T}(\epsilon) are a bisectrice as defined above; and

  6. (f)

    if ν∈Vert⁡(Λ)\nu\in\operatorname{Vert}(\Lambda) is univalent and equal to a focus-focus value b∈Bfocb\in B^{\operatorname{foc}}, in which case the direction ±𝒯​(ϵ)\pm\mathcal{T}(\epsilon) of the adjacent edge ϵ∈Edge⁡(Λ)\epsilon\in\operatorname{Edge}(\Lambda) is equal to the direction Πb∈T​B\Pi_{b}\in TB of the associated shear from (4); 222We differ from Mikhalkin [27] in that we do not allow univalent vertices on facets of the moment polytope; these require non-orientable local models.

A tropical graph Λ\Lambda is primitive if

  1. (a)

    each edge direction 𝒯​(ϵ),ϵ∈Edge⁡(Λ)\mathcal{T}(\epsilon),\epsilon\in\operatorname{Edge}(\Lambda) is a primitive lattice vector in Tℤ​BT_{\mathbb{Z}}B,

  2. (b)

    there are no interior vertices v∈Vert⁡(Λ)v\in\operatorname{Vert}(\Lambda) of valence |v||v| more than three, and

  3. (c)

    each boundary vertex ν∈Vert⁡(Λ)\nu\in\operatorname{Vert}(\Lambda) has valence one if ν\nu lies in a codimension one or two face QQ of Φ​(X)\Phi(X).

Definition 2.7.

For any tropical graph Λ\Lambda with a trivalent vertex ν∈Vert⁡(Λ)\nu\in\operatorname{Vert}(\Lambda), define the local self-intersection number

δ​(ν)=12​(m​(ν)−1)\delta(\nu)={\frac{1}{2}}(m(\nu)-1)

where m​(ν)m(\nu) is the determinant from Definition 1.5 (b).

Choose a metric on BB and for δ>0\delta>0 let

Uδ​(Vert⁡(Λ))⊂BU_{\delta}(\operatorname{Vert}(\Lambda))\subset B

denote an δ\delta-neighborhood around the vertices Vert⁡(Λ)\operatorname{Vert}(\Lambda).

Definition 2.8.

A tropical graph Λ⊂Φ​(X)\Lambda\subset\Phi(X) is Lagrangian realizable if there exists a family of proper Lagrangian immersions, called Lagrangian realizations

ιρ:L→X,ρ∈(0,ρ0)\iota_{\rho}:L\to X,\ \rho\in(0,\rho_{0})

smoothly dependent ρ\rho with the following properties: For any δ>0\delta>0 there exists κ0\kappa_{0} so that for κ<κ0\kappa<\kappa_{0}

  1. (a)

    Φ​(ικ​(L))\Phi(\iota_{\kappa}(L)) is contained in the union of Λ\Lambda with Uδ​(Vert⁡(Λ))U_{\delta}(\operatorname{Vert}(\Lambda));

  2. (b)

    for each μ\mu in Λ−Uδ​(Vert⁡(Λ))\Lambda-U_{\delta}(\operatorname{Vert}(\Lambda)), the intersection of LL with Φ−1​(μ)≅Tn\Phi^{-1}(\mu)\cong T^{n} is an affine subtorus of the form a​(μ)​Aa(\mu)A for some codimension one torus A⊂TnA\subset T^{n} and a​(μ)∈Aa(\mu)\in A; and

  3. (c)

    for every vertex v∈Vert⁡(Λ)v\in\operatorname{Vert}(\Lambda), the inverse image Φ−1​(Uδ​(v))\Phi^{-1}(U_{\delta}(v)) is diffeomorphic to the product of a two-sphere S2S^{2} with some number of holes removed

    S2−∪i=1kBκi(vi)S^{2}-\cup_{i=1}^{k}B_{\kappa_{i}}(v_{i})

    times a torus Tn−2T^{n-2}, if vv does not map to BfocB^{\operatorname{foc}}; or a transverse self-intersection (ℝ2∪i​ℝ2)×(S1)n−2(\mathbb{R}^{2}\cup i\mathbb{R}^{2})\times(S^{1})^{n-2}, if vv maps to BfocB^{\operatorname{foc}}.

We call any realization of a tropical graph a tropical Lagrangian.

Theorem 2.9.

(following Mikhalkin [27] in the toric case, Hicks [14, Definition 7.1.0.1]) Any allowable embedded primitive tropical graph Λ\Lambda in the image Φ​(X)\Phi(X) of an almost toric fibration XX is tropically realizable by a Lagrangian LL whose genus is equal to the first Betti number of Λ\Lambda, and whose self-intersection number is the sum of the local self-intersections

[L].[L]=∑v∈Vert⁡(Λ)δ​(v).[L].[L]=\sum_{v\in\operatorname{Vert}(\Lambda)}\delta(v).

Mikhalkin [27] proves the special case of Theorem 2.9 for toric varieties. The proof in both this case and in the toric case relies on [27, Lemma 4.4] which describes how to patch together the local models for the Lagrangian near the vertices: Let ℂ×:=ℂ−{0}.\mathbb{C}^{\times}:=\mathbb{C}-\{0\}.

Lemma 2.10.

Let Φ:(ℂ×)n→ℝn\Phi:(\mathbb{C}^{\times})^{n}\to\mathbb{R}^{n} be the standard moment map arising from the identification (ℂ×)n≅(T∗​S1)n(\mathbb{C}^{\times})^{n}\cong(T^{*}S^{1})^{n}. Let ϵj⊂ℝn,j=1,2\epsilon_{j}\subset\mathbb{R}^{n},j=1,2 be two disjoint open intervals parallel to the same integer vector 𝒯​(ϵj)∈ℤn\mathcal{T}(\epsilon_{j})\in\mathbb{Z}^{n} and Lj⊂(ℂ×)nL_{j}\subset(\mathbb{C}^{\times})^{n} be two smooth connected Lagrangian submanifolds such that Φ​(Lj)⊂Ej\Phi(L_{j})\subset E_{j} , and LjL_{j} is relatively closed in Φ−1​(Ej)\Phi^{-1}(E_{j}). There exists a Lagrangian submanifold L⊂(ℂ×)nL\subset(\mathbb{C}^{\times})^{n} diffeomorphic to ℝ×(S1)n−1\mathbb{R}\times(S^{1})^{n-1} such that L1L_{1} and L2L_{2} are its submanifolds if and only if E1E_{1} and E2E_{2} belong to the same line E⊂ℝnE\subset\mathbb{R}^{n}. Furthermore, if E1,E2⊂EE_{1},E_{2}\subset E then the Lagrangian submanifold LL can be chosen so that Φ​(L)⊂E\Phi(L)\subset E.

Example 2.11.

The Lemma is proved using various local models.

  1. (a)

    (Lagrangian pair of pants) The model for trivalent vertices is the Lagrangian pair of pants from [27, Lemma 4.2, 4.3]. In coordinates zj=qj+i​pj∈ℂ×z_{j}={q_{j}+ip_{j}}\in\mathbb{C}^{\times}, j=1,2j=1,2 the holomorphic pair of pants given by the hyperplane

    H={z1+z2−1=0}⊂(ℂ×)2H=\{z_{1}+z_{2}-1=0\}\subset(\mathbb{C}^{\times})^{2}

    has hyper-Kähler rotation in the coordinates q1,p1,q2,p2q_{1},p_{1},q_{2},p_{2} (see (3)) the Lagrangian pair of pants

    L={1−(q1−i​p2)+(q2+i​p1)=0}⊂ℂ×,2.L=\{1-(q_{1}-ip_{2})+(q_{2}+ip_{1})=0\}\subset\mathbb{C}^{\times,2}.

    See Hicks [13, Section 3], Matessi [22], and also the definition of the surgery trace considered in Biran-Cornea [1]. The Lagrangian LL has three cylindrical ends, corresponding to the intersections with z1=0,z2=0z_{1}=0,z_{2}=0, and z1=z2=∞z_{1}=z_{2}=\infty.

  2. (b)

    (Diagonals) The diagonal embedding of L=ℝ2L=\mathbb{R}^{2} in X=ℝ4X=\mathbb{R}^{4} is a Lagrangian with a single cylindrical end, corresponding to a univalent vertex with a bisectrice edge.

  3. (c)

    (Vanishing thimbles) Consider the diagonal

    {p1=q1,p2=q2}⊂ℝ4.\{p_{1}=q_{1},p_{2}=q_{2}\}\subset\mathbb{R}^{4}.

    The image under the projection to the base

    {Φ​(q1,p1,q2,p2)=(q1​p1+q2​p2,q1​p2−q2​p1)}⊂ℝ2\{\Phi(q_{1},p_{1},q_{2},p_{2})=(q_{1}p_{1}+q_{2}p_{2},q_{1}p_{2}-q_{2}p_{1})\}\subset\mathbb{R}^{2}

    is a ray emanating out of the focus-focus value in the shear direction (1,0)(1,0). In the case of univalent or bivalent vertices mapping to the singular set, one can take the Lagrangian to be the vanishing thimble ℝ2×(S1)n−2\mathbb{R}^{2}\times(S^{1})^{n-2} resp. (ℝ2∪i​ℝ2)×(S1)n−2(\mathbb{R}^{2}\cup i\mathbb{R}^{2})\times(S^{1})^{n-2} in the local model for the focus-focus singularities.

Proof of Theorem 2.9.

Given the local models the statement of the first part of the Theorem now follows from Lemma 2.10. The proof of the self-intersection formula is the same as in Mikhalkin [25]. ∎

Remark 2.12.

By the construction in the proof of Lemma 2.10, the Lagrangian LL in Theorem 2.9 realizing a graph Λ\Lambda is unique up to isotopy. If LL is a sphere then LL is unique up to Hamiltonian isotopy since the first homology vanishes.

Remark 2.13.

There are other tropical graphs Γ\Gamma besides those described in Theorem 2.9 that are realizable in a similar sense: as pointed out to us by Annie Wei, the balancing condition ±𝒯​(e1)±𝒯​(e2)±𝒯​(e3)=0\pm\mathcal{T}(e_{1})\pm\mathcal{T}(e_{2})\pm\mathcal{T}(e_{3})=0 for any triple of edges e1,e2,e3e_{1},e_{2},e_{3} meeting at a trivalent vertex vv of Γ\Gamma is sufficient. See also the work of Hicks [13].

The following seems to be a folklore theorem known among experts:

Proposition 2.14.

Each monotone del Pezzo surface XX contains a Manin configuration of tropical Lagrangian spheres Lα,α∈SL_{\alpha},\alpha\in S of the corresponding simple root system SS.

Proof.

The configurations may be read off from the almost toric diagrams constructed in Vianna [36], as in Figure 19. For example, in the case of X=Bl3⁡(ℙ2)X=\operatorname{Bl}^{3}(\mathbb{P}^{2})

  • •

    the graphs Γ1,Γ2,Γ3\Gamma_{1},\Gamma_{2},\Gamma_{3} with no trivalent vertices, each consisting of a single segment, correspond to Lagrangian spheres L1,L2,L3L_{1},L_{2},L_{3} with intersection numbers

    Li⋅Li+1=1,∀i∈ℤ/3​ℤL_{i}\cdot L_{i+1}=1,\quad\forall i\in\mathbb{Z}/3\mathbb{Z}

    and give an affine A2A_{2} root system, while;

  • •

    a graph Γ0\Gamma_{0} with a single trivalent vertex gives an A1A_{1} root system.

In the case of X=Bl4⁡(ℙ2)X=\operatorname{Bl}^{4}(\mathbb{P}^{2}), the obvious graphs Γ1,…,Γ4\Gamma_{1},\ldots,\Gamma_{4} connecting the focus-focus values and two of the vertices give a collection of Lagrangian sphere L1,…,L4L_{1},\ldots,L_{4} forming A4A_{4} root system; adding the surgery L5L_{5} at each intersection point would give Lagrangians L1,…,L5L_{1},\ldots,L_{5} forming an affine A4A_{4} root system. In the remaining cases, the “obvious” tropical Lagrangians give an affine root system of the required type. ∎

Example 2.15.

Let XX be the blow-up of ℙ1×ℙ1\mathbb{P}^{1}\times\mathbb{P}^{1} at a torus-fixed point corresponding to a vertex of the moment polytope Φ​(X)=[0,1]2\Phi(X)=[0,1]^{2}. There is a Lagrangian sphere L⊂XL\subset X whose tropical graph Λ⊂ℝ2\Lambda\subset\mathbb{R}^{2} is the trivalent graph shown on the right in Figure 18. In fact, the Mikhalkin multiplicity at the unique vertex is equal to one, and so the self-intersection computed in Mikhalkin [27] is equal to zero.

2.3. Isotopies of tropical Lagrangians

For the purposes of counting disks in the next section, we wish to study Hamiltonian isotopies of Lagrangians associated to tropical graphs that change the vertices in the tropical graph:

Theorem 2.16.

Suppose an almost toric base diagram BB is modified by a nodal slide in which a critical value b∈Bfocb\in B^{\operatorname{foc}} passes through an edge ϵ\epsilon of the graph Λ\Lambda of a tropical Lagrangian LL in BB, resulting in a new tropical base diagram B′B^{\prime}. Then there exists a tropical graph Λ′\Lambda^{\prime} in B′B^{\prime} realized by a Lagrangian L′L^{\prime} isotopic to LL, with Λ′\Lambda^{\prime} obtained by adding an edge in the direction of the shear at bb.

Proof.

We describe how to obtain the new Lagrangian by a handle attachment. Let x∈Xx\in X be the focus-focus singularity corresponding to bb, and let ϕ:U→ℝ2\phi:U\to\mathbb{R}^{2} be a coordinate chart containing bb. By assumption, the projection to the base Φ|U\Phi|_{U} in the coordinates ϕ\phi is given by a pair of functions (Φ1,Φ2)(\Phi_{1},\Phi_{2}) so that the second component Φ2\Phi_{2} is Morse. Let {κ}×I⊂U\{\kappa\}\times I\subset U be disjoint from the critical value. By assumption, LL is a realization of Λ\Lambda, so we may assume LL is a circle bundle over an interval {κ}×I\{\kappa\}\times I. Let L′L^{\prime} denote the tropical Lagrangian corresponding to {−κ}×I\{-\kappa\}\times I. The function Φ2\Phi_{2} is Morse and the focus-focus point represents a singularity of Φ2\Phi_{2} of index 22; as in Milnor [28], gradient flow of Φ2\Phi_{2} defines a diffeomorphism of the level sets of Φ2\Phi_{2} away from the unstable and unstable manifolds. Since LL intersects the stable manifold in a single point, L′L^{\prime} is isotopic to the manifold obtained from LL by adding a handle, that is, removing a small disk from LL and gluing in an isotropic disk (the vanishing thimble) passing through the critical point.

In order to construct an isotopy of Lagrangians, we construct an isotopy of holomorphic curves and then perform a hyper-Kähler rotation. Let XX be the blow-up ℙ1×ℙ1\mathbb{P}^{1}\times\mathbb{P}^{1} at (z,0)(z,0) for some point z≠0,∞z\neq 0,\infty. We view XX as a Lefschetz fibration over ℙ1\mathbb{P}^{1} with a single nodal fiber. The union YY of the proper transforms of the divisors

P1×{0},ℙ1×{∞},{0}×ℙ1,{∞}×{0}P^{1}\times\{0\},\quad\mathbb{P}^{1}\times\{\infty\},\quad\{0\}\times\mathbb{P}^{1},\quad\{\infty\}\times\{0\}

is naturally identified ℂ2−{z1​z2=1}\mathbb{C}^{2}-\{z_{1}z_{2}=1\}. Indeed, we may identify XX the the blow-up of ℙ1×ℙ1\mathbb{P}^{1}\times\mathbb{P}^{1} at (0,0)(0,0). Removing three divisors gives the torus-invariant chart ℂ2\mathbb{C}^{2} containing the nodal point, and then X−YX-Y is identified with the complement of a single fiber of the map ℂ2→ℂ,(z1,z2)↦z1​z2\mathbb{C}^{2}\to\mathbb{C},(z_{1},z_{2})\mapsto z_{1}z_{2}. In particular,

𝕏:=X−Y\mathbb{X}:=X-Y

is a cylindrical-end manifold with a hyper-Kähler structure. After hyper-Kähler rotation we obtain a singular Lagrangian fibration with a single nodal fiber with a single node, giving a local model for a focus-focus singularity.

We produce the needed family of Lagrangians as a rotation of a family of holomorphic curves. Let

ut:ℙ1→𝕏,z↦(z,t)u_{t}:\mathbb{P}^{1}\to\mathbb{X},\quad z\mapsto(z,t)

be the family of sections taking non-zero values. The Gromov limit u0u_{0} of the family of stable maps utu_{t} is described as follows, and shown in Figure 20. The image of u0u_{0} is the union of the proper transform of the zero section with the exceptional divisor EE. Indeed, the homology class [u0][u_{0}] of u0u_{0} must equal that of [ut][u_{t}] by preservation of homology class and the limit must project to ℙ1×{0}\mathbb{P}^{1}\times\{0\} in ℙ1×ℙ1\mathbb{P}^{1}\times\mathbb{P}^{1}.

We now compute the limit in the sense of symplectic field theory, treating the target as a manifold with cylindrical end. The symplectic field theory limit of utu_{t} has two levels (u+,u−)(u_{+},u_{-}) described as follows.

  • •

    The map u+u_{+} is the (unique up to isomorphism) relative stable map in the total space of the degree-−1-1 line bundle 𝒪​(−1)→ℙ1\mathcal{O}(-1)\to\mathbb{P}^{1}, so that u+u_{+} has graph the trivalent graph with directions (−1,−1),(0,1),(1,0)(-1,-1),(0,1),(1,0), and has cylindrical puncture at the fiber corresponding to the exceptional curve.

  • •

    The map u−u_{-} is the map to 𝕏\mathbb{X} whose image is contained in exceptional curve EE.

Indeed, the broken map (u+,u−)(u_{+},u_{-}) must map to the stable map u0u_{0} of the previous paragraph, under the canonical functor from broken maps to stable maps. This functor assigns to any neck piece the composition with the projection 𝒪​(−1)→ℙ1\mathcal{O}(-1)\to\mathbb{P}^{1}, and assigns to any finite energy map to the complement of the zero section, the map to XX obtained by removal of singularities. In particular, the sft limit of utu_{t} must have at least two components. one of which is the map u−u_{-}. The map u+u_{+} must satisfy a matching condition with u−u_{-}, and so meet infinity at the the fiber corresponding the exceptional curve. By conservation of homology class u+u_{+} must meet the fibers at 0 and ∞\infty, and have zero intersection with the zero section. It follows that u−u_{-} is the section with a single pole, as claimed.

By hyper-Kähler rotation (3) in 𝕏\mathbb{X}, one obtains a family LtL_{t} of Lagrangians limiting as t→0t\to 0 (in the Gromov sense, pre-rotation) to a gluing L∞L_{\infty} (in the sense of gluing of pseudoholomorphic curves) of a curve L∞,−L_{\infty,-} corresponding to a trivalent graph and a punctured disk L∞,+L_{\infty,+}, and as t→∞t\to\infty to a cylinder. That is, as the parameter t→0t\to 0, the curve L∞L_{\infty} Gromov-converges to the union of L∞,−L_{\infty,-} and L∞,+L_{\infty,+}.

To check that the Lagrangians are exact isotopic, it suffices to show that the areas of homotopy classes of disks are unchanged. Let LtL_{t} be an isotopy from LL to L′L^{\prime}. It suffices to show that for each isotopy class of disks u:S→Xu:S\to X with boundary in LL , there exists an isotopy utu_{t} of uu bounding LtL_{t} so that the areas A​(ut)A(u_{t}) are constant in tt. We may assume, by deforming the boundary u​(∂S)u(\partial S) away from the attaching sphere, that the disk boundary u​(∂S)u(\partial S) lies in the region V⊂LV\subset L so that the intersection V∩LtV\cap L_{t} is independent of tt. Indeed the region VV is the complement of a small ball in LL. Then the constant disk ut=uu_{t}=u extends over the isotopy. ∎

Remark 2.17.

Applying mutations to an Manin configuration Lα,α∈SL_{\alpha},\alpha\in S for a root system SS for some base diagram BB produces new Manin configurations Lα′,α∈SL^{\prime}_{\alpha},\alpha\in S whose tropical graphs Λ\Lambda lie in the mutated toric base diagram B′B^{\prime}. An example in the case of the del Pezzo of degree one shown in Figure 21.

Proposition 2.18.

Let X=Bl2⁡ℙ2=Bl1⁡(ℙ1×ℙ1)X=\operatorname{Bl}^{2}\mathbb{P}^{2}=\operatorname{Bl}^{1}(\mathbb{P}^{1}\times\mathbb{P}^{1}) equipped with a monotone symplectic form. There is a unique Hamiltonian isotopy class of embedded Lagrangian spheres in XX containing the inverse image LL of the diagonal in ℙ1×ℙ1\mathbb{P}^{1}\times\mathbb{P}^{1}. In particular, the Lagrangian L′L^{\prime} whose tropical graph Λ\Lambda is the trivalent graph shown in the last diagram in Figure 22 (discussed in Example 2.15) is Hamiltonian isotopic to LL, by a Hamiltonian isotopy (Lt)t∈[0,1](L_{t})_{t\in[0,1]} so that every LtL_{t} is a tropical realization of some graph in a diagram for XX, except for a single value of tt.

Example 2.19.

The existence of the Hamiltonian isotopy is a special case of a generalization of a result of Hind [16] due to Evans [9]: For the del Pezzo surfaces Blk⁡ℙ2\operatorname{Bl}^{k}\mathbb{P}^{2} for k≤4k\leq 4 and any given Lagrangian homology class [L]∈H2​(X)[L]\in H_{2}(X), the Lagrangian spheres LL in [L][L] are related by Hamiltonian isotopy. The isotopy of the Proposition can be constructed explicitly using the remarks in Section 2.3. The second diagram in Figure 22 is obtained from the first by a nodal trade (see [34, Section 2]); the third by the handle attachment in Theorem 2.16; the fourth by a reverse nodal trade; the fifth and sixth by change of basis.

3. Tropical limits of holomorphic disks

We describe the degeneration techniques from Venugopalan-Woodward [35]. The moduli space of disks is cobordant to a moduli space of broken disks, each of which has an associated tropical graph. The cobordism implies tropical formulas for the disks potential and open-closed map.

3.1. Broken Maps

Broken maps arise as a limit of neck stretching associated to some polyhedral decomposition of an almost toric fibration. Suppose that BB is the base of an almost toric fibration, so that B−BfocB-B^{\operatorname{foc}} has an affine structure.

Definition 3.1.

A polyhedral decomposition is a collection 𝒫={P}\mathcal{P}=\{P\} of subsets P⊂BP\subset B so

  1. (a)

    each PP is locally a simple rational convex polyhedra with respect to the affine structure on B−BfocB-B^{\operatorname{foc}}

  2. (b)

    each face of P∈𝒫P\in\mathcal{P} is also in 𝒫\mathcal{P}, and

  3. (c)

    the intersection of any two elements of 𝒫\mathcal{P} is either empty or a face of each.

Definition 3.2.

A polyhedral decomposition 𝒫\mathcal{P} is admissible for XX if each element P∈𝒫P\in\mathcal{P} is a simple polytope that is transverse to the moment map Φ:X→B\Phi:X\to B, and so the inverse image Φ−1​(P)\Phi^{-1}(P) of each P∈𝒫P\in\mathcal{P} is a smooth submanifold of codimension codim⁡(P)\operatorname{codim}(P).

For an admissible decomposition, if dim(B)=2\dim(B)=2 then each component of BfocB^{\operatorname{foc}} is contained in the interior of some polyhedron. In higher dimension, the functions cutting out the polytopes are smooth components of the map Φ:X→B\Phi:X\to B. Let 𝔱P⊂Tb​B\mathfrak{t}_{P}\subset T_{b}B denote the vector space that is the annihilator of the tangent space to PP, independent up to isomorphism of the choice of b∈Pb\in P. Let TPT_{P} denote the torus with Lie algebra 𝔱P\mathfrak{t}_{P}, and TP​(v),ℂT_{P(v),\mathbb{C}} its complexification. By assumption, TPT_{P} acts with finite stabilizers on Φ−1​(P)\Phi^{-1}(P), for each P∈𝒫P\in\mathcal{P}.

A polyhedral decomposition leads to a degeneration of the given symplectic manifold. By definition, the composition of Φ:X→B\Phi:X\to B with the projection near Φ−1​(P)\Phi^{-1}(P) to 𝔱P∨\mathfrak{t}_{P}^{\vee} is a moment map for a TPT_{P} action near Φ−1​(P)\Phi^{-1}(P). The degeneration associated to 𝒫\mathcal{P} is the union

X𝒫=∪P∈𝒫int⁡(XP),int⁡(XP)=Φ−1​(int⁡(P))/TP.X_{\mathcal{P}}=\cup_{P\in\mathcal{P}}\operatorname{int}(X_{P}),\quad\operatorname{int}(X_{P})=\Phi^{-1}(\operatorname{int}(P))/T_{P}.

We denote by

XP:=⋃Q⊂Pint⁡XQX_{P}:=\bigcup_{Q\subset P}\operatorname{int}X_{Q}

the union over faces QQ of PP. Because of the simplicity assumption on the elements P∈𝒫P\in\mathcal{P}, for admissible decompositions each XPX_{P} is a symplectic orbifold (although a precise definition of orbifold is not necessary for this paper.) Existence of a cutting data for the cuts considered in this paper is proved in [35, Proposition 3.28, Example 3.30]. Associated to each face PP we have a thickened piece

(5) 𝕏P=Φ−1​(P)×𝔱P∨\mathbb{X}_{P}=\Phi^{-1}(P)\times\mathfrak{t}_{P}^{\vee}

which is equipped with an almost complex structure invariant under translation by the 𝔱P\mathfrak{t}_{P}-action. Denote by

𝕏=⋃P∈𝒫𝕏P\mathbb{X}=\bigcup_{P\in\mathcal{P}}\mathbb{X}_{P}

the disjoint union.

The neck-stretching limit for requires the choice of cutting data describing the rate of neck stretching in each neck: for each polytope P∈𝒫P\in\mathcal{P}, a dual polyhedron

P∨⊂𝔱P∨P^{\vee}\subset\mathfrak{t}_{P}^{\vee}

of top dimension so that whenever QQ is a face of PP, the inclusion of 𝔱P\mathfrak{t}_{P} in 𝔱Q\mathfrak{t}_{Q} induces an isomorphism of P∨P^{\vee} with a face of Q∨Q^{\vee}; see [35] for a more detailed description. An example is given in Figure 23 for the degree one del Pezzo, from [34].

The results in Venugopalan-Woodward [35] on the limits of holomorphic disks under neck-stretching extend to Lagrangian boundary conditions under translation-invariance conditions on the Lagrangians near the facets of the polytope.

Definition 3.3.

(Partial translation actions on the necks) For each P∈𝒫P\in\mathcal{P}, identify a neighborhood of Φ−1​(P)×{0}\Phi^{-1}(P)\times\{0\} in 𝕏P\mathbb{X}_{P} with a neighborhood of Φ−1​(P)\Phi^{-1}(P) in XX; this neighborhood is then equipped with a partial action of the group 𝔱P∨\mathfrak{t}_{P}^{\vee} by translation in the second factor in (5) under the identification with an open subset of Φ−1​(P)×𝔱P∨\Phi^{-1}(P)\times\mathfrak{t}_{P}^{\vee}, meaning an open subset of {0×𝕏P\{0\ \times\mathbb{X}_{P} in 𝔱P∨×𝕏P\mathfrak{t}_{P}^{\vee}\times\mathbb{X}_{P} so that the partial translation map

U→𝕏P,(t,(t0,l))↦(t+t0,l)U\to\mathbb{X}_{P},(t,(t_{0},l))\mapsto(t+t_{0},l)

is defined and satisfies the usual axioms for an action. Similarly, for any face QQ of PP, the inverse image of an open neighborhood of QQ in the thickening 𝕏P\mathbb{X}_{P} has a partial action of 𝔱Q\mathfrak{t}_{Q}.

Definition 3.4.
  1. (a)

    A subset L⊂XL\subset X is translation-invariant for P∈𝒫P\in\mathcal{P} if t​l∈Ltl\in L for all such pairs (t,l)∋𝔱P∨×L(t,l)\ni\mathfrak{t}_{P}^{\vee}\times L for which the action is defined.

  2. (b)

    A breakable Lagrangian with respect to a polyhedral decomposition 𝒫\mathcal{P} for XX is a Lagrangian submanifold L⊂XL\subset X so that for any P∈𝒫P\in\mathcal{P}, the Lagrangian LL is translation-invariant in a neighborhood of Φ−1​(P)∩L\Phi^{-1}(P)\cap L.

Remark 3.5.

Breakable Lagrangians give rise to totally-real submanifolds in cylinders as follows. The product

𝕃P:=(XP∩L)×𝔱P∨{\mathbb{L}}_{P}:=(X_{P}\cap L)\times\mathfrak{t}_{P}^{\vee}

is a totally real submanifold in the product 𝕏P=Φ−1​(P)×𝔱P∨\mathbb{X}_{P}=\Phi^{-1}(P)\times\mathfrak{t}_{P}^{\vee}. Note that while 𝕏P\mathbb{X}_{P} has an (at least orbifold) compactification 𝕏¯P\overline{\mathbb{X}}_{P} whose image under the moment map is the product P×P∨P\times P^{\vee}, the Lagrangian 𝕃P{\mathbb{L}}_{P} typically does not have a smooth compactification but rather is a closed subset of an immersed Lagrangian submanifold with clean self-intersection; see [2, Section 4.1].

For our regularization scheme, we choose a collection of Donaldson hypersurfaces as follows. A broken Donaldson hypersurface for (𝕏,𝕃)(\mathbb{X},{\mathbb{L}}) is a collection of symplectic submanifolds

(6) 𝔻¯=(DP)P∈𝒫\underline{\mathbb{D}}=(D_{P})_{P\in\mathcal{P}}

satisfying the compatibility condition [35, Section 5.3], so that the projection of DPD_{P} to XP=𝕏P/TℂX_{P}=\mathbb{X}_{P}/T_{\mathbb{C}} is a Donaldson hypersurface, and the image of 𝕃P{\mathbb{L}}_{P} in XPX_{P} is exact in the complement of DPD_{P}.

Lemma 3.6.

Let 𝕃=(𝕃P){\mathbb{L}}=({\mathbb{L}}_{P}) be a broken Lagrangian with the property that 𝕃P{\mathbb{L}}_{P} is empty whenever P∈𝒫P\in\mathcal{P} is a vertex. Then a Donaldson hypersurface exists for (𝕏,𝕃)(\mathbb{X},{\mathbb{L}}).

Proof.

The construction of such hypersurfaces for rational 𝕃{\mathbb{L}} used in this paper given in [35, Section 5.3] (for the case where the Lagrangians do not extend over the neck) and [2, Proposition 4.7] (for the case where the Lagrangians do extend over the neck). ∎

Broken maps are collections of holomorphic maps corresponding to vertices of tropical graphs defined as follows.

Definition 3.7.

For each P∈𝒫P\in\mathcal{P} denote the space of Reeb orbits

ℛ∙​(P)={γ:S1→Φ−1​(P),t↦tμ​x}\mathcal{R}_{\bullet}(P)=\{\gamma:S^{1}\to\Phi^{-1}(P),\ t\mapsto t^{\mu}x\}

where μ∈𝔱P,ℤ\mu\in\mathfrak{t}_{P,\mathbb{Z}} is some integral direction and x∈Φ−1​(P)x\in\Phi^{-1}(P). Similarly, for L⊂XL\subset X a breakable Lagrangian denote the space of Reeb chords

(7) ℛ∘​(P)={γ:[0,1]→Φ−1​(P),t↦tμ​x,γ​({0,1})⊂L}.\mathcal{R}_{\circ}(P)=\{\gamma:[0,1]\to\Phi^{-1}(P),\quad t\mapsto t^{\mu}x,\quad\gamma(\{0,1\})\subset L\}.

The set of directions of Reeb chords is

𝔱P,∘={μ∈𝔱P|tμ​x∈ℛ∘​(P)}.\mathfrak{t}_{P,\circ}=\{\mu\in\mathfrak{t}_{P}\ |\ t^{\mu}x\ \in\mathcal{R}_{\circ}(P)\}.

Analogously the set of directions of Reeb orbits is 𝔱P​(e),∙:=𝔱P​(e),ℤ\mathfrak{t}_{P(e),\bullet}:=\mathfrak{t}_{P(e),\mathbb{Z}}.

Lemma 3.8.

Let 𝒫\mathcal{P} be an admissible polyhedral decomposition for XX. For each polytope P∈𝒫P\in\mathcal{P}, the set 𝔱P,∘\mathfrak{t}_{P,\circ} of directions of Reeb chords is discrete.

Proof.

The statement of the Lemma follows immediately from the fact that the intersection of LL with each TPT_{P}-orbit in Φ−1​(P)\Phi^{-1}(P) is finite. Indeed, the Lagrangian LL is 𝔱P∨\mathfrak{t}_{P}^{\vee} invariant by assumption near Φ−1​(P)\Phi^{-1}(P). ∎

Definition 3.9.

An open-closed tropical graph for a polyhedral decomposition 𝒫\mathcal{P} is a tropical graph Γ\Gamma in B∨B^{\vee}, as in Definition 2.3, for which

  1. (a)

    the edge set Edge⁡(Γ)\operatorname{Edge}(\Gamma) consists of a collection of edges of finite type Edge<∞⁡(Γ)\operatorname{Edge}_{<\infty}(\mathbb{\Gamma}) each containing two vertices, and leaves Edge→⁡(Γ)\operatorname{Edge}_{\rightarrow}(\Gamma) each containing a single edge;

  2. (b)

    the edge and vertex sets admit partitions into subsets of closed resp. open subsets

    Edge⁡(Γ)=Edge∙⁡(Γ)∪Edge∘⁡(Γ),Vert⁡(Γ)=Vert∙⁡(Γ)∪Vert∘⁡(Γ)\operatorname{Edge}(\Gamma)=\operatorname{Edge}_{\bullet}(\Gamma)\cup\operatorname{Edge}_{\circ}(\Gamma),\quad\operatorname{Vert}(\Gamma)=\operatorname{Vert}_{\bullet}(\Gamma)\cup\operatorname{Vert}_{\circ}(\Gamma)

    corresponding to cylinders and strips, for edges of finite type, and holomorphic sphere and holomorphic disk components, for vertices; the leaves Edge→⁡(Γ)\operatorname{Edge}_{\rightarrow}(\Gamma) are all of interior type and correspond to intersections with the Donaldson hypersurface 𝔻\mathbb{D}; the open vertices Vert∘⁡(Γ)\operatorname{Vert}_{\circ}(\Gamma) and open edges Edge∘⁡(Γ)\operatorname{Edge}_{\circ}(\Gamma) are required to map to the dual complex Λ∨⊂B∨\Lambda^{\vee}\subset B^{\vee} of (1);

  3. (c)

    for each closed edge e∈Edge∙⁡(Γ)e\in\operatorname{Edge}_{\bullet}(\Gamma) a direction 𝒯​(e)\mathcal{T}(e) in the integral lattice

    𝔱P​(e),∙:=𝔱P​(e),ℤ\mathfrak{t}_{P(e),\bullet}:=\mathfrak{t}_{P(e),\mathbb{Z}}

    and

  4. (d)

    for each open edge e∈Edge∘⁡(Γ)e\in\operatorname{Edge}_{\circ}(\Gamma) the corresponding direction 𝒯​(e)\mathcal{T}(e) lies in the set 𝔱P​(e),∘\mathfrak{t}_{P(e),\circ} of Definition 3.7.

Definition 3.10.

Let Γ\Gamma be an open-closed tropical graph in B∨B^{\vee}. A broken map modelled on Γ\Gamma consists of the following data:

  1. (a)

    for each closed vertex v∈Vert∙⁡(Γ)v\in\operatorname{Vert}_{\bullet}(\Gamma), a holomorphic sphere

    uv:Sv→𝕏P​(v)u_{v}:S_{v}\to\mathbb{X}_{P(v)}

    with finite Hofer energy as defined in [35, Section 3] (so that the map uvu_{v} extends to a map u¯v\overline{u}_{v} to the compactification 𝕏¯P​(v)\overline{\mathbb{X}}_{P(v)} described in [35, Section 3]);

  2. (b)

    for each open vertex v∈Vert∘⁡(Γ),v\in\operatorname{Vert}_{\circ}(\Gamma), a holomorphic disk uvu_{v} in 𝕏P​(v)\mathbb{X}_{P(v)} with Lagrangian boundary condition in 𝕃P​(v){\mathbb{L}}_{P(v)} with finite Hofer energy (so that uvu_{v} extends to a disk in 𝕏¯P​(v)\overline{\mathbb{X}}_{P(v)} with boundary condition 𝕃¯P​(v)\overline{{\mathbb{L}}}_{P(v)});

  3. (c)

    at each node we=(we−,we+)w_{e}=(w^{-}_{e},w^{+}_{e}) corresponding to a closed edge ee of the tropical graph, a framing given by an isomorphism

    (8) fre:Twe+​Sv+⊗Twe−​Sv−→ℂ;{\operatorname{fr}}_{e}:T_{w^{+}_{e}}S_{v_{+}}\otimes T_{w^{-}_{e}}S_{v_{-}}\to\mathbb{C};
  4. (d)

    at each node we=(we−,we+)w_{e}=(w^{-}_{e},w^{+}_{e}) corresponding to an open edge ee of the tropical graph, a framing given by an isomorphism

    (9) fre:Twe+​Sv+⊗Twe−​Sv−→ℍ:={z≥0|z∈ℂ}{\operatorname{fr}}_{e}:T_{w^{+}_{e}}S_{v_{+}}\otimes T_{w^{-}_{e}}S_{v_{-}}\to\mathbb{H}:=\{z\geq 0|z\in\mathbb{C}\}

    mapping the tangent space to the boundary to ℝ\mathbb{R}; and

  5. (e)

    for each leaf e∈Edge→⁡(Γ)e\in\operatorname{Edge}_{\rightarrow}(\Gamma), a marking ze∈Cvz_{e}\in C_{v} mapping to the corresponding Donaldson hypersurface 𝔻P\mathbb{D}_{P} from (6);

satisfying the following matching conditions: For any holomorphic coordinate

(10) z±:(Uwe±,we±)→(ℂ,0)z_{\pm}:(U_{w_{e}^{\pm}},w_{e}^{\pm})\to(\mathbb{C},0)

in the neighborhood of nodal lifts we±w_{e}^{\pm} that respect the framing, that is,

(11) d⁡z+​(we+)⊗d⁡z−​(we−)=fre,{\operatorname{d}}z_{+}(w^{+}_{e})\otimes{\operatorname{d}}z_{-}(w^{-}_{e})={\operatorname{fr}}_{e},

the following matching condition is satisfied:

(12) limz−→0z−−𝒯​(e)​uv−​(z−)=limz+→0z+𝒯​(e)​uv+​(z+).\lim_{z_{-}\to 0}z_{-}^{-\mathcal{T}(e)}u_{v_{-}}(z_{-})=\lim_{z_{+}\to 0}z_{+}^{\mathcal{T}(e)}u_{v_{+}}(z_{+}).

Here z±±𝒯​(e):=exp⁡(±ln⁡(z±)​𝒯​(e))z_{\pm}^{\pm\mathcal{T}(e)}:=\exp(\pm\ln(z_{\pm})\mathcal{T}(e)) using the exponential map 𝔱P​(e)→TP​(e)\mathfrak{t}_{P(e)}\to T_{P(e)} and the partial translation action was defined in Definition 3.3.

The possibilities for the directions meeting vertices of rigid graphs corresponding to toric surfaces are particularly easy to describe: They correspond to triples satisfying the balancing condition described in (2); in general the description of possible vertices is more complicated. A sample polyhedral degeneration of the almost toric diagram of the degree four del Pezzo surface is shown in Figure 24.

Definition 3.11.

An open-closed map type Γ\mathbb{\Gamma} is an open-closed tropical graph Γ\Gamma with vertices v∈Vert⁡(Γ)v\in\operatorname{Vert}(\Gamma) labelled by homotopy classes [uv][u_{v}] of disks uvu_{v} (for open vertices vv) or spheres [uv][u_{v}] (for closed vertices vv). Denote by ℳΓ​(𝕏,𝕃)\mathcal{M}_{\mathbb{\Gamma}}(\mathbb{X},{\mathbb{L}}) the moduli space of maps of type Γ\mathbb{\Gamma}.

Definition 3.12.

(c.f. [35, Definition 8.48]) A broken map of type Γ\mathbb{\Gamma} is rigid if the tropical graph Γ\Gamma is rigid, the moduli space ℳΓ​(𝕏,𝕃)\mathcal{M}_{\mathbb{\Gamma}}(\mathbb{X},{\mathbb{L}}) is expected dimension zero, the stratum of the source moduli space ℳΓ\mathcal{M}_{\Gamma} is of codimension zero in the moduli space of treed disks. Let ℳ​(𝕏,𝕃)\mathcal{M}(\mathbb{X},{\mathbb{L}}) denote the collection of rigid broken maps, written as a union over rigid types Γ\mathbb{\Gamma}
