ℳ​(𝕏,𝕃)=⋃ΓℳΓ​(𝕏,𝕃).\mathcal{M}(\mathbb{X},{\mathbb{L}})=\bigcup_{\mathbb{\Gamma}}\mathcal{M}_{\mathbb{\Gamma}}(\mathbb{X},{\mathbb{L}}).
Definition 3.13.

The area of a broken map u=(uv)u=(u_{v}) of type Γ\mathbb{\Gamma} is the sum

(13) A​(u)=∑v∈Vert⁡(Γ)A​(uv),A​(uv):=A​(πP​(v)∘uv)A(u)=\sum_{v\in\operatorname{Vert}(\mathbb{\Gamma})}A(u_{v}),\quad A(u_{v}):=A(\pi_{P(v)}\circ u_{v})

of the areas of the maps uv∘πP​(v)u_{v}\circ\pi_{P(v)} to the symplectic manifolds XP​(v)X_{P(v)} obtained by composing uvu_{v} with the projection

πP​(v):𝕏P​(v)→XP​(v)\pi_{P(v)}:{\mathbb{X}}_{P(v)}\to{X}_{P(v)}

whose fibers are (either empty or) the complexified tori TP​(v),ℂT_{P(v),\mathbb{C}}.

Definition 3.14.

The Maslov index of a broken map u=(uv)u=(u_{v}) of type Γ\mathbb{\Gamma} is the sum of Maslov indices

(14) I​(u)=∑v∈Vert⁡(Γ)I​(uv),I(u)=\sum_{v\in\operatorname{Vert}(\mathbb{\Gamma})}I(u_{v}),

where I​(uv)I(u_{v}) is the Maslov index of the bundle pair defined as follows: Consider the compactification S¯v\overline{S}_{v} obtained by taking the real blow-up of SvS_{v} at the interior and boundary punctures; this has the effect of adding a circle at each interior puncture, and an interval at each boundary puncture. The bundles uv∗​T​𝕏P​(v)u_{v}^{*}T\mathbb{X}_{P(v)} and (∂uv)∗​T​𝕃P​(v)(\partial u_{v})^{*}T{\mathbb{L}}_{P(v)} have natural extensions to the compactification, given by adding the tangent space to 𝕏P​(v)\mathbb{X}_{P(v)} resp. 𝕃P​(v){\mathbb{L}}_{P(v)} along corresponding Reeb orbit resp. chord at infinity along the cylindrical or strip-like end. The bundle 𝕏P​(v)\mathbb{X}_{P(v)} along any Reeb orbit γ:S1→𝕏P​(v)\gamma:S^{1}\to\mathbb{X}_{P(v)} is trivializable, with a trivialization given by choose a trivialization at any point any translating along the Reeb orbit. Choose any Lagrangian subspace of Tγ​(0)​𝕏P​(v)T_{\gamma(0)}\mathbb{X}_{P(v)}; by translation one obtains a Lagrangian sub-bundle along γ\gamma. Denote by (T​𝕏P​(v))∞,ℝ(T\mathbb{X}_{P(v)})_{\infty,\mathbb{R}} the Lagrangian sub-bundle over the circles at the limits of the cylindrical ends obtained in this way. Then the Maslov index of uvu_{v} is defined by

I​(uv):=I​(uv∗​T​𝕏P​(v),(∂uv)∗​T​𝕃P​(v)∪(T​𝕏P​(v))∞,ℝ).I(u_{v}):=I(u_{v}^{*}T\mathbb{X}_{P(v)},(\partial u_{v})^{*}T{\mathbb{L}}_{P(v)}\cup(T\mathbb{X}_{P(v)})_{\infty,\mathbb{R}}).
Remark 3.15.

Associated to any broken map u=(uv)v∈Vert⁡(Γ)u=(u_{v})_{v\in\operatorname{Vert}(\Gamma)} is a homology class in [u]∈H2​(X,ℤ)[u]\in H_{2}(X,\mathbb{Z}), defined by [u]=[u~][u]=[\tilde{u}] where u~:C→X\tilde{u}:C\to X is the result of the pre-gluing procedure in Venugopalan-Woodward [35, Section 9.1] to (uv)(u_{v}), using any collection of gluing parameters to glue along cylindrical and strip-like ends. Then A​(u)=A​(u~)A(u)=A(\tilde{u}) and I​(u)=I​(u~)I(u)=I(\tilde{u}), by standard gluing properties of area and index.

Associated to the leaves of the graph are various evaluation maps. Denote by Edge→⁡(Γ)\operatorname{Edge}_{\rightarrow}(\Gamma) the leaves of Γ\Gamma. Associated to each edge e∈Edge→⁡(Γ)e\in\operatorname{Edge}_{\rightarrow}(\Gamma) with zero direction 𝒯​(e)=0\mathcal{T}(e)=0 is an evaluation map

eve:ℳΓ​(𝕏,𝕃)→𝕃P​(e).\operatorname{ev}_{e}:\mathcal{M}_{\Gamma}(\mathbb{X},{\mathbb{L}})\to{\mathbb{L}}_{P(e)}.
Definition 3.16.

A system of constraints for a map type Γ\mathbb{\Gamma} is a collection of oriented compact embedded submanifolds

𝕐¯=(𝕐e⊂𝕃P​(e))e∈Edge→⁡(Γ)\underline{\mathbb{Y}}=(\mathbb{Y}_{e}\subset{\mathbb{L}}_{P(e)})_{e\in\operatorname{Edge}_{\rightarrow}(\mathbb{\Gamma})}

as follows:

  1. (a)

    If e∈Edge∙⁡(Γ)e\in\operatorname{Edge}_{\bullet}(\Gamma) is a closed leaf then 𝕐e\mathbb{Y}_{e} is a compact, oriented, submanifold of the space of Reeb orbits ℛ∙​(P​(e))=𝕏P​(e)\mathcal{R}_{\bullet}(P(e))=\mathbb{X}_{P(e)}.

  2. (b)

    If e∈Edge∘⁡(Γ)e\in\operatorname{Edge}_{\circ}(\Gamma) is an open leaf, representing a strip-like end, then 𝕐e\mathbb{Y}_{e} is a compact oriented submanifold of the space of Reeb chords ℛ∘​(P​(e))\mathcal{R}_{\circ}(P(e)) as in (7).

Denote by ℳ​(𝕏,𝕃,𝕐¯)⊂ℳ​(𝕏,𝕃)\mathcal{M}(\mathbb{X},{\mathbb{L}},\underline{\mathbb{Y}})\subset\mathcal{M}(\mathbb{X},{\mathbb{L}}) the moduli space with the given constraints.

Definition 3.17.

A cartoon diagram of a broken map u=(uv)u=(u_{v}) is a collection of subsets

ℭ=(ℭv⊂P​(v))\mathfrak{C}=(\mathfrak{C}_{v}\subset P(v))

so that

  1. (a)

    each ℭv\mathfrak{C}_{v} is the moment image ΦP​(v)∘uv′​(Cv)\Phi_{P(v)}\circ u^{\prime}_{v}(C_{v}) for some smooth map uv′u_{v}^{\prime} in the homotopy class of uvu_{v}, and

  2. (b)

    near any intersection of Φv\Phi_{v} with the polytope P​(e)P(e) corresponding to an adjacent edge e∋ve\ni v, Φ​(v)\Phi(v) is given by a path which meets P​(e)P(e) at a point with tangent given by the direction 𝒯​(e)\mathcal{T}(e).

Remark 3.18.

The tropical graph can be constructed from the cartoon diagram, by reading off the directions of the edges at the vertices.

3.2. Tropical limit theorems

The results of [35] on the broken limit of holomorphic maps require minor modifications in the definitions and results for Lagrangians extending over the neck.

Theorem 3.19.

( Venugopalan-Woodward [35] for moment fibers, Chanda [4] for Lagrangians passing through the neck) Let L⊂XL\subset X be a breakable monotone Lagrangian submanifold for a polyhedral decomposition 𝒫\mathcal{P}. There exists a compact one-dimensional cobordism from the moduli space ℳ​(X,L,Y¯)\mathcal{M}(X,L,\underline{Y}) of rigid disks of Maslov-index-two passing through a generic point Y¯=({p}⊂L)\underline{Y}=(\{p\}\subset L) to the moduli space of rigid broken disks of Maslov-index-two passing through a generic point Y¯={p}⊂𝕃\underline{Y}=\{p\}\subset{\mathbb{L}}

ℳ​(X,L,Y¯)∼⋃ΓℳΓ​(𝕏,𝕃,Y¯).\mathcal{M}(X,L,\underline{Y})\sim\bigcup_{\Gamma}\mathcal{M}_{\Gamma}(\mathbb{X},{\mathbb{L}},\underline{Y}).
Sketch of Proof.

We describe the other modifications of the results of Venugopalan-Woodward [35] necessary to apply the degenerations in cases where the Lagrangians extend over the neck; unfortunately, the notation in [35] was already so involved that we felt that this additional layer of complication would make the exposition there even longer.

  1. (a)

    (Exponential Convergence) In the proof of [35, Theorem 8.2], to obtain exponential convergence along the ends we used the fact that the composition of a holomorphic map to 𝕏P​(v)\mathbb{X}_{P(v)} with projection to the base XP​(v)X_{P(v)} satisfies removal of singularities. For each vv, consider the projection 𝕏P​(v)→XP​(v)\mathbb{X}_{P(v)}\to X_{P(v)} with fibers TP​(v),ℂT_{P(v),\mathbb{C}}. The various branches of the Lagrangian 𝕃P​(v){\mathbb{L}}_{P(v)} project to a fixed Lagrangian LP​(v)L_{P(v)} in XP​(v)X_{P(v)}. Each fiber is a collection of copies of the Lie algebra 𝔱P​(v).\mathfrak{t}_{P(v)}. The necessary exponential convergence results follow by considering the projection to the base. There one has the usual annulus lemma. Applying the arguments in the proof of [35, Proposition 8.18] to obtain the necessary exponential decay results for the difference between the given map and a constant cylinder.

  2. (b)

    (Directions of open edges) In [35, Definition 4.25], there is no notion of primitive direction for edges corresponding to boundary nodes. Instead, the boundary edges have only rational directions.

  3. (c)

    (Monotonicity) The monotonicity [35, Lemma 7.21] for pseudoholomorphic maps requires the version for Lagrangian boundary conditions, as in Cieliebak-Ekholm-Latschev [6, Lemma 3.6].

  4. (d)

    (Removal of singularities) The argument in removal of singularities [35, Section 7.5] requires modification to include the case of boundary punctures. In this case, one uses the fact that the closure of the Lagrangian in the compactification of each piece has clean self-intersection, and removal of singularities for clean intersection is well-known; see for example Schmäschke [30].

  5. (e)

    (Gromov convergence) The definition of Gromov convergence of [35, Section 8.1] requires inclusion of the case of strips passing through the neck; cylinders in the definition are replaced by cylinders or strips in the definition.

  6. (f)

    (Fredholm theory) In [35, Section 6.4] on Fredholm theory, one needs that the linearized Cauchy-Riemann operator on the given weighted spaces with totally real boundary conditions is Fredholm. In the case that the almost complex structure is compatible, the tangential part of the linearized operator to the boundary at infinity is self-adjoint and the Fredholm property is standard as in [37, Lemma 3.5]. The case that the almost complex structure is only tamed seems not to be treated in the literature, but the Fredholm property for the linearized Cauchy-Riemann operator seems to follow from Coriasco-Schrohe-Seiler [7, Theorem 2.22]. However, we only need the case that the almost complex structure is compatible.

The remaining changes in the proof are straightforward (replacing cylinders by cylinders or strips in the proofs, depending on the type of edges.) ∎

Remark 3.20.

In particular, holomorphic disks in the limit always correspond to tropical graphs, rather than tropical subvarieties of higher dimension as in [12, Figure 3].

3.3. Relative maps

We introduce the following terminology of relative maps similar to that of levels in symplectic field theory, so that a broken map is a collection of relative maps satisfying matching conditions.

Definition 3.21.

Given a tropical graph Γ\Gamma denote by Edge×⁡(Γ)⊂Edge⁡(Γ)\operatorname{Edge}_{\times}(\Gamma)\subset\operatorname{Edge}(\Gamma) the set of edges ee with zero direction 𝒯​(e)=0\mathcal{T}(e)=0. Collapsing the zero-direction edges Edge×⁡(Γ)\operatorname{Edge}_{\times}(\Gamma) in Γ\Gamma gives a graph Γ0\Gamma_{0} equipped with a tropical structure so that every edge e∈Γ0e\in\Gamma_{0} has non-zero direction 𝒯​(e)≠0\mathcal{T}(e)\neq 0.For each vertex v0∈Vert⁡(Γ0)v_{0}\in\operatorname{Vert}(\Gamma_{0}), let Γv0\Gamma_{v_{0}} denote its inverse image; the corresponding collection of components uv0:=(uv,v∈Γv0)u_{v_{0}}:=(u_{v},v\in\Gamma_{v_{0}}) will be called a level of the broken map uu, and the edges collapsed under the morphism Γ→Γ0\Gamma\to\Gamma_{0} correspond to nodes of uv0u_{v_{0}}.

We view the levels of a broken map as relative maps to a cylindrical end manifold as follows. We give a utilitarian definition, since all of our cylindrical end manifolds arise from symplectic cutting and removing divisors.

Definition 3.22.
  1. (a)

    A cylindrical end symplectic manifold is a component X=𝕏PX=\mathbb{X}_{P} of a broken manifold 𝕏\mathbb{X} as defined in [34], with PP some polytope in a polyhedral decomposition 𝒫\mathcal{P}.

  2. (b)

    A cylindrical end Lagrangian is a component L=𝕃PL={\mathbb{L}}_{P} of a broken Lagrangian 𝕃=(𝕃P){\mathbb{L}}=({\mathbb{L}}_{P}) contained in 𝕏P\mathbb{X}_{P}.

Remark 3.23.

In particular, any cylindrical end symplectic manifold XX has a map Φ\Phi to the interior int⁡(P)\operatorname{int}(P) of a polytope P⊂BP\subset B, with the property that for any face QQ of PP, there is an open neighborhood UU of QQ in PP and a symplectomorphism from Φ−1​(U)\Phi^{-1}(U) to a manifold 𝕏Q=ZQ×𝔱Q∨\mathbb{X}_{Q}=Z_{Q}\times\mathfrak{t}_{Q}^{\vee} as in (5), where ZQZ_{Q} is a smooth manifold equipped with an action of TQT_{Q} with only finite stabilizers. The two-form on 𝕏Q\mathbb{X}_{Q} is given by

ω=π1∗​ωQ+d⁡⟨αQ,π2∗⟩\omega=\pi_{1}^{*}\omega_{Q}+{\operatorname{d}}\langle\alpha_{Q},\pi_{2}^{*}\rangle

where ωQ\omega_{Q} is a closed two-form on ZQZ_{Q}, αQ\alpha_{Q} is a connection form for the TQT_{Q} action on ZQZ_{Q}, and π1,π2\pi_{1},\pi_{2} are projections on the factors.

Any cylindrical end manifold XX has a natural orbifold compactification, obtained by adding back in the divisors XQX_{Q} corresponding to faces of P×P∨P\times P^{\vee}, as in [35, Section 3]. The closure L¯\overline{L} of LL in X¯\overline{X} is a subset of a cleanly-self-intersecting immersed Lagrangian submanifold, as in [2, Proposition 2.22].

Our previous work [34] defines relative maps in XX with boundary in LL as components of broken maps. Moduli spaces of relative maps ℳΓ​(X,L)\mathcal{M}_{\mathbb{\Gamma}}(X,L) have additional evaluation maps at the strip-like and cylindrical ends given framings.

Definition 3.24.

Given a subset Edge−⁡(Γ)\operatorname{Edge}^{-}(\mathbb{\Gamma}) of the set of edges Edge⁡(Γ)\operatorname{Edge}(\mathbb{\Gamma}), a relative map with partial framings is a relative map with the additional data of, for each edge e∈Edge−⁡(Γ)e\in\operatorname{Edge}^{-}(\mathbb{\Gamma}), a trivialization of the tangent space at the corresponding marked point wew_{e}

(15) fre:Twe​Sw​(e)→ℂ.{\operatorname{fr}}_{e}:T_{w_{e}}S_{w(e)}\to\mathbb{C}.

A local coordinate zz near the puncture zez_{e} is compatible with the framing if the differential d⁡z{\operatorname{d}}z is equal to fre{\operatorname{fr}}_{e}.

Remark 3.25.

For any leaf e∈Edge−⁡(Γ)e\in\operatorname{Edge}^{-}(\mathbb{\Gamma}) representing a boundary puncture we have an evaluation map

eve:ℳΓ​(X,L)→ℛ∘​(P​(e)),u↦(t↦lims→∞exp⁡(−𝒯​(e)​(s+i​t))​uv−​(s+i​t))\operatorname{ev}_{e}:\ \mathcal{M}_{\mathbb{\Gamma}}(X,L)\to\mathcal{R}_{\circ}(P(e)),\quad u\mapsto(t\mapsto\lim_{s\to\infty}\exp(-\mathcal{T}(e)(s+it))u_{v_{-}}(s+it))

where s+i​t=−ln⁡(z)s+it=-\ln(z) and zz is compatible with the framing. There are similar evaluation maps for cylindrical ends mapping to 𝕏P​(e)\mathbb{X}_{P(e)}.

Definition 3.26.

A system of constraints for a type Γ\mathbb{\Gamma} of relative map is a collection of submanifolds for a subset of constrained edges Edge−⁡(Γ)\operatorname{Edge}^{-}(\mathbb{\Gamma})

(16) 𝕐¯=(𝕐e)e∈Edge−⁡(Γ)\underline{\mathbb{Y}}=(\mathbb{Y}_{e})_{e\in\operatorname{Edge}^{-}(\mathbb{\Gamma})}

where each YeY_{e} is the target of the evaluation map eve\operatorname{ev}_{e}, that is, either the space of Reeb orbits, chords, or the Lagrangian itself.

Definition 3.27.

Given a system of constraints 𝕐¯\underline{\mathbb{Y}}, a constrained relative map is a relative map with framings at Edge−⁡(Γ)\operatorname{Edge}^{-}(\mathbb{\Gamma}) satisfying the following matching conditions: For any holomorphic coordinate

(17) z:(Uwe,we)→(ℂ,0)z:(U_{w_{e}},w_{e})\to(\mathbb{C},0)

that respect the framing, that is,

(18) d⁡z​(we)=fre.{\operatorname{d}}z(w_{e})={\operatorname{fr}}_{e}.

the following matching condition is satisfied:

(19) eve⁡(u)∈𝕐e.\operatorname{ev}_{e}(u)\in\mathbb{Y}_{e}.

In general, the counts of maps with constraints depend on the choice of almost complex structure, in the same way that counts of holomorphic disks depend on this choice in the closed case as well. However, in the prime case these counts are invariant.

Definition 3.28.

A stable map type Γ\mathbb{\Gamma} is prime if Γ\mathbb{\Gamma} does not admit a degeneration to different stable type Γ′\mathbb{\Gamma}^{\prime}. That is, with respect to the natural partial order ⪯\preceq on types corresponding to degeneration so that the boundary of ℳΓ​(𝕏,𝕃)\mathcal{M}_{\mathbb{\Gamma}}(\mathbb{X},{\mathbb{L}}) is contained in the union of strata ℳΓ′​(𝕏,𝕃)\mathcal{M}_{\mathbb{\Gamma}^{\prime}}(\mathbb{X},{\mathbb{L}}) for which Γ′≺Γ\mathbb{\Gamma}^{\prime}\prec\mathbb{\Gamma} there is no type Γ′\mathbb{\Gamma}^{\prime} so that Γ′≺Γ\mathbb{\Gamma}^{\prime}\prec\mathbb{\Gamma}.

Example 3.29.

Let Γ\mathbb{\Gamma} be map type so P​(v)P(v) is top-dimensional and A​(uv)A(u_{v}) is non-zero and minimal for each v∈Vert⁡(Γ)v\in\operatorname{Vert}(\Gamma), and the edges e∈Edge⁡(Γ)e\in\operatorname{Edge}(\mathbb{\Gamma}) have the property that no two directions 𝒯​(e1),𝒯​(e2)\mathcal{T}(e_{1}),\mathcal{T}(e_{2}) lie in the same dual cone to a face of PP. Then Γ\mathbb{\Gamma} is prime. Indeed, only possible degeneration corresponds to the formation of zero-area components containing at least two such edges e1,e2e_{1},e_{2}. Such a degeneration is impossible since 𝒯​(e1),𝒯​(e2)\mathcal{T}(e_{1}),\mathcal{T}(e_{2}) must lie in the dual cone to some face QQ of PP; see [35, (1.7)].

Lemma 3.30.

Let XX be a cylindrical end manifold and L⊂XL\subset X a cylindrical end Lagrangian, and let Γ\mathbb{\Gamma} be a prime type of broken map to XX with boundary in LL with a single vertex. Let Y¯=(Ye)e∈Γ\underline{Y}=(Y_{e})_{e\in\mathbb{\Gamma}} be a collection of constraints so that the moduli space ℳ​(L,Y¯)\mathcal{M}(L,\underline{Y}) of maps of type Γ\mathbb{\Gamma} with constraints Y¯\underline{Y} is expected dimension zero. Then the count m​(Γ)m(\mathbb{\Gamma}) of maps of type Γ\mathbb{\Gamma} is independent of the choice of domain-dependent almost complex structure JΓJ_{\Gamma} and depends only on the isotopy class of the constraints Y¯\underline{Y}.

Proof.

The proof of the statement of the Lemma is a standard cobordism argument: Given two such data (Jb,P¯b)(J_{b},\underline{P}_{b}) for b∈{0,1}b\in\{0,1\} let (Jt,P¯t),t∈[0,1](J_{t},\underline{P}_{t}),t\in[0,1] be a generic homotopy between them. Let ℳ~Γ​(L,Y)\tilde{\mathcal{M}}_{\mathbb{\Gamma}}(L,Y) be the parametrized moduli space for the family. By assumption, degeneration of the domain is impossible in the parametrized moduli space. The parameterized moduli space is therefore a compact oriented cobordism between the moduli spaces for (Jb,P¯b)(J_{b},\underline{P}_{b}) for b∈{0,1}b\in\{0,1\}, and the signed count of the moduli spaces at the ends is equal. The argument for variation of constraints Y¯\underline{Y} is similar. ∎

Remark 3.31.

All of the combinatorial types described in Definition 1.5 are easily seen to be prime with the following exception: Let 𝕃v⊂(ℂ×)2{\mathbb{L}}_{v}\subset(\mathbb{C}^{\times})^{2} be the diagonal, and uvu_{v} a punctured holomorphic disk with boundary in 𝕃v{\mathbb{L}}_{v}. Thus the tropical graph Λ\Lambda of the Lagrangian LL has a single edge ee with direction 𝒯​(e)=(1,1)\mathcal{T}(e)=(1,1), and the open edge ϵ\epsilon of the tropical graph Γ\Gamma of uvu_{v} has direction 𝒯​(ϵ)=(1/2,1/2)\mathcal{T}(\epsilon)=(1/2,1/2). Suppose the point constraint p∈𝕃vp\in{\mathbb{L}}_{v} approaches the real part Δℝ\Delta_{\mathbb{R}} of the diagonal (thinking of the Lagrangian as the anti-holomorphic diagonal). In the limit, the boundary of uvu_{v} lies on Δℝ\Delta_{\mathbb{R}}, and the Gromov limit of uvu_{v} consists of this map an a bubble containing both the open and closed ends. The real diagonal Δℝ≅ℝ+⊔ℝ−\Delta_{\mathbb{R}}\cong\mathbb{R}_{+}\sqcup\mathbb{R}_{-} separates L≅ℝ×S1≅ℂ×L\cong\mathbb{R}\times S^{1}\cong\mathbb{C}^{\times} into two parts, each isomorphic to ℝ×(−1,1)\mathbb{R}\times(-1,1).

  1. (a)

    If the point constraint pp lies in the first part, there is a holomorphic pant uvu_{v} with a closed end in the direction (1,0)(1,0).

  2. (b)

    If the point constraint pp lies in the second part, there is a holomorphic pant uvu_{v} with a closed end with direction (0,1)(0,1).

The count therefore depends on which half of the Lagrangian 𝕃P​(v){\mathbb{L}}_{P(v)} the point constraint is chosen to lie in, or equivalently, on the choice of almost complex structure, since there exists a symplectomorphism which maps a point in one half of 𝕃P​(v){\mathbb{L}}_{P(v)} to the other. We choose the count to be −1/2-1/2 for each of these possibilities, so that the count is “maximally symmetric”.

Remark 3.32.

Given a choice of relative spin structure on LL and orientations on the constraints Y¯\underline{Y}, the moduli spaces ℳΓ​(X,L,Y)\mathcal{M}_{\mathbb{\Gamma}}(X,L,Y) are oriented non-canonically, by choosing relative spin structures on the extensions described in Definition 3.14 and applying the constructions of Fukaya-Oh-Ohta-Ono [10], see also Wehrheim-Woodward [38].

3.4. Distribution of constraints

In this section we show that the moduli space of rigid broken maps to a broken symplectic four-manifold is, up to cobordism, a product of moduli spaces associated to the vertices, after adding an appropriate set of constraints in the sense of (16).

Theorem 3.33.

Let 𝕃⊂𝕏{\mathbb{L}}\subset\mathbb{X} be a broken Lagrangian as above. Let Γ\mathbb{\Gamma} be a rigid type of broken map bounding 𝕃{\mathbb{L}} so that the homotopy types of graphs Γ​(v)⊂Γ\mathbb{\Gamma}(v)\subset\mathbb{\Gamma} associated to the vertices vv are all prime. Then there exists a collection of constraints

𝕐¯=(𝕐¯v),v∈Vert⁡(Γ),𝕐¯v=(𝕐e⊂𝕏P​(e))e∋v\underline{\mathbb{Y}}=(\underline{\mathbb{Y}}_{v}),\quad v\in\operatorname{Vert}(\mathbb{\Gamma}),\quad\underline{\mathbb{Y}}_{v}=(\mathbb{Y}_{e}\subset\mathbb{X}_{P(e)})_{e\ni v}

where each 𝕐e\mathbb{Y}_{e}, if non-trivial, is a collection of point constraints so that the moduli space ℳΓ​(𝕃)\mathcal{M}_{\Gamma}({\mathbb{L}}) of unframed broken maps with underlying graph Γ\Gamma admits an oriented bijection with the product of partially framed moduli spaces with constraints 𝕐¯v\underline{\mathbb{Y}}_{v}:

ℳΓ​(𝕃)≅∏v∈Vert⁡(Γ)ℳΓ​(v)​(𝕃P​(v),𝕐¯v).\mathcal{M}_{\Gamma}({\mathbb{L}})\cong\prod_{v\in\operatorname{Vert}(\Gamma)}\mathcal{M}_{\mathbb{\Gamma}(v)}({\mathbb{L}}_{P(v)},\underline{\mathbb{Y}}_{v}).
Proof.

The proof of the statement of the Theorem is an induction on the number of vertices in the graph. For each vertex v∈Vert⁡(Γ)v\in\operatorname{Vert}(\mathbb{\Gamma}), the moduli space ℳΓ​(v)​(𝕃P​(v))\mathcal{M}_{\mathbb{\Gamma}(v)}({\mathbb{L}}_{P(v)}) is non-negative dimension, by regularity.

Case 1: There exists a univalent closed vertex v∈Vert∙⁡(Γ)v\in\operatorname{Vert}_{\bullet}(\mathbb{\Gamma}). The vertex vv is connected to a unique distinct vertex v′∈Vert∙⁡(Γ)v^{\prime}\in\operatorname{Vert}_{\bullet}(\mathbb{\Gamma}) by some edge ee. Cutting at the edge ee divides Γ\mathbb{\Gamma} into two graphs Γ​(v)\mathbb{\Gamma}(v) and Γ′\mathbb{\Gamma}^{\prime}, and the moduli space ℳΓ′​(𝕃P​(v))\mathcal{M}_{\mathbb{\Gamma}^{\prime}}({\mathbb{L}}_{P(v)}) must be rigid. Since the matching condition cuts down the dimension by at most two, ℳΓ​(v)​(𝕃P​(v))\mathcal{M}_{\mathbb{\Gamma}(v)}({\mathbb{L}}_{P(v)}) must be either dimension zero or two.

Case 1a: The dimension of ℳΓ​(v)​(𝕃P​(v))\mathcal{M}_{\mathbb{\Gamma}(v)}({\mathbb{L}}_{P(v)}) is two. By the inductive hypothesis, there exist constraints 𝕐¯′\underline{\mathbb{Y}}^{\prime} so that ℳΓ′​(𝕃′)\mathcal{M}_{\mathbb{\Gamma}^{\prime}}({\mathbb{L}}^{\prime}) is a product of ℳΓ′​(v′)​(𝕃P​(v′),𝕐¯v′)\mathcal{M}_{\mathbb{\Gamma}^{\prime}(v^{\prime})}({\mathbb{L}}_{P(v^{\prime})},\underline{\mathbb{Y}}_{v^{\prime}}) over its vertices v′.v^{\prime}. For each element of ℳΓ′​(𝕃′)\mathcal{M}_{\mathbb{\Gamma}^{\prime}}({\mathbb{L}}^{\prime}), choose a trivialization of the tangent space Twe+​Sv+T_{w^{+}_{e}}S_{v_{+}}, that is, a framing at the node. Let 𝕐~v\tilde{\mathbb{Y}}_{v} be the collection of points that is the image of ℳΓ′​(𝕃′,𝕐¯′)\mathcal{M}_{\mathbb{\Gamma}^{\prime}}({\mathbb{L}}^{\prime},\underline{\mathbb{Y}}^{\prime}) under the evaluation map at we+w^{+}_{e}. The forgetful map

ℳΓ​(𝕃)→ℳΓ​(v)​(𝕃P​(v),𝕐~v),(uv)↦uv\mathcal{M}_{\mathbb{\Gamma}}({\mathbb{L}})\to\mathcal{M}_{\mathbb{\Gamma}(v)}({\mathbb{L}}_{P(v)},\tilde{\mathbb{Y}}_{v}),\quad(u_{v})\mapsto u_{v}

is a bijection. Indeed, the inverse to the bijection is obtained by gluing on the corresponding element of ℳΓ′​(𝕃′,𝕐¯′)\mathcal{M}_{\mathbb{\Gamma}^{\prime}}({\mathbb{L}}^{\prime},\underline{\mathbb{Y}}^{\prime}). The claim follows from the inductive hypothesis.

Case 1b: The dimension of ℳΓ​(v)​(𝕃P​(v))\mathcal{M}_{\mathbb{\Gamma}(v)}({\mathbb{L}}_{P(v)}) is zero. Let

𝕐~e⊂𝕏P​(e)\tilde{\mathbb{Y}}_{e}\subset\mathbb{X}_{P(e)}

denote the image of the moduli space ℳΓ​(v)\mathcal{M}_{\mathbb{\Gamma}(v)} under the evaluation map eve\operatorname{ev}_{e}, and 𝕐¯′\underline{\mathbb{Y}}^{\prime} the point constraint at the edge ee, and the given constraints at the other edges. Then ℳΓ′​(𝕃′,𝕐¯′)\mathcal{M}_{\mathbb{\Gamma}^{\prime}}({\mathbb{L}}^{\prime},\underline{\mathbb{Y}}^{\prime}) is rigid. The claim follows again by the inductive hypothesis.

Case 2: There is a univalent open vertex v∈Vert∘⁡(Γ)v\in\operatorname{Vert}_{\circ}(\Gamma). Let Γ′\mathbb{\Gamma}^{\prime} denote the graph obtained from Γ\Gamma by removing vv. Since the matching condition cuts down the dimension by one, the sum of the dimensions ℳΓ​(v)​(𝕃P​(v))\mathcal{M}_{\mathbb{\Gamma}(v)}({\mathbb{L}}_{P(v)}) and ℳΓ′​(𝕃′)\mathcal{M}_{\mathbb{\Gamma}^{\prime}}({\mathbb{L}}^{\prime}) is equal to one. If ℳΓ​(v)​(𝕃P​(v))\mathcal{M}_{\mathbb{\Gamma}(v)}({\mathbb{L}}_{P(v)}) is dimension one, then ℳΓ′​(𝕃′)\mathcal{M}_{\mathbb{\Gamma}^{\prime}}({\mathbb{L}}^{\prime}) is rigid and is a product of moduli spaces associated to its vertices, up to cobordism, by the inductive hypothesis. Otherwise, ℳΓ​(v)​(𝕃P​(v))\mathcal{M}_{\mathbb{\Gamma}(v)}({\mathbb{L}}_{P(v)}) is rigid and ℳΓ′​(𝕃′,𝕐¯′)\mathcal{M}_{\mathbb{\Gamma}^{\prime}}({\mathbb{L}}^{\prime},\underline{\mathbb{Y}}^{\prime}) is rigid for some constraint 𝕐¯′\underline{\mathbb{Y}}^{\prime} given at ee by the output of ℳΓ​(v)​(𝕃P​(v))\mathcal{M}_{\mathbb{\Gamma}(v)}({\mathbb{L}}_{P(v)}). The discussion proceeds as in the closed case. ∎

Example 3.34.

We give an example of distribution of constraints for a Maslov-index-two disk with point constraint contributing to the potential for the degree seven del Pezzo, with a fixed point constraint p∈𝕃p\in{\mathbb{L}}. The broken disk u=(uv)u=(u_{v}) has three components corresponding to vertices v∈{v0,v1,v2}=Vert⁡(Γ)v\in\{v_{0},v_{1},v_{2}\}=\operatorname{Vert}(\Gamma) as shown in the cartoon diagram in Figure 25, with point constraint in the piece in between the boundary and the trivalent vertex of the Lagrangian. The component uv0u_{v_{0}} whose corresponding to the univalent vertex v0v_{0} mapping to zero is rigid, and it’s evaluation at the end corresponding to the only edge is then considered a constraint for the component uv1u_{v_{1}} whose vertex v1v_{1} is a bivalent vertex with adjacent open and closed edges. This component is rigid taking into account the point constraint at pp, so that the number of point constraints on this component is two. Its evaluation at the end is considered as a point constraint for the final piece uv2u_{v_{2}} which is a punctured holomorphic sphere meeting the toric boundary, which is then rigid.

3.5. The definition of the disk counts

We now give the weighted counts of disks used in, for example, the definition of the disk potential. Only in certain cases will these counts be invariant of the choice of almost complex structure and perturbations.

Definition 3.35.

Let u=(uv)v∈Vert⁡(Γ)u=(u_{v})_{v\in\operatorname{Vert}(\Gamma)} be a broken map with tropical graph Γ\Gamma. Denote the number of interior leaves

d∙​(Γ)∈ℤ≥0d_{\bullet}(\Gamma)\in\mathbb{Z}_{\geq 0}

of the graph Γ\Gamma. Let

γ​(u)∈{±1}\gamma(u)\in\{\pm 1\}

denote the orientation sign defined using the relative spin structure. The unbroken holomorphic disk count with constraints Y¯\underline{Y} is given as the sum over rigid maps uu

(20) m​(L,Y¯)=∑Γ∑u∈ℳΓ​(L,Y¯)(d∙​(Γ)!)−1​γ​(u)∈ℚm(L,\underline{Y})=\sum_{\mathbb{\Gamma}}\sum_{u\in\mathcal{M}_{\mathbb{\Gamma}}(L,\underline{Y})}(d_{\bullet}(\Gamma)!)^{-1}\gamma(u)\in\mathbb{Q}

Given a polyhedral decomposition 𝒫\mathcal{P}, the definition of the broken count m​(𝕃,𝕐¯)m({\mathbb{L}},\underline{\mathbb{Y}}) is similar, and given by a sum over elements of ℳΓ​(𝕃,𝕐¯)\mathcal{M}_{\mathbb{\Gamma}}({\mathbb{L}},\underline{\mathbb{Y}}).

We emphasize that the numbers m​(v)m(v) are independent of the choice of almost complex structure only in the case that Γ​(v)\mathbb{\Gamma}(v) is prime, as in Lemma 3.30. Theorem 3.33 immediately implies:

Corollary 3.36.

Let XX be an almost toric surface and L⊂XL\subset X a monotone almost toric moment fiber. The potential WLW_{L} is given by a sum over graphs Γ\mathbb{\Gamma} resp. Γ0\mathbb{\Gamma}_{0} with resp. without interior markings

WL\displaystyle W_{L} =\displaystyle= ∑Γ1d∙​(Γ)!​∏v∈Vert⁡(Γ)d∙​(Γ​(v))!​m​(v)\displaystyle\sum_{\mathbb{\Gamma}}\frac{1}{d_{\bullet}(\mathbb{\Gamma})!}\prod_{v\in\operatorname{Vert}({\mathbb{\Gamma}})}d_{\bullet}(\mathbb{\Gamma}(v))!m(v)
=\displaystyle= ∑Γ01#​Aut⁡(Γ0)​∏v∈Vert⁡(Γ0)m​(v)\displaystyle\sum_{\mathbb{\Gamma}_{0}}\frac{1}{\#\operatorname{Aut}(\mathbb{\Gamma}_{0})}\prod_{v\in\operatorname{Vert}({\mathbb{\Gamma}}_{0})}m(v)

where the first resp. second sum is a sum over isomorphism classes of types of broken disks with resp. without semi-infinite edges of interior type.

Proof.

The first equality in the statement of the Corollary is an immediate consequence of the cobordism in Theorem 3.33. The second equality is obtained by noting that for each type Γ\mathbb{\Gamma}, the numbers m​(v)m(v) are independent of the choice of ordering of interior edges associated to the Donaldson hypersurface, and the map Γ↦Γ0\mathbb{\Gamma}\mapsto\mathbb{\Gamma}_{0} which forgets the interior edges has d∙​(Γ)/Aut⁡(Γ0)d_{\bullet}(\mathbb{\Gamma})/\operatorname{Aut}(\mathbb{\Gamma}_{0}) points in its fiber. ∎

4. Holomorphic curves in the elementary pieces

By an elementary piece, we mean a cylindrical-end almost toric four-manifold equipped with a connected cylindrical-end tropical Lagrangian containing at most one focus-focus singularity or vertex of the tropical graph of a tropical Lagrangian. By the degeneration theory in the previous chapter, to count holomorphic disks bounding tropical Lagrangians it suffices to count the disks in these elementary pieces. The general strategy is to apply some degeneration technique to the model problems considered in Sections 4.1, 4.2 below. Knowledge of all but one of the open Gromov-Witten invariants involved gives the value the last invariant from the computation of the model problem. The multiplicities (a), (b), (c), (d) of Definition 1.5 were described in Venugopalan-Woodward [34] in the current foundational scheme, although these formulas already appeared elsewhere in the literature.

4.1. Disks in the product of projective lines

We make two explicit computations of numbers of holomorphic disks in the product of projective lines.

Example 4.1.

In the first example, we count disks with boundary in the anti-diagonal two-sphere in the product of projective lines X=ℙ1×ℙ1X=\mathbb{P}^{1}\times\mathbb{P}^{1} with equal symplectic forms on the factors. That is, let

L={(z,z¯)}≅ℙ1⊂X.L=\{(z,\overline{z})\}\cong\mathbb{P}^{1}\subset X.

The Lagrangian LL is the fixed point set XτX^{\tau} of the anti-symplectic involution

τ:X→X,(z1,z2)↦(z¯2,z¯1).\tau:X\to X,\quad(z_{1},z_{2})\mapsto(\overline{z}_{2},\overline{z}_{1}).
Lemma 4.2.

Let L⊂XL\subset X be the antidiagonal as above. The number m​(L,Y¯)m(L,\underline{Y}) of holomorphic disks u:S→Xu:S\to X of Maslov index 44 with boundary in LL and for some given generic p1,p2,p∈ℙ1p_{1},p_{2},p\in\mathbb{P}^{1},

u​(z∙)=(p1,p2),u​(z∘)=(p,p¯)u(z_{\bullet})=(p_{1},p_{2}),\quad u(z_{\circ})=(p,\overline{p})

for an interior point z∙∈Sz_{\bullet}\in S and a boundary point z∘z_{\circ}, is equal to −1-1.

A tropical picture of the desired curve is shown in Figure 26.

Proof.

We apply the operation of “removing a diagonal seam” discussed in Wehrheim-Woodward [38, Section 5.4] to reduce the count to one of holomorphic spheres. Holomorphic disks in XX with boundary in LL correspond to holomorphic maps from ℙ1\mathbb{P}^{1} to ℙ1\mathbb{P}^{1} in the following sense: Given a holomorphic disk u:C→Xu:C\to X with components (u−,u+)(u_{-},u_{+}) one obtains a holomorphic sphere

C′=C¯∪∂CCC^{\prime}=\overline{C}\cup_{\partial C}C

by gluing together the maps u−​(z¯),u+​(z),z∈Cu_{-}(\overline{z}),u_{+}(z),z\in C along the boundary. This correspondence is an orientation-preserving diffeomorphism between the moduli space of disks and spheres by [38, Proposition 5.4.6]. The constraints on the disk translate to constraints on the sphere

v​(z¯∙)=p1,v​(z∙)=p2,v​(z∘)=pv(\overline{z}_{\bullet})=p_{1},\quad v(z_{\bullet})=p_{2},\quad v(z_{\circ})=p

Complex conjugation on ℙ1\mathbb{P}^{1} is orientation reversing, and so maps the dual class [p1][p_{1}] to p1p_{1} to minus the dual class −[p1¯]-[\overline{p_{1}}] of p1¯\overline{p_{1}} in H2​(ℙ1)H^{2}(\mathbb{P}^{1}).

We claim that the count of holomorphic spheres vv with these three point constraints is equal to −1-1. Indeed, each such map corresponds to an automorphism φ:ℙ1→ℙ1\varphi:\mathbb{P}^{1}\to\mathbb{P}^{1} with three given point constraints, and any such automorphism is uniquely specified by three such point constraints. Since one of the constraints has the opposite of the standard orientation, the orientation sign is negative. ∎

Example 4.3.

Consider the problem of counting affine linear maps from the affine line to itself.

Lemma 4.4.

The number m​(L,Y¯)m(L,\underline{Y}) of holomorphic maps from S=ℂS=\mathbb{C} to 𝕏=ℂ\mathbb{X}=\mathbb{C} passing through two fixed generic points in 𝕏\mathbb{X} is equal to one. Similarly, the number of holomorphic maps from the half-space S=ℍS=\mathbb{H} to 𝕏=ℂ\mathbb{X}=\mathbb{C} with boundary in ℝ\mathbb{R} passing through points p1∈𝕃p_{1}\in{\mathbb{L}} and asymptotic to a fixed Reeb chord at infinity is equal to −1-1.

Proof.

The first statement of the Lemma is equivalent to the existence of a unique line between any two generic points. A holomorphic map u:ℂ→𝕏u:\mathbb{C}\to\mathbb{X} of the form

u​(z)=a​z+b,a,b∈ℂ,a≠0u(z)=az+b,a,b\in\mathbb{C},a\neq 0

has finite Hofer energy. Given p1≠p2∈𝕏p_{1}\neq p_{2}\in\mathbb{X} and z1≠z2∈ℂz_{1}\neq z_{2}\in\mathbb{C}, there are unique values of a,ba,b so that u​(zk)=pk,k=1,2u(z_{k})=p_{k},k=1,2. The relative Gromov-Witten invariant in this case is one for the standard complex structure, and the answer is independent of the choice of almost complex structure by Lemma 3.30.

In the real case, maps to ℂ×ℂ\mathbb{C}\times\mathbb{C} with boundary in the diagonal glue to holomorphic degree one maps ℂ→ℂ\mathbb{C}\to\mathbb{C} of the form u~​(z)=a​z+b\tilde{u}(z)=az+b, by taking the first resp. second component to be the restriction of u~\tilde{u} to Im⁡(z)≥0\operatorname{Im}(z)\geq 0 resp. its restriction to the complement. ∎

Note, for later use, that the first resp. second component of uu has a zero away from the the boundary ℝ⊂ℂ\mathbb{R}\subset\mathbb{C} if Im⁡(−b/a)\operatorname{Im}(-b/a) is positive resp. negative.

Remark 4.5.

We choose the relative spin structure for the moduli space of relative broken maps so that the count has a positive sign.

4.2. Disks in the blow-up of the product of projective lines

We make three explicit computations of numbers of holomorphic disks in the blow-up of the product of projective lines.

Example 4.6.

We count disks in a blow-up. Let X=Bl⁡(ℙ1×ℙ1)X=\operatorname{Bl}(\mathbb{P}^{1}\times\mathbb{P}^{1}) be the blow-up of the product of projective lines and L≅S2L\cong S^{2} the inverse image of the anti-diagonal in Lemma 4.2 under the blow-down map. Let E≅ℙ1⊂XE\cong\mathbb{P}^{1}\subset X denote the exceptional locus.

Lemma 4.7.

Let L⊂XL\subset X be as above. The number m​(L,Y¯)m(L,\underline{Y}) of holomorphic disks in XX with boundary in LL , intersecting EE with multiplicity one and passing through a generic point in XX and in LL is equal to minus one.

Proof.

We reduce to the case of disks in the product of projective lines as follows. Let Γ\mathbb{\Gamma} be the type of holomorphic disk in X=Bl⁡(ℙ1×ℙ1)X=\operatorname{Bl}(\mathbb{P}^{1}\times\mathbb{P}^{1}) with boundary in LL meeting the exceptional curve EE once transversally. Under the blow-down map π:X→ℙ1×ℙ1\pi:X\to\mathbb{P}^{1}\times\mathbb{P}^{1}, these are identified with disks u¯\overline{u} in ℙ1×ℙ1\mathbb{P}^{1}\times\mathbb{P}^{1} meeting the anti-diagonal and passing through the blow-up point π​(E)\pi(E), by removal of singularities for pseudoholomorphic disks; see [33, Theorem 6.1]. By Lemma 4.2 these have count −1-1. ∎

Example 4.8.

Consider disks in the blow-up of the product of projective lines with boundary in the tropical Lagrangian whose graph has a single trivalent vertex. Let XX be the blow-up of ℙ1×ℙ1\mathbb{P}^{1}\times\mathbb{P}^{1} at a point and LL the embedded Lagrangian sphere in XX considered in Examples 2.15, 2.19.

Lemma 4.9.

The count of Maslov-index-two disks with boundary in the tropical Lagrangian L⊂X=Bl⁡(ℙ1×ℙ1)L\subset X=\operatorname{Bl}(\mathbb{P}^{1}\times\mathbb{P}^{1}) whose graph Γ\Gamma has a single trivalent vertex v∈Vert⁡(Γ)v\in\operatorname{Vert}(\Gamma) and three edges e1,e2,e3∈Edge⁡(Γ)e_{1},e_{2},e_{3}\in\operatorname{Edge}(\Gamma) in the blow-up XX of ℙ1×ℙ1\mathbb{P}^{1}\times\mathbb{P}^{1} at a torus-fixed-point is equal to −1-1.

Proof.

The previous Lemma 4.7 covered the case of the inverse image of the diagonal. Example 2.19 described an the isotopy of the given tropical Lagrangian to the inverse image of the diagonal. The statement of the Lemma now follows by invariance of the disk counts under Hamiltonian isotopy. Compare with Hicks [13, Figure 2]. ∎

Lemma 4.10.

The count m​(L,Y¯)m(L,\underline{Y}) of Maslov-index-four disks bounding the Lagrangian LL in Examples 2.15, 2.19 and passing through three generic points p1,p2,p3∈Lp_{1},p_{2},p_{3}\in L is equal to 11.

A tropical picture of the disks in question is shown on the right in Figure 27 below. The left shows that tropical picture for the lift of the diagonal. Note that the intersection number of the disks with the anticanonical divisor is three, for the disks on the right; this reflects the fact that the Maslov index of a broken disk u=(uv)u=(u_{v}) with boundary in LL is not in general equal to the number #​Γ∩∂B∨\#\Gamma\cap\partial B^{\vee} of intersections of the tropical graph Γ\Gamma with the boundary ∂B∨\partial B^{\vee}, but rather has a correction from trivalent vertices v∈Vert⁡(Γ),|v|=3v\in\operatorname{Vert}(\Gamma),|v|=3 explained further in Remark 4.21.

Proof.

An isotopy of the given tropical Lagrangian to the inverse image of the diagonal was described in Example 2.19. We first consider the possibilities for the intersection number with the exceptional divisor. Let Y¯=(p1,p2,p3)\underline{Y}=(p_{1},p_{2},p_{3}) with p1,p2,p3∈Lp_{1},p_{2},p_{3}\in L generic. The moduli space ℳ​(L,Y¯)\mathcal{M}(L,\underline{Y}) consists of rigi Maslov-index-four disks uu with boundary in LL with constrains Y¯\underline{Y}, and breaks up into a disjoint union of subsets ℳ​(L,Y¯)d\mathcal{M}(L,\underline{Y})_{d} consisting of maps uu whose intersection multiplicity [u].[E][u].[E] with the exceptional divisor E⊂XE\subset X is equal to d∈ℤ≥0d\in\mathbb{Z}_{\geq 0}. The manifold XX is monotone and we may assume that the monotonicity constant is one, so that the symplectic class [ω][\omega] is represented by the boundary divisor Y=Φ−1​(∂Φ​(X))Y=\Phi^{-1}(\partial\Phi(X)). The immersed submanifold YY has the exceptional curve EE as an irreducible component. Since the area A​(u)A(u) of uu is two, the intersection number dd must equal 0,10,1 or 22. Since non-transverse intersections with EE are codimension two, we may assume after choosing a generic domain-dependent almost complex structure that the dd intersection points are disjoint; see [33, Lemma 5.27]. Composition with the projection π:Bl⁡(ℙ1×ℙ1)→ℙ1×ℙ1\pi:\operatorname{Bl}(\mathbb{P}^{1}\times\mathbb{P}^{1})\to\mathbb{P}^{1}\times\mathbb{P}^{1} gives a holomorphic disk u′=π∘uu^{\prime}=\pi\circ u in ℙ1×ℙ1\mathbb{P}^{1}\times\mathbb{P}^{1} passing through the blow-up point π​(E)\pi(E) dd times, with Maslov index 44, 66, or 88.

The projected Lagrangian is the fixed point set of an anti-symplectic involution and the usual correspondence between disks and spheres in this case may be used to count the disks. Since the number of such disk uu is independent of the choice of almost complex structure, we may assume that the almost complex structure is the standard one. In particular, the almost complex structure is anti-invariant under the standard anti-symplectic involution (z1,z2)↦(z¯2,z¯1)(z_{1},z_{2})\mapsto(\overline{z}_{2},\overline{z}_{1}). By the doubling construction, any such disk uu corresponds to a holomorphic sphere u~\tilde{u} in ℙ1×ℙ1\mathbb{P}^{1}\times\mathbb{P}^{1} passing through the original points p1,p2,p3p_{1},p_{2},p_{3}. In addition, the spheres u~\tilde{u} obtained by doubling pass through the blow-up point π​(E)\pi(E) 2​d2d times. The Chern number of such a sphere u~\tilde{u} is 2,32,3 or 44, and the bidegree of u~\tilde{u} is (e,e)(e,e) with either e=1e=1 or e=2e=2. However, in the e=2e=2 case, the disk uu meets the anticanonical divisor in XX only at two points in EE. Its projection u′u^{\prime} meets the anti-canonical divisor in π​(Y)⊂ℙ1×ℙ1\pi(Y)\subset\mathbb{P}^{1}\times\mathbb{P}^{1} only at (0,0)(0,0) and (∞,∞)(\infty,\infty). Such a curve u′u^{\prime} is necessarily a double cover of a curve of degree (1,1)(1,1), and the corresponding disk uu in XX is therefore a double cover of a disk of Maslov-index-two. Such curves uu are disjoint from a choice of three generic points on the Lagrangian; the same holds after perturbation. It follows that the corresponding count vanishes. ∎

4.3. Disks ending on cylinders

We now prove the various cases of Theorem 1.4 in Definition 1.5. The general strategy is to deduce these relative counts from the absolute counts in the previous two sections. The first count is that of holomorphic disks whose tropical graphs meet the graph of the Lagrangian perpendicularly:

Lemma 4.11.

Let 𝕏P​(v)\mathbb{X}_{P(v)} be a cylindrical end manifold obtained from some compact toric orbifold 𝕏¯P​(v)\overline{\mathbb{X}}_{P(v)} be removing the inverse image of the boundary of the moment polytope. Let 𝕃P​(v)⊂𝕏P​(v){\mathbb{L}}_{P(v)}\subset\mathbb{X}_{P(v)} be a Lagrangian realization of a tropical graph Λ\Lambda with a single edge Edge⁡(Λ)={ϵ}\operatorname{Edge}(\Lambda)=\{\epsilon\} in P​(v)P(v). The count of punctured disks uv:Cv→𝕏P​(v)u_{v}:C_{v}\to\mathbb{X}_{P(v)} bounding 𝕃P​(v){\mathbb{L}}_{P(v)} with a single cylindrical end and no strip-like ends with a single point constraint Ye=(pe∈𝕃P​(v)),Edge∘⁡(Γ)={e}Y_{e}=(p_{e}\in{\mathbb{L}}_{P(v)}),\operatorname{Edge}_{\circ}(\Gamma)=\{e\} is equal to m​(v)=−1m(v)=-1, as in Definition 1.5 (e).

Proof.

We first consider the case that the target is a quotient of the product of projective lines 𝕏¯P​(v)≅(ℙ1×ℙ1)/ℤ2\overline{\mathbb{X}}_{P(v)}\cong(\mathbb{P}^{1}\times\mathbb{P}^{1})/\mathbb{Z}_{2} and consider the arbitrary toric case afterwards. We decompose the moduli space of Maslov index four disks with boundary in the anti-diagonal with a point constraint as follows. Let

L={(z,z¯)}≅ℙ1⊂X:=(ℙ1)2L=\{(z,\overline{z})\}\cong\mathbb{P}^{1}\subset X:=(\mathbb{P}^{1})^{2}

denote the Lagrangian anti-diagonal. Let

ptX=(0,∞)∈ℙ1×ℙ1.\operatorname{pt}_{X}=(0,\infty)\in\mathbb{P}^{1}\times\mathbb{P}^{1}.

The point ptX\operatorname{pt}_{X} is a torus-fixed-point not on the Lagrangian LL. The count of holomorphic Maslov-index-four disks with boundary in LL passing through ptX\operatorname{pt}_{X} equal to −1-1 by Lemma 4.2. Consider a decomposition 𝒫={P}\mathcal{P}=\{P\} of the moment polytope of 𝕏¯P​(v)\overline{\mathbb{X}}_{P(v)}, consider a broken map u=(u0,…,uk)u=(u_{0},\ldots,u_{k}) with pieces mapping to 𝕏P0,…,𝕏Pk\mathbb{X}_{P_{0}},\ldots,\mathbb{X}_{P_{k}} of the following form: The piece 𝕏¯P0\overline{\mathbb{X}}_{P_{0}} contains the constraint YeY_{e} has polytope a non-regular hexagon, and is connected by a sequence of pieces whose polytopes are rectangles to a piece 𝕏¯Pk\overline{\mathbb{X}}_{P_{k}} containing the Lagrangian 𝕃Pk≅ℝ×S1{\mathbb{L}}_{P_{k}}\cong\mathbb{R}\times S^{1}. Each piece not containing the constraint or Lagrangian is a ℤ2\mathbb{Z}_{2}-quotient of ℙ1×ℙ1\mathbb{P}^{1}\times\mathbb{P}^{1}

𝕏¯Pi≅(ℙ1×ℙ1)/ℤ2,i=1,…,k−1.\overline{\mathbb{X}}_{P_{i}}\cong(\mathbb{P}^{1}\times\mathbb{P}^{1})/\mathbb{Z}_{2},\quad i=1,\ldots,k-1.

Let P1P_{1} denote the polytope containing the image of ptX\operatorname{pt}_{X}.

Broken maps in the degeneration are classified as follows. The only rigid broken maps u=(uv)u=(u_{v}) are those meeting only 𝕏P0\mathbb{X}_{P_{0}} and 𝕏Pk\mathbb{X}_{P_{k}} consisting of two levels given by disks uv0u_{v_{0}} in 𝕏P0\mathbb{X}_{P_{0}} and spheres uvku_{v_{k}} in 𝕏P1\mathbb{X}_{P_{1}}. Indeed, LL is the fixed point set of an anti-symplectic involution, and the decomposition into pieces 𝕏P\mathbb{X}_{P} is compatible with the involution. We may view uu as half of a broken map u~\tilde{u} of Chern number four, with tropical graph Γ~\tilde{\mathbb{\Gamma}} obtained by “doubling” Γ\mathbb{\Gamma}. All other univalent vertices must lie in the intersections of Γ~\tilde{\mathbb{\Gamma}} with the boundary of the moment polytope ∂Φ​(X)\partial\Phi(X). It follows that these vertices correspond to intersections of u~\tilde{u} with the anti-canonical divisor. Since the Chern number of u~\tilde{u} is 22, the only intersections with the boundary are (0,∞),(∞,0)∈ℙ1×ℙ1(0,\infty),(\infty,0)\in\mathbb{P}^{1}\times\mathbb{P}^{1}. Hence the only possible univalent vertex of Γ\mathbb{\Gamma} is at the image of (0,∞)(0,\infty). Thus there are no trivalent vertices v∈Vert⁡(Γ)v\in\operatorname{Vert}(\mathbb{\Gamma}) in the type Γ\mathbb{\Gamma} of any rigid map. There must be two univalent vertices v0,vk∈Vert⁡(Γ)v_{0},v_{k}\in\operatorname{Vert}(\mathbb{\Gamma}) mapping to P1∨P_{1}^{\vee} and the dual Pk∨P_{k}^{\vee}. The contribution m​(vk)m(v_{k}) of the sphere uvku_{v_{k}} in 𝕏P1\mathbb{X}_{P_{1}} is equal to one, since uv1u_{v_{1}} is transversally cut out and orientation signs for regular holomorphic spheres are always positive. It follows that the number of holomorphic disks in 𝕏P0\mathbb{X}_{P_{0}} with boundary in LL with a point constraint on the boundary and with a single cylindrical end is equal to

m​(v0)=−1.m(v_{0})=-1.

Now that we have proved the formula for the multiplicity for the case that the target is (ℙ1×ℙ1)/ℤ2(\mathbb{P}^{1}\times\mathbb{P}^{1})/\mathbb{Z}_{2}, the formula for arbitrary toric targets follows from Theorem 1.4 case Definition 1.5 (a) and the decomposition formula in Theorem 1.4, by taking a decomposition in which the piece 𝕏P0\mathbb{X}_{P_{0}} containing the intersection with the Lagrangian has polytope P0P_{0} such a rectangle. ∎

Proof of Theorem 1.4 case (e).

The case of primitive directions was proved in Lemma 4.11. To prove the claim for arbitrary directions, we consider the case of holomorphic disks uu in X=Bl⁡(ℙ1×ℙ1)X=\operatorname{Bl}(\mathbb{P}^{1}\times\mathbb{P}^{1}) with a tangency at order d∈ℤ≥0d\in\mathbb{Z}_{\geq 0} at a point z∈Cz\in C mapping to the exceptional divisor EE. These are naturally in bijection with disks u′u^{\prime} with tangency of order 11 as follows: Given u′u^{\prime}, a map uu is obtained composing with the dd-fold covering z↦zdz\mapsto z^{d}. The proof that this gives a bijection is similar to the arguments in the proof of Lemma 4.11. Namely, any such disk uu corresponds to a sphere u~\tilde{u} in ℙ1×ℙ1\mathbb{P}^{1}\times\mathbb{P}^{1} passing through (0,0)(0,0) and (∞,∞)(\infty,\infty), and vanishing to order dd at those points. Such a sphere u~\tilde{u} is locally, hence globally, a dd-fold ramified cover of its image. The relative spin structure for such a cover u~\tilde{u} differs from the trivial relative spin structure by the dd-th power of the corresponding map for the corresponding embedding uu. The relative spin structure on (∂u~)∗​T​L(\partial\tilde{u})^{*}TL is a dd-fold twist of the relative spin structure for (∂u)∗​T​L(\partial u)^{*}TL. By [11, Proposition 8.1.16] the orientation of the moduli space at u~\tilde{u} is (−1)d(-1)^{d} times the standard orientation of a point. ∎

Example 4.12.

We give an example where non-primitive directions occur in Theorem 1.4 case Definition 1.5 (e), that is, the case d>1d>1 occurs. Figure 29 shows an almost toric diagram for the del Pezzo of degree two, as in Vianna [36].

In our previous paper [34] we computed the potential of the monotone torus L⊂XL\subset X for this diagram and found that the potential WL:ℂ×,2→ℂW_{L}:\mathbb{C}^{\times,2}\to\mathbb{C} had critical values

Crit(WL)={−12,52.}\operatorname{Crit}(W_{L})=\{-12,52.\}

Consider the two Lagrangian spheres L1,L2L_{1},L_{2} whose possible tropical graphs Λ1,Λ2\Lambda_{1},\Lambda_{2} consist of a long vertical or horizontal segment ϵ1,ϵ2\epsilon_{1},\epsilon_{2}.

  • •

    The tropical graphs Γ1\Gamma_{1} corresponding to Maslov-index-two disks bounding L1L_{1} have initial direction (0,±1)(0,\pm 1) and interact with 22 out of the 44 possible focus-focus singularities, for a total of

    WL1=2​(−1)​6=−12.W_{L_{1}}=2(-1)6=-12.
  • •

    For the tropical Lagrangians L2L_{2} with vertical graph Λ2\Lambda_{2} the possible initial directions of the tropical graphs Γ2′,Γ2′′\Gamma_{2}^{\prime},\Gamma_{2}^{\prime\prime} corresponding to disks bounding L2L_{2} are (±1,0)(\pm 1,0) and (±2,0)(\pm 2,0). The tropical graphs of holomorphic disks of the latter type Γ2′′\Gamma_{2}^{\prime\prime} have a single vertex with multiplicity 22, for a total contribution of 2​(2)=42(2)=4. The first type of tropical graphs Γ2′\Gamma_{2}^{\prime} have two vertices v1,v2v_{1},v_{2} of multiplicity m​(v1)=m​(v2)=1m(v_{1})=m(v_{2})=1, and interact with one of the eight focus-focus singularities along the vertical axis, for a total contribution of 2​(−8)=−162(-8)=-16. Thus the potential of these tropical Lagrangians is

    WL2=4−16=−12.W_{L_{2}}=4-16=-12.

Thus these Lagrangians L1,L2L_{1},L_{2} have the same potential, that is, lie in the same Fukaya eigencategory Fuk−12⁡(X)\operatorname{Fuk}_{-12}(X) of XX. The same fact can be seen from the simpler observation that the Lagrangians L1,L2L_{1},L_{2} have a single intersection point (the unique focus-focus point in the intersection of their preimages). It follows that the Floer boundary operator on C​F​(L1,L2)CF(L_{1},L_{2}) has vanishing square, since the operator has odd degree and C​F​(L1,L2)CF(L_{1},L_{2}) has rank one. On the other hand, Oh’s results in [29] imply that the square of the Floer differential is the difference in potentials WL2−WL1W_{L_{2}}-W_{L_{1}}.

4.4. The holomorphic pant

Next we investigate holomorphic curves with one cylindrical and one strip-like end, that is, the holomorphic pant.

Theorem 4.13.

Suppose 𝕏P​(v)\mathbb{X}_{P(v)} is a component of 𝕏\mathbb{X} not containing a focus-focus singularity x∈Xfocx\in X^{\operatorname{foc}}, so that the compactification 𝕏¯P​(v)\overline{\mathbb{X}}_{P(v)} is a compact toric orbifold with polytope the convex hull of {(±1,0),(0,±1)}\{(\pm 1,0),(0,\pm 1)\}. Let 𝕃P​(v)⊂𝕏P​(v){\mathbb{L}}_{P(v)}\subset\mathbb{X}_{P(v)} be a Lagrangian realization of a tropical graph Λ\Lambda with a singe edge with direction (1,1)(1,1). For a particular almost complex structure, The count of punctured holomorphic disks in 𝕏P​(v)\mathbb{X}_{P(v)} bounding 𝕃P​(v){\mathbb{L}}_{P(v)} with a single strip-like end e∘∈Edge∘⁡(Γ)e_{\circ}\in\operatorname{Edge}_{\circ}(\mathbb{\Gamma}) asymptotic to a fixed Reeb chord γ∘\gamma_{\circ} with direction 𝒯​(e∘)\mathcal{T}(e_{\circ}) and single cylindrical-end e∙∈Edge∙⁡(Γ)e_{\bullet}\in\operatorname{Edge}_{\bullet}(\mathbb{\Gamma}) at the interior puncture asymptotic to a fixed Reeb orbit γ∙\gamma_{\bullet} at the puncture with direction 𝒯​(e∙)\mathcal{T}(e_{\bullet}) is equal to

m​(v)=(−1)|det(𝒯​(e∙)​𝒯​(e∘))|​|det(𝒯​(e∙)​𝒯​(e∘))|m(v)=(-1)^{|\det(\mathcal{T}(e_{\bullet})\mathcal{T}(e_{\circ}))|}|\det(\mathcal{T}(e_{\bullet})\mathcal{T}(e_{\circ}))|

as in Theorem 1.4 case Definition 1.5 (f).

We first show the following special case:

Lemma 4.14.

Let 𝕃P​(v)⊂𝕏P​(v):=(ℂ×)2{\mathbb{L}}_{P(v)}\subset\mathbb{X}_{P(v)}:=(\mathbb{C}^{\times})^{2} denote the antidiagonal Lagrangian. The count of punctured holomorphic disks in 𝕏P​(v)\mathbb{X}_{P(v)} bounding 𝕃P​(v){\mathbb{L}}_{P(v)} with strip-like end with direction (−1,−1)(-1,-1) and one cylindrical-end with direction either (1,0)(1,0) or (0,1)(0,1), and asymptotic to a fixed Reeb orbit

Ye1={γe1},{e1}=Edge∘⁡(Γ)Y_{e_{1}}=\{\gamma_{e_{1}}\},\{e_{1}\}=\operatorname{Edge}_{\circ}(\Gamma)

at the interior puncture and point

Ye2∈𝕃P​(v)Y_{e_{2}}\in{\mathbb{L}}_{P(v)}

is equal to

m​(v)=−1.m(v)=-1.
Proof.

The model problem of counting real lines was considered in Lemma 4.4. The count of such lines was shown to be equal to −1/2-1/2 for a fixed Reeb orbit at infinity and a point constraint on the boundary. From the count of lines we obtain a count of relative maps as follows. Consider the symplectic cut of X=ℂ2X=\mathbb{C}^{2} along a line perpendicular to the direction of the moment image Λ\Lambda of the Lagrangian and two lines parallel to Λ\Lambda as in Figure 30. The only rigid tropical graph Γ\Gamma is the one shown in Figure 30, with a single bivalent vertex v∈Vert⁡(Γ)v\in\operatorname{Vert}(\Gamma). Indeed, any additional vertex vertex v′∈Vert⁡(Γ)v^{\prime}\in\operatorname{Vert}(\Gamma) would have deformable position, and so the graph Γ\Gamma and broken map uu would not be rigid in the sense of Definition 3.12, a contradiction. Since the count of holomorphic spheres in 𝕏Q\mathbb{X}_{Q} is equal to one, there are two disks for each sphere. The orientation at one of the constraints is reversed, so the count of curves in 𝕏P\mathbb{X}_{P} must be m​(v)=−1/2m(v)=-1/2, as in Theorem 1.4 case Definition 1.5 (f). The answer is independent of the choice of almost complex structure by Lemma 3.30. ∎

Remark 4.15.

A holomorphic disk with boundary in the Lagrangian pair of pants as in Theorem 1.4 case Definition 1.5 (f) can be described explicitly as follows. Consider a holomorphic pair of pants u:C=ℙ1→ℙ1×ℙ1u:C=\mathbb{P}^{1}\to\mathbb{P}^{1}\times\mathbb{P}^{1}. Define

u​(z)=(z,1−z)u(z)=(z,1-z)

of so that uu has bidegree (1,1)(1,1). Let u−1​(Δ)u^{-1}(\Delta) denote the inverse image of the anti-diagonal

L={z2=z¯1}.L=\{z_{2}=\overline{z}_{1}\}.

We have

u−1​(L)={z=1−z¯}.u^{-1}(L)=\{z=1-\overline{z}\}.

This inverse image is a curve separating ℙ1−{0,1,∞}\mathbb{P}^{1}-\{0,1,\infty\} into two disks that are the images of maps u−,u+u_{-},u_{+}, as in Figure 30.

To compute the multiplicities of graphs with arbitrary directions at a trivalent vertex, we consider a finite cover operation as in Mikhalkin [25, Lemma 8.21]. Let X=(ℂ×)2X=(\mathbb{C}^{\times})^{2} with the reversed almost complex structure on the first factor, viewed as the interior of the toric orbifold whose moment polytope Φ​(X)\Phi(X) has vertices (±1,0),(0,±1)(\pm 1,0),(0,\pm 1). Let

L={(z,z¯),z∈ℂ×}⊂XL=\{(z,\overline{z}),z\in\mathbb{C}^{\times}\}\subset X

be the diagonal Lagrangian. Note that the diagonal is the fixed point set of the antisymplectic involution

σ:(z1,z2)↦(z¯2,z¯1).\sigma:(z_{1},z_{2})\mapsto(\overline{z}_{2},\overline{z}_{1}).

It has two cylindrical ends corresponding to the punctures at z=0z=0 and z=∞z=\infty. For integers n−,n+n_{-},n_{+} consider the holomorphic map

τn−,n+:X→X,(w−,w+)↦(w−n−​w+n+,w−n+​w+n−)\tau_{n_{-},n_{+}}:X\to X,\quad(w_{-},w_{+})\mapsto(w_{-}^{n_{-}}w_{+}^{n_{+}},w_{-}^{n_{+}}w_{+}^{n_{-}})

is σ\sigma-equivariant, and preserves the fixed point set LL. Define

(21) Tn−,n+=[n−n+n+n−].T_{n_{-},n_{+}}=\left[\begin{array}[]{cc}n_{-}&n_{+}\\ n_{+}&n_{-}\end{array}\right].

The following is immediate from the definition:

Lemma 4.16.

Let u:C→Xu:C\to X be a holomorphic disk with boundary in the diagonal LL intersecting with a single cylindrical end ee with direction 𝒯​(e)∈ℤ2.\mathcal{T}(e)\in\mathbb{Z}^{2}. Then u~\tilde{u} has a cylindrical end e~\tilde{e} with direction 𝒯​(e~)=Tn−,n+​𝒯​(e)\mathcal{T}(\tilde{e})=T_{n_{-},n_{+}}\mathcal{T}(e).

We claim that all curves with such an end arise this way:

Lemma 4.17.
  1. (a)

    Any punctured disk u~:S→X\tilde{u}:S\to X with a unique cylindrical end with direction (n−,n+)(n_{-},n_{+}) and with boundary in the diagonal is the composition of τn−,n+\tau_{n_{-},n_{+}} with some disk u:S→Xu:S\to X.

  2. (b)

    The number of such lifts uu for a given map u~\tilde{u} is equal to n++n−n_{+}+n_{-}.

  3. (c)

    The map uu is regular if and only if u~\tilde{u} is regular.

Proof.

The two claims follow from standard lifting properties of holomorphic covering maps, with uu being a lift of u~\tilde{u} under τn−,n+\tau_{n_{-},n_{+}}. Namely, the group ℤn−+n+\mathbb{Z}_{n_{-}+n_{+}} of n−+n+n_{-}+n_{+}-roots of unity acts naturally on XX by

γ​(z−,z+)=(γ​z−,γ¯​z+)\gamma(z_{-},z_{+})=(\gamma z_{-},\overline{\gamma}z_{+})

and preserves the fixed point set LL. Any two lifts differ by an action of some γ∈ℤn−+n+\gamma\in\mathbb{Z}_{n_{-}+n_{+}} acting diagonally, since the other elements of the group of deck transformations of τn−,n+\tau_{n_{-},n_{+}} do not preserve the boundary condition. For any pair of points in LL, there are (n−+n+)2(n_{-}+n_{+})^{2} lifts under τn−,n+\tau_{n_{-},n_{+}}, and two lifts have the same projection if they differ by an element γ∈ℤn−+n+\gamma\in\mathbb{Z}_{n_{-}+n_{+}}. Since there is a unique lift for each pair of given constraints, the number of curves u~\tilde{u} with the given contraints is n−+n+n_{-}+n_{+}. (This is the ”real version” of the proof of Mikhalkin’s [25, Lemma 8.21].)

The claim on regularity follows from an identification of the linearized operators DuD_{u} and Du~D_{\tilde{u}} (see [35, (6.39)]) give as follows: The identification u~∗​T​X→u∗​T​X\tilde{u}^{*}TX\to u^{*}TX given by the differential of τn−,n+\tau_{n_{-},n_{+}} induces an isomorphism of real boundary conditions and Cauchy-Riemann operators. ∎

Proof of Theorem 4.13.

We use Lemma 4.17 to reduce to the case of directions (1,0),(1,0)(1,0),(1,0) covered in Theorem 4.13. The number of lifts u~\tilde{u} of a given disk uu is equal to the number of points in each fiber of τn−,n+\tau_{n_{-},n_{+}}, which is the determinant of the matrix in (21). On the other hand, the count of disks in Theorem 4.14 is, for an appropriate choice of relative spin structure as in Remark 3.32

m​(v0)=−1/2.m(v_{0})=-1/2.

The boundary conditions u~∗​T​L\tilde{u}^{*}TL and u∗​T​Lu^{*}TL are naturally identified, but the relative spin structures are defined by capping procedure, detailed in [38]. The two relative spin structures on the extensions of u~∗​T​L\tilde{u}^{*}TL and u∗​T​Lu^{*}TL differ in relative spin structure by a sign

(−1)n++n−−1=(−1)n++n−−1=(−1)n+2−n−2−1=(−1)|det(Tn−,n+)|−1.(-1)^{n_{+}+n_{-}-1}=(-1)^{n_{+}+n_{-}-1}=(-1)^{n_{+}^{2}-n_{-}^{2}-1}=(-1)^{\left|\det(T_{n_{-},n_{+}})\right|-1}.

(The number of half turns of u~\tilde{u} at the open puncture is n++n−n_{+}+n_{-}, while the number of half-turns in uu is 11.) Since the number of such disks, 11, is half the determinant 22, the statement of the Theorem now follows from the identities

m​(v)\displaystyle m(v) =\displaystyle= m​(v0)​#​{u~↦u}\displaystyle m(v_{0})\#\{\tilde{u}\mapsto u\}
=\displaystyle= −12​(−1)|n++n−−1|​|det(Tn−,n+)|\displaystyle-{\frac{1}{2}}(-1)^{|n_{+}+n_{-}-1|}\left|\det(T_{n_{-},n_{+}})\right|
=\displaystyle= −12​(−1)|det(μ∙​r​(μ∙))|​|det(μ∙​r​(μ∙))|.\displaystyle-{\frac{1}{2}}(-1)^{|\det(\mu_{\bullet}r(\mu_{\bullet}))|}|\det(\mu_{\bullet}r(\mu_{\bullet}))|.

∎

4.5. Curves with boundary in the pair of pants

Next, we consider curves with boundary in the Lagrangian pair of pants from Example 2.11 (a). We use the isotopy of the previous section to compute the number of holomorphic disks u:C→Xu:C\to X with boundary in the pair of pants LL of various topological types.

Lemma 4.18.

Let X=(ℂ×)2X=(\mathbb{C}^{\times})^{2}. For a judicious choice of relative spin structure as in Definition 3.14, the number m​(L,Y¯)m(L,\underline{Y}) of holomorphic disks u:C→Xu:C\to X with boundary in Lagrangian pair of pants L≅ℝ2−{0,1}L\cong\mathbb{R}^{2}-\{0,1\} from Example 2.11 (a) so that CC has a single strip-like end and no cylindrical ends is equal to 11, as in Definition 1.5 (g)

Proof.

The number of disks with boundary in the compactification of the Lagrangian pair of pants was computed in Lemma 4.9: Let X0=Bl⁡(ℙ1×ℙ1)X_{0}=\operatorname{Bl}(\mathbb{P}^{1}\times\mathbb{P}^{1}) be the blow-up at point ℙ1×ℙ1\mathbb{P}^{1}\times\mathbb{P}^{1}. Consider the Lagrangian L0L_{0} whose image Φ​(L0)\Phi(L_{0}) under the moment map Φ\Phi is a trivalent graph Λ\Lambda shown in Figure 33 centered at 0 in the moment polytope Φ​(X)\Phi(X), so that Λ\Lambda intersects the image of the exceptional divisor EE twice. The number of disks u:C→X0u:C\to X_{0} bounding L0L_{0} of Maslov index I​(u)=2I(u)=2 is equal to −1-1, by Lemma 4.9.

We deduce from the previous paragraph the number of holomorphic disks with boundary in the Lagrangian pair of pants as in the Lemma. Consider the polyhedral decomposition 𝒫={P}\mathcal{P}=\{P\} shown in Figure 32. Let u=(uv)u=(u_{v}) be a broken Maslov-index-two disk in the resulting broken symplectic manifold 𝕏\mathbb{X} passing through the given point p∈Lp\in L as shown, with tropical graph Γ\Gamma.

Claim: There is no vertex of vv mapping to the polytope P​(v)P(v) containing the vertex of Λ\Lambda; that is, the tropical graph Γ\Gamma maps to a single leg of Λ\Lambda. Indeed, since the intersection number of [u][u] with the class of the divisor [D][D] is 11, uvi:Cvi→𝕏P​(vi)u_{v_{i}}:C_{v_{i}}\to\mathbb{X}_{P(v_{i})} in the pieces 𝕏P​(vi)\mathbb{X}_{P(v_{i})} corresponding to the other legs of the trivalent graph Λ\Lambda of LL in uu. Indeed, removing any such vertex v0v_{0} would break Γ\Gamma into pieces Γi\Gamma_{i} the univalent vertices of Γi\Gamma_{i} other than v0v_{0} represent other intersection point with the divisor DD. Thus each piece Γi\Gamma_{i} contributes at least one to the intersection number with [D][D], and the graph Γ\Gamma of the broken map uu must be as shown.

By the already proved parts of Theorem 1.4 (namely the two multiplicities appearing in Figure 30) the count of holomorphic spheres uv:Cv→𝕏P​(v)u_{v}:C_{v}\to\mathbb{X}_{P(v)} in the lower left in Figure 33 is equal to −1-1. So the number of holomorphic disks uv′:Cv′→𝕏P​(v′)u_{v^{\prime}}:C_{v^{\prime}}\to\mathbb{X}_{P(v^{\prime})} with boundary in the Lagrangian 𝕃P​(v′){\mathbb{L}}_{P(v^{\prime})} with the given point constraint is equal to 11. This justifies Theorem 1.4 case Definition 1.5 (f). ∎

Remark 4.19.

For the standard complex structure, the Lagrangian pair of pants in Lemma 4.18 above can be described explicitly as follows. Consider the holomorphic pair of pants H={1+z1+z2=0}H=\{1+z_{1}+z_{2}=0\} in ℂ2\mathbb{C}^{2}, and let L⊂ℝ4L\subset\mathbb{R}^{4} be its Lagrangian hyper-Kähler rotation. The diagonal Δ={(z,z),z∈ℂ}\Delta=\{(z,z),z\in\mathbb{C}\} is divided into two pieces by its intersection with LL, as in Hicks [13, Figure 2]. Each half of the holomorphic curve Δ\Delta is the image of a holomorphic disk, whose tropical graph Γ\Gamma is shown on the left in Figure 33, in blue. Note that the canonical trivialization of u∗​T​Lu^{*}TL at the strip-like end, together with the obvious trivialization of uT​Lu^{T}L along the boundary into tangential and normal parts, do not combine to a trivialization of the extended boundary value problem described in Definition 3.14. Therefore, the definition of relative spin structure on the extension.

Next, we compute the number of holomorphic disks with boundary in the Lagrangian pair of pants with two or three strip-like ends.

Lemma 4.20.

The number m​(v)m(v) of holomorphic disks in 𝕏P​(v)=(ℂ×)2⊂Bl3⁡(ℙ2)\mathbb{X}_{P(v)}=(\mathbb{C}^{\times})^{2}\subset\operatorname{Bl}^{3}(\mathbb{P}^{2}) with boundary in the Lagrangian pair of pants 𝕃P​(v)≅ℝ2−{0,1}{\mathbb{L}}_{P(v)}\cong\mathbb{R}^{2}-\{0,1\} with two or three strip-like ends and a point constraint p∈𝕃P​(v)p\in{\mathbb{L}}_{P(v)}, is equal to 11.

Proof.

We recall the model problem in Lemma 4.10: For the trivalent graph shown in Figure 34 the number of disks u:C→Xu:C\to X with boundary in LL with three point constraints

u​(z1)=p1,u​(z2)=p2,u​(z3)=p3,z1,z2,z3∈∂Cu(z_{1})=p_{1},u(z_{2})=p_{2},u(z_{3})=p_{3},\quad z_{1},z_{2},z_{3}\in\partial C

is equal to 11, independent of the choice of almost complex structure. We take a polyhedral decomposition as in Figure 34, into a central piece P0P_{0} and pieces PiP_{i} near the vertices. The number m​(vi)m(v_{i}) of lines in the pieces near the vertices are equal to 11, for a suitable extension of relative spin structures. For this choice, we must also have that

m​(v0)=1m(v_{0})=1

where m​(v0)m(v_{0}) is the number of disks u:C→X∘u:C\to X^{\circ} with boundary in the Lagrangian pair of pants LL in the complement X∘X^{\circ} of the toric boundary in ℙ2\mathbb{P}^{2} with either three fixed Reeb chords γe1,γe2,γe3\gamma_{e_{1}},\gamma_{e_{2}},\gamma_{e_{3}} at infinity, or two Reeb chords γe1,γe2\gamma_{e_{1}},\gamma_{e_{2}} and one boundary constraint p3∈L0p_{3}\in L_{0}, or with three boundary constraints p1,p2,p3∈L0p_{1},p_{2},p_{3}\in L_{0}. In the last case, there are two possible orderings of the boundary constraints; we may assume that the ordering is compatible with the orientation around the boundary. This justifies Theorem 1.4 case Definition 1.5 (h) and (i). ∎

Remark 4.21.

We make some comments on the combinatorial formulas for area and index. The area

A​(u)=∑v∈Vert⁡(Γ)A​(uv)A(u)=\sum_{v\in\operatorname{Vert}(\mathbb{\Gamma})}A(u_{v})

of a broken map u=(uv)u=(u_{v}) may be computed as a sum of the areas of its pieces, attached to the vertices of Γ\Gamma; this quantity is not related to the lattice length of the graph. For example, the areas A​(u1),A​(u2)∈ℝ≥0A(u_{1}),A(u_{2})\in\mathbb{R}_{\geq 0} of the curves u1,u2u_{1},u_{2} corresponding to the tropical graphs Γ1,Γ2\Gamma_{1},\Gamma_{2} shown in the left and right of Figure 34 are equal, since they both correspond to Maslov index four disks with boundary in the anti-diagonal.

To further explain this point, note that the realizability theorem for tropical Lagrangians in Theorem 2.9 does not produce Lagrangians LL that are translationally invariant over the edges ϵ∈Edge⁡(Λ)\epsilon\in\operatorname{Edge}(\Lambda), but rather Lagrangians LL that are asymptotic to codimension one tori {a}×Tn−1\{a\}\times T^{n-1} at the ends of the edges ϵ∈Edge⁡(Λ)\epsilon\in\operatorname{Edge}(\Lambda). The inverse images of the interiors of edges in the Lagrangian are not translationally invariant, and the area A​(u)A(u) of the holomorphic strips u:C→Xu:C\to X over the edges ϵ∈Edge⁡(Λ)\epsilon\in\operatorname{Edge}(\Lambda) is not proportional to the length of ϵ∈Edge⁡(Λ)\epsilon\in\operatorname{Edge}(\Lambda).

On the other hand, the Maslov index may be read off from the pieces via the formula

I​(u)=∑v∈Vert⁡(Γ)I​(uv).I(u)=\sum_{v\in\operatorname{Vert}(\mathbb{\Gamma})}I(u_{v}).

Since the dimension of the relative moduli space of disks resp. spheres containing uvu_{v} is 2+I​(uv)−dim(aut⁡(Cv))2+I(u_{v})-\dim(\operatorname{aut}(C_{v})), resp. 4+I​(uv)−dim(aut⁡(Cv))4+I(u_{v})-\dim(\operatorname{aut}(C_{v})) we see that

I​(uv)={2for the closed univalent vertices0for bivalent vertices and open univalent vertices and−2for the trivalent verticesI(u_{v})=\begin{cases}2&\text{for the closed univalent vertices}\\ 0&\text{for bivalent vertices and open univalent vertices and}\\ -2&\text{for the trivalent vertices}\end{cases}

For example, closed univalent vertices have automorphism group of dimension two and a dimension two moduli space, and so must be Maslov index two. Similarly, for the disks bounding the pairs of pants, the moduli space computed in Lemma 4.20 is dimension zero, so by Riemann-Roch dim(L)+I​(uv)=0\dim(L)+I(u_{v})=0 so I​(uv)=−2I(u_{v})=-2. In particular, the Maslov number of a broken disk for an elementary decomposition is not twice the number of times the graph intersects the boundary of the dual complex.

4.6. Collisions on the boundary

We want to show that for a good choice of cutting data, all of the graphs of Maslov-index-two broken disks with valence at least two have vertices in the interior of the dual complex; that is, the collisions occur in the interior in the sense of Definition 1.3. We suppose that the polyhedral decomposition 𝒫\mathcal{P} of BB is the intersection of polyhedral decompositions 𝒫0\mathcal{P}_{0} and 𝒫1\mathcal{P}_{1}, where 𝒫0\mathcal{P}_{0} is the collection of polytopes obtained by a sequence of cuts parallel to the facets of Φ​(X)\Phi(X) as in Figure 35.

Given a family of cutting datum for 𝒫0\mathcal{P}_{0} and 𝒫1\mathcal{P}_{1} we obtained a family of cutting data for 𝒫\mathcal{P} as explained in [34, Lemma 5.5]. Multiplying the cutting data for 𝒫0\mathcal{P}_{0} by a parameter ρ\rho gives a family of dual complexes Bρ∨B^{\vee}_{\rho} depending on the parameter ρ\rho.

Lemma 4.22.

For any ρ0>0\rho_{0}>0, there exists a real number T>0T>0 so that the number of tropical graphs Γ\Gamma for gluing data ρ>ρ0\rho>\rho_{0} of broken Maslov-index-two disks u=(uv)u=(u_{v}) is at most TT.

Proof.

By monotonicity, the set of broken Maslov-index-two map has energy, hence area, bounded by some number E0E_{0}. On the other hand, suppose that the broken map uu is non-constant. If P∈𝒫P\in\mathcal{P} is top dimensional polytope, then any component uvu_{v} with P​(v)=PP(v)=P has positive area, since it is non-constant. Hence the number of vertices of Γ\Gamma mapping to vertices of B∨B^{\vee} that Γ\Gamma is universally bounded. It follows in particular that there exists a number T0T_{0} so that the number of focus-focus values b∨∈B∨,b∈Bfocb^{\vee}\in B^{\vee},b\in B^{\operatorname{foc}} that Γ\Gamma intersects is less than T0T_{0}, for any graph Γ\Gamma. In particular, the number of univalent vertices v∈Vert⁡(Γ)=1v\in\operatorname{Vert}(\Gamma)=1 is universally bounded for tropical graphs Γ\Gamma of Maslov-index-two broken disks. The bound on the number of combinatorial types follows, since each type has underlying graph that is a tree. ∎

We show that any rigid tropical graph consists of a collection of rigid graphs in the interior of the dual complex a graph in the boundary of the dual complex: Any tropical graph Γ\Gamma in B∨B^{\vee} admits a decomposition

Γ=int⁡(Γ)∪∂Γ\Gamma=\operatorname{int}(\Gamma)\cup\partial\Gamma

where int⁡Γ\operatorname{int}\Gamma resp. ∂Γ\partial\Gamma maps to int⁡(B∨)\operatorname{int}(B^{\vee}) resp. ∂B∨\partial B^{\vee}.

Theorem 4.23.

Let X→BX\to B be a compact almost toric manifold without elliptic singularities and 𝒫={P}\mathcal{P}=\{P\} an elementary polyhedral decomposition. There exists ρ0>0\rho_{0}>0 so that for any ρ>ρ0\rho>\rho_{0}, the tropical graph Γ\Gamma of any broken Maslov-index-two disk with boundary in LL consists of a connected piece int⁡(Γ)\operatorname{int}(\Gamma) mapping to the interior int⁡(B∨)\operatorname{int}(B^{\vee}) and a connected piece ∂Γ\partial\Gamma mapping to the boundary ∂B∨\partial B^{\vee}, joined by a single edge ee whose direction 𝒯​(e)\mathcal{T}(e) is normal to one of the facets of Φ​(X)\Phi(X).

We begin with some remarks on holomorphic spheres in Hirzebruch surfaces. By a Hirzebruch surface we mean the projectivization

X=ℙ​(𝒪​(d)⊕𝒪​(0)),d∈ℤX=\mathbb{P}(\mathcal{O}(d)\oplus\mathcal{O}(0)),d\in\mathbb{Z}

of a line bundle over ℙ1\mathbb{P}^{1}. The space XX has a natural torus action, arising from the action of scalar multiplication on first factor, and a lift of the circle action on the base, for which the moment polytope can be taken to be the convex hull of the points (0,0),(0,1),(1,1),(1,1+d).(0,0),(0,1),(1,1),(1,1+d). By an orbifold Hirzebruch surface, we mean any projective toric surface XX whose moment polytope Φ​(X)\Phi(X) is a trapezoid. We may view such a surface as the projectivization of an orbifold bundle over the orbifold projective line with two orbifold points.

Lemma 4.24.

Suppose that P∈𝒫P\in\mathcal{P} is such that either

  1. (a)

    (Boundary edge piece) P∩Φ​(X)P\cap\Phi(X) is a trapezoid that intersects ∂Φ​(X)\partial\Phi(X) in a single face of P∩Φ​(X)P\cap\Phi(X), and XPX_{P} is the corresponding (possibly orbifold) Hirzebruch surface and YY is the union of fibers over 0,∞∈ℙ10,\infty\in\mathbb{P}^{1} and the section at infinity, isomorphic to ℙ1\mathbb{P}^{1}; or

  2. (b)

    (Boundary vertex piece) P∩Φ​(X)P\cap\Phi(X) is a rectangle intersecting ∂Φ​(X)\partial\Phi(X) in two faces of P∩Φ​(X)P\cap\Phi(X), in which case, XP=ℙ1×ℙ1X_{P}=\mathbb{P}^{1}\times\mathbb{P}^{1} and Y=(ℙ1×{∞})∪({∞}×ℙ1)Y=(\mathbb{P}^{1}\times\{\infty\})\cup(\{\infty\}\times\mathbb{P}^{1}) is the divisor correponding to two adjacent faces of P∩Φ​(X)P\cap\Phi(X).

Let Γ\mathbb{\Gamma} be a rigid type with a vertex vv whose adjacent edges e1,…,eke_{1},\ldots,e_{k} have the property that at least one of which, say e1e_{1}, has P​(e1)P(e_{1}) equal to one of the faces not meeting ∂Φ​(X)\partial\Phi(X). Let Γ​(v)\mathbb{\Gamma}(v) denote the graph consisting of the vertex vv and adjacent edges, and ℳΓ​(v)​(𝕏P​(v))\mathcal{M}_{\mathbb{\Gamma}(v)}(\mathbb{X}_{P(v)}) the moduli space of relative maps to 𝕏P​(v)\mathbb{X}_{P(v)} without constraints. Let Γ1,…,Γk\mathbb{\Gamma}_{1},\ldots,\mathbb{\Gamma}_{k} denote the graphs obtained from Γ\mathbb{\Gamma} by breaking the edges e1,…,eke_{1},\ldots,e_{k}, in addition to Γ​(v)\Gamma(v), and ℳΓi​(𝕏)\mathcal{M}_{\mathbb{\Gamma}_{i}}(\mathbb{X}) the corresponding moduli spaces. Then ℳΓ​(v)​(𝕏P​(v))\mathcal{M}_{\mathbb{\Gamma}(v)}(\mathbb{X}_{P(v)}) is dimension at least 2​k2k, and the moduli spaces ℳΓi​(𝕏)\mathcal{M}_{\mathbb{\Gamma}_{i}}(\mathbb{X}) are dimension zero. Furthermore, in the case of a (Boundary vertex piece), the vertex vv is adjacent to a single edge e∈Edge⁡(Γ)e\in\operatorname{Edge}(\Gamma).

Proof.

Consider the first case of a (Boundary edge piece). Suppose first that dd is integral, so that XPX_{P} is a smooth Hirzebruch surface. Each edge corresponds to an intersection of uvu_{v} with the boundary divisor YY, and the union of YY with the zero section ZZ is anti-canonical divisor of XX:

(22) c1​(X)=[Y]∨+[Z]∨.c_{1}(X)=[Y]^{\vee}+[Z]^{\vee}.

Riemann-Roch implies that the dimension of the moduli space of relative maps to 𝕏P​(v)\mathbb{X}_{P(v)} of type Γ​(v)\mathbb{\Gamma}(v) is

(23) dim(ℳΓ​(v)​(𝕏P​(v)))=dim(X)−6+2​k+c1​(u)−∑i=1kcodim⁡P​(ei).\dim(\mathcal{M}_{\mathbb{\Gamma}(v)}(\mathbb{X}_{P(v)}))=\dim(X)-6+2k+c_{1}(u)-\sum_{i=1}^{k}\operatorname{codim}P(e_{i}).

The first Chern number c1​(u)c_{1}(u) is the sum of the intersection multiplicities with YY and ZZ. After removing the intersection points with ZZ, we view uu as a map to the interior of the toric variety 𝕏¯P​(v)\overline{\mathbb{X}}_{P(v)} with polytope Δ:=P​(v)∩Φ​(X)\Delta:=P(v)\cap\Phi(X). Each puncture maps to the boundary YY, and represents an intersection of multiplicity at least two if it maps to a corner. Hence

(24) [Y]∨.[uv]−∑i=1kcodim⁡P​(ei)≥0.[Y]^{\vee}.[u_{v}]-\sum_{i=1}^{k}\operatorname{codim}P(e_{i})\geq 0.

Let

μ~i∈ℝ2,i=1,…,k~\tilde{\mu}_{i}\in\mathbb{R}^{2},i=1,\ldots,\tilde{k}

denote the directions at the punctures. Thus in particular we may assume μ~i=𝒯​(e1)\tilde{\mu}_{i}=\mathcal{T}(e_{1}) has non-trivial second component. The balancing condition (2) reads

∑i=1k~μi~=0.\sum_{i=1}^{\tilde{k}}\tilde{\mu_{i}}=0.

This implies that at least one of the other vectors μ~i,i>1\tilde{\mu}_{i},i>1 has positive second component. We take the normal directions corresponding to the boundary facets of Δ\Delta to be (0,1),(0,−1),(1,0),(−1,−d)(0,1),(0,-1),(1,0),(-1,-d). Any edge direction μ~i\tilde{\mu}_{i} is a combination of the normal vectors to adjacent facets to the polytope P​(ei)P(e_{i}). Since the only facet of P​(v)P(v) whose normal direction has positive first component is bottom facet, the image of the zero section ZZ, the intersection number of uu with ZZ is positive. It follows that 2​[u].[Z]2[u].[Z] is at least two. Hence

(25) dim(X)−6+2​[u].[Z]≥0.\dim(X)-6+2[u].[Z]\geq 0.

Combining (23) , (25) and (24) gives

dimℳΓ​(v)​(𝕏P​(v))≥2​k.\dim\mathcal{M}_{\mathbb{\Gamma}(v)}(\mathbb{X}_{P(v)})\geq 2k.

Rigidity of the moduli space of maps of type Γ\Gamma implies that the moduli spaces for the graphs on the other side of the edges e1,…,eke_{1},\ldots,e_{k} are rigid

dimℳΓi​(𝕏)=0\dim\mathcal{M}_{\mathbb{\Gamma}_{i}}(\mathbb{X})=0

since the fiber product of ℳΓ​(v)​(𝕏P​(v))\mathcal{M}_{\mathbb{\Gamma}(v)}(\mathbb{X}_{P(v)}) with the spaces ℳΓi​(𝕏)\mathcal{M}_{\mathbb{\Gamma}_{i}}(\mathbb{X}) has expected dimension zero.

The general case follows from resolution of singularities. Let 𝕏~P​(v)\tilde{\mathbb{X}}_{P(v)} be a smooth toric resolution of singularities of 𝕏¯P​(v)\overline{\mathbb{X}}_{P(v)} obtained by repeated blow-ups at the inverse images of the vertices of the moment polytope, corresponding to a polytope QQ. For suitable choices of blow-ups, the directions μi\mu_{i} of each edge are normal to some facet. As before, there must be at least one edge either meeting the bottom facet, or mapping to one of the facets that is collapsed to the bottom vertices under the blow-down map. The resulting moduli space is contained in the codimension two locus of relative maps whose evaluation at eie_{i} is the inverse image of vertex of Δ\Delta, and the same argument holds.

The case of a (Boundary vertex piece) is similar. The dimension of the moduli space of genus zero maps to ℙ1×ℙ1\mathbb{P}^{1}\times\mathbb{P}^{1} of bidegree (d1,d2)∈ℤ≥02(d_{1},d_{2})\in\mathbb{Z}_{\geq 0}^{2} is

dimℳ​(ℙ1×ℙ1,(d1,d2))=4+4​(d1+d2)−6=4​(d1+d2)−2.\dim\mathcal{M}(\mathbb{P}^{1}\times\mathbb{P}^{1},(d_{1},d_{2}))=4+4(d_{1}+d_{2})-6=4(d_{1}+d_{2})-2.

Such maps cannot be made rigid by adding 2​(d1+d2)2(d_{1}+d_{2}) constraints (such as fixing the evaluation map, or requiring additional tangency conditions), unless (d1,d2)∈{(1,0),(0,1)}(d_{1},d_{2})\in\{(1,0),(0,1)\}. ∎

It remains to identify the kind of graphs that can appear in the boundary of the dual complex. We already identified one type of such graph, namely a cylinder that hits the boundary in a perpendicular direction to one on the facets of the moment polytope.

Lemma 4.25.

Suppose P∩Φ​(X)P\cap\Phi(X) is a trapezoid intersects ∂Φ​(X)\partial\Phi(X) in a single face of P∩Φ​(X)P\cap\Phi(X), and XP=ℙ​(𝒪​(d)⊕𝒪​(0))X_{P}=\mathbb{P}(\mathcal{O}(d)\oplus\mathcal{O}(0)) is a Hirzebruch surface with d≥0d\geq 0 and Y=(ℙ1×{∞})∪({0}×ℙ1)∪({∞}×ℙ1)Y=(\mathbb{P}^{1}\times\{\infty\})\cup(\{0\}\times\mathbb{P}^{1})\cup(\{\infty\}\times\mathbb{P}^{1}). Let Γ\Gamma be a graph with a single vertex vv, and exactly two adjacent edges e1,e2e_{1},e_{2} with e1e_{1} mapping to the corner disjoint from ∂Φ​(X)\partial\Phi(X) so that 𝔱P​(e1)≅ℝ2\mathfrak{t}_{P(e_{1})}\cong\mathbb{R}^{2}. Then the second edge e2e_{2} maps to a face P​(e2)P(e_{2}) of PP with 𝔱P​(e2)≅ℝ\mathfrak{t}_{P(e_{2})}\cong\mathbb{R}, and the direction 𝒯​(e2){\mathcal{T}}(e_{2}) is the projection of 𝒯​(e1)∈ℝ2{\mathcal{T}}(e_{1})\in\mathbb{R}^{2} onto the first factor via the canonical map 𝔱P​(e1)/ℝ​ν≅𝔱P​(e2)\mathfrak{t}_{P(e_{1})}/\mathbb{R}\nu\cong\mathfrak{t}_{P(e_{2})}, where ν=(0,1)\nu=(0,1) is the normal to the facet of the moment polytope that is not in YY, i.e. not a relative divisor.

A picture of the situation is shown in Figure 36.

Proof.

The proof is a computation with intersection multiplicities. Let u:C→Xu:C\to X be a map with such a graph Γ\Gamma. Let X′=ℙ2X^{\prime}=\mathbb{P}^{2} equipped with a toric birational equivalence with XX, and u′:C→X′u^{\prime}:C\to X^{\prime} the map given by composing with the birational equivalence. The map u′u^{\prime} intersects the boundary of X′X^{\prime} exactly twice. The image is therefore a line intersecting a torus fixed-point. Such a curve given by a one-parameter subgroup to the interior (ℂ×)2(\mathbb{C}^{\times})^{2}. Suppose the direction of the first edge is 𝒯​(e1)=(μ1,μ2)∈ℤ2{\mathcal{T}}(e_{1})=(\mu_{1},\mu_{2})\in\mathbb{Z}^{2}, in the standard coordinates for which the moment polytope is the trapezoid with vertices at (0,0),(0,1),(1,1),(0,1+d).(0,0),(0,1),(1,1),(0,1+d).
