Without loss of generality μ1<0\mu_{1}<0 and the intersection numbers of uu with {∞}×ℙ1\{\infty\}\times\mathbb{P}^{1} resp. ℙ1×{∞}\mathbb{P}^{1}\times\{\infty\} are −μ1-\mu_{1} resp. −μ2-\mu_{2}. The other intersection of uu with the toric boundary is at (0,0)(0,0), with intersection multiplicities −μ1,−μ2+d​μ1-\mu_{1},-\mu_{2}+d\mu_{1}. Now (0,0)(0,0) is in the pre-image of the hyperplane P​(e2)P(e_{2}) with normal vector (1,0)(1,0), and the intersection multiplicity of uu with {0}×ℙ1\{0\}\times\mathbb{P}^{1} is the projection −μ1-\mu_{1} of 𝒯​(e2){\mathcal{T}}(e_{2}) onto the first factor ℝ\mathbb{R}, as claimed. ∎

Proof of Theorem 4.23.

Suppose that Γ\Gamma is a tropical graph in B∨B^{\vee} containing edges e1,…,eke_{1},\ldots,e_{k} that meet in the boundary ∂B∨\partial B^{\vee}, as shown in Figure 37. We may choose coordinates so that

∂B~∨=B~∨∩(ℝ×{−ρ}).\partial\tilde{B}^{\vee}=\tilde{B}^{\vee}\cap(\mathbb{R}\times\{-\rho\}).

In particular the tangent space to the boundary has direction (1,0)(1,0). Suppose without loss of generality that the edges e1,…,eℓe_{1},\ldots,e_{\ell} have directions 𝒯​(e1),…,𝒯​(eℓ)\mathcal{T}(e_{1}),\ldots,\mathcal{T}(e_{\ell}) with positive projection onto T​∂B~∨T\partial\tilde{B}^{\vee}, and eℓ+1,…,eke_{\ell+1},\ldots,e_{k} have directions with negative projection. Choose ρ0\rho_{0} so that for ρ>ρ0\rho>\rho_{0}, the intersections of ℝ×{−ρ}\mathbb{R}\times\{-\rho\} with e1,…,eℓe_{1},\dots,e_{\ell} are to the right of the intersections of ℝ×{−ρ}\mathbb{R}\times\{-\rho\} with eℓ+1,…,eke_{\ell+1},\ldots,e_{k}, as in Figure 37.

Any vertices involve collisions between the edges e1,…,eℓe_{1},\ldots,e_{\ell} or between eℓ+1,…,eke_{\ell+1},\ldots,e_{k}. Any edge direction resulting from such a collision has positive resp. negative projection onto the first component. Since the two types of edges never intersect, there is no connected graph which involves combinations of these edges, which is a contradiction. Repeating the process for each graph Γ\Gamma (without tropical structure) produces a constant ρ0\rho_{0} so that for ρ>ρ0\rho>\rho_{0}, all collisions are in the interior. ∎

Example 4.26.

Continuing Example 1.7, we give an example where bivalent vertices map to the boundary of the dual complex. Let p1p_{1} be a constraint with moment image Φ​(p1)=(−ϵ,−ϵ),ϵ>0\Phi(p_{1})=(-\epsilon,-\epsilon),\epsilon>0. In order to compute the multiplicities in this case, we need to orient the Lagrangian so that the direction of the edge containing this point is (−1,1)(-1,1).

There are four tropical graphs Γ1,Γ2,Γ3,Γ4\Gamma_{1},\Gamma_{2},\Gamma_{3},\Gamma_{4} (shown in the lower part of Figure 38) contributing to the count: There are two tropical graphs Γ1,Γ2\Gamma_{1},\Gamma_{2} with directions (1,1)(1,1) and (−1,−1)(-1,-1), respectively. Each of Γ1,Γ2\Gamma_{1},\Gamma_{2} has two vertices, with multiplicities m​(v1)=−1,m​(v2)=1m(v_{1})=-1,m(v_{2})=1. The total contribution of each graph is therefore

m​(Γk)=m​(v1)​m​(v2)=−1m(\Gamma_{k})=m(v_{1})m(v_{2})=-1

to the count. On the other hand, there is a graph Γ3\Gamma_{3} with a bivalent vertex at Φ​(p1)\Phi(p_{1}) and edges e1,e2e_{1},e_{2} with directions 11 (from the Lagrangian) and (0,1)(0,1). The determinant of the matrix formed by these directions is one and so

m​(Γ3)=m​(v1)​m​(v2)​m​(v3)=1​(−1)​1/2=−1/2.m(\Gamma_{3})=m(v_{1})m(v_{2})m(v_{3})=1(-1)1/2=-1/2.

The final graph Γ4\Gamma_{4} has a bivalent vertex at Φ​(p1)\Phi(p_{1}) and edges e1,e2e_{1},e_{2} with directions 11 (from the Lagrangian) and (2,1)(2,1). The contribution from the graph is

m​(Γ4)=m​(v1)​m​(v2)​m​(v3)=1​(3)​1/2=3/2m(\Gamma_{4})=m(v_{1})m(v_{2})m(v_{3})=1(3)1/2=3/2

The total disk count is therefore

WL=(−1)+(−1)+(1/2)+(−3/2)=−1.W_{L}=(-1)+(-1)+(1/2)+(-3/2)=-1.
Remark 4.27.

In Definition 1.5 there is one additional vertex which is generic. This vertex has two adjacent open edges and one adjacent closed edge. Consider trivalent vertices v∈Vert∘⁡(Γ)v\in\operatorname{Vert}_{\circ}(\Gamma) mapping to an edge ϵ\epsilon of LL with open edges e∘,e∘′⊂ϵe_{\circ},e_{\circ}^{\prime}\subset\epsilon and closed edge e∙e_{\bullet} with directions satisfying the balancing condition

𝒯​(e∘′)+𝒯​(e∘)+𝒯​(e∙)+𝒯​(r​(e∙))=0.\mathcal{T}(e_{\circ}^{\prime})+\mathcal{T}(e_{\circ})+\mathcal{T}(e_{\bullet})+\mathcal{T}(r(e_{\bullet}))=0.

The tropical graph Γ\Gamma is half of a tropical graph Γ~\tilde{\Gamma} with a four-valent vertex v~∈Vert(Γ)~\tilde{v}\in\operatorname{Vert}(\tilde{\Gamma)} that corresponds to a holomorphic sphere. As explained in [35], for holomorphic sphere counts, four-valent vertices v∈Vert⁡(Γ),|v|=4v\in\operatorname{Vert}(\Gamma),|v|=4 can be be avoided by choosing the constraints generically, so after perturbation two three-valent vertices v1,v2∈Vert⁡(Γ)v_{1},v_{2}\in\operatorname{Vert}(\Gamma) appear rather than a single four-valent vertex. However, in the case of open-closed graphs Γ\Gamma, the position of the open edges e∈Edge∘⁡(Γ)e\in\operatorname{Edge}_{\circ}(\Gamma) cannot be chosen generically because the Lagrangian boundary condition cannot in general be perturbed. As a result, the diagram in Figure 39 occurs even generically. However, this vertex does not include any of our examples, as a little thought shows that the contribution of such a vertex to the Maslov index is zero, and since there are three punctures, the Maslov index of the corresponding holomorphic disk must be at least four.

5. Potentials of Lagrangian spheres in del Pezzo surfaces

In this section we complete the proof of Theorem 1.1, in the sense that we show that every integer eigenvalue in Table 1 with non-maximal modulus is the potential of a Lagrangian sphere. We assume the completeness of Table 1, which we plan to show in the sequel to this paper on the surjectivity of the corresponding open-closed maps. The reader will note that, in each case, there is at most one such eigenvalue, except for the del Pezzo of degree six whose associated Manin system is semisimple but not simple, in which case there are two.

Proof of Theorem 1.1.

Let XX be a del Pezzo surface equipped with its monotone symplectic form. We deal with the various cases in descending degree. The case that XX is the product of projective lines is trivial: The minimal Chern number of XX is two, and the minimal Maslov number is four. It follows that the potential of the anti-diagonal LL is WL=0W_{L}=0, as there are no Maslov-index-two disks. The other spectral values

±4∈Spec(c1⋆)\pm 4\in\operatorname{Spec}(c_{1}\star)

have maximal modulus |±4|=4|\pm 4|=4 among all spectral values. The case of the del Pezzo of degree eight, that is, the blow-up of the projective plane, is also trivial, as there are no integer spectral values. The case of the del Pezzo of degree seven, that is, the blow-up of ℙ2\mathbb{P}^{2} at two points, was treated in Example 1.7. The degree six case was partly treated in Example 1.6. Figure 40 shows that three Lagrangians (with trivial local systems) giving rise to the spectral values

6,−2,−3∈Spec(c1⋆)6,-2,-3\in\operatorname{Spec}(c_{1}\star)

respectively. The monotone torus L1L_{1} with its trivial local system gives rise to the critical value of the potential WL1​(1)=6W_{L_{1}}(1)=6, with the six tropical graphs corresponding to the Cho-Oh disks shown on the left in Figure 40. A Lagrangian sphere L2L_{2} with WL2=−2W_{L_{2}}=-2 is shown in the middle picture in Figure 40. The tropical graph Λ\Lambda is a single segment with two univalent vertices; this Lagrangian L2L_{2} together with the Lagrangians L2′,L2′′L_{2}^{\prime},L_{2}^{\prime\prime} for the rotations of the graph Λ\Lambda by 2​π/32\pi/3 and 4​π/34\pi/3 form the A2A_{2} part of a Manin system of Lagrangians.

The del Pezzo of degree four was treated in Example 1.8. The del Pezzo of degree two was treated in Example 4.12. We show the computation for the del Pezzo of degree one in Figure 41. The figure shows four different types of tropical graphs of broken holomorphic disks of Maslov-index-two bounding a tropical Lagrangian sphere whose total count is WL=−60W_{L}=-60. Note that the graph of the Lagrangian lies along one of the cut loci, so the affine structures on either side of the Lagrangian are related by a shear. However, the only graphs contributing are those with intial direction perpendicular to the Lagrangian, which are invariant under the shear, and so this complication may be overlooked.

  • •

    The first three types Γ1,Γ2,Γ3\Gamma_{1},\Gamma_{2},\Gamma_{3} corresponding to disks uu of Maslov index I​(u)=2I(u)=2 have initial direction 𝒯​(e1)=(−1,−1)\mathcal{T}(e_{1})=(-1,-1), and interact with two of the focus-focus values as shown.

  • •

    The last type Γ4\Gamma_{4} has initial direction 𝒯​(e1)=(1,1)\mathcal{T}(e_{1})=(1,1), and interacts with two of the focus-focus values near the bottom vertex of the moment polytope Φ​(X)\Phi(X) shown in the Figure.

There are also two other types Γ5,Γ6\Gamma_{5},\Gamma_{6} of tropical graphs of Maslov-index-two disks, each intersecting a single focus-focus value near the bottom vertex of Φ​(X)\Phi(X), whose contributions m​(Γ5)=−m​(Γ6)m(\Gamma_{5})=-m(\Gamma_{6}) cancel as explained in Proposition 6.6 of [34]. In particular, the proof of that proposition explains that graphs Γ5\mathbb{\Gamma}_{5} with two edges ending at the same focus-focus value with directions (1,0)(1,0) cancel with those types Γ6\mathbb{\Gamma}_{6} with a single edge ending at the same focus-focus value with direction (2,0)(2,0). We leave the cases of del Pezzos of degree five and three to the reader. ∎

Instructions for reporting errors

We are continuing to improve HTML versions of papers, and your feedback helps enhance accessibility and mobile support. To report errors in the HTML that will help us improve conversion and rendering, choose any of the methods listed below:

  • Click the "Report Issue" () button, located in the page header.

Tip: You can select the relevant text first, to include it in your report.

Our team has already identified the following issues. We appreciate your time reviewing and reporting rendering errors we may not have found yet. Your efforts will help us improve the HTML versions for all readers, because disability should not be a barrier to accessing research. Thank you for your continued support in championing open access for all.

Have a free development cycle? Help support accessibility at arXiv! Our collaborators at LaTeXML maintain a list of packages that need conversion, and welcome developer contributions.
